topics.title: Evolution of Internet and World Wide Web - Web Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evolution of Internet and World Wide Web - Web Basics # üìò SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Internet > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > The **Internet** is a global, decentralized network of interconnected computer networks that use the standardized **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** suite to communicate. It is a *network of networks* ‚Äî a public, cooperative, and self-governing infrastructure spanning every continent. ### üßÝ Intuitive Analogy Think of the **Internet** as the **international postal system** üè§. - Every house (computer) has a unique postal address (**IP address**). - Letters (data packets) are dropped into local post offices (routers), which forward them hop-by-hop toward the destination. - No single postmaster controls the whole system; it's a federation of cooperating postal services ‚Äî just as the Internet is a federation of cooperating Autonomous Systems (AS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric / Constant (Memorize):** > - **IP Address Size:** **32 bits** in IPv4 (‚âà 4.29 √ó 10‚Åπ addresses) and **128 bits** in IPv6. > - The Internet is **transport-layer agnostic** ‚Äî it carries Web, Email, FTP, VoIP, Streaming, etc. --- ## 1.2 The World Wide Web (WWW / W3) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is an *information system* running on top of the Internet that enables **hypertext** documents and resources (text, images, video, audio) to be accessed over the network via a **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** and transferred using **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)**. ### üßÝ Intuitive Analogy If the **Internet is the postal system**, then the **WWW is the library catalogue** üìö sitting on top of it. - The library buildings & roads = Internet infrastructure - The books & index cards = Web pages and hyperlinks - The librarian who fetches books on demand = **HTTP** - The card-catalog reference number = **URL** - The card-catalog system itself = **WWW** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Distinction (Board-Exam Favourite):** > - **Internet ‚âÝ Web.** The Internet is the *infrastructure*; the Web is one *application* running on it (others: Email, FTP, BitTorrent, SSH). > - The WWW was invented in **1989** by **Sir Tim Berners-Lee** at **CERN, Geneva**. --- ## 1.3 Web Basics ‚Äî The Foundational Triad A working Web system requires **three** coordinated building blocks: | # | Component | Role | Analogy | |---|-----------|------|---------| | 1 | **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** | Address of a resource on the Web | House address | | 2 | **HTTP / HTTPS (Protocol)** | Language/rules for requesting & transferring resources | Postal rules & language | | 3 | **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** | Document format that the browser renders | The letter's content & formatting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > The KTU Module-1 syllabus treats the above triad as the *atomic unit* of web programming. Every later concept (CSS, JS, frameworks, REST APIs) builds on this triad. # üìò SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of the Internet ‚Äî A Timeline The Internet did not appear overnight. It evolved through **four major research eras**: ### üîπ Era 1 ‚Äî The Cold War Seed (1960s) - **1957**: USSR launches **Sputnik** ‚Üí US forms **ARPA** (Advanced Research Projects Agency). - **1962**: J.C.R. Licklider (MIT) proposes a *Galactic Network* ‚Äî a globally interconnected set of computers. - **1969**: **ARPANET** goes live with **4 nodes** (UCLA, Stanford, UCSB, Univ. of Utah). First message: "**LO**" ‚Äî the system crashed before completing "LOGIN". - **1972**: Ray Tomlinson creates **email** and introduces the `@` symbol. ### üîπ Era 2 ‚Äî Standardization (1970s‚Äì1980s) - **1973**: Vint Cerf & Bob Kahn publish the **TCP/IP** paper ‚Äî the *lingua franca* of the Internet. - **1983**: ARPANET switches to **TCP/IP** on **January 1** ‚Äî this date is the **official birthday of the modern Internet**. - **1983**: **Domain Name System (DNS)** introduced by Paul Mockapetris ‚Äî humans get `google.com` instead of `142.250.x.x`. ### üîπ Era 3 ‚Äî The Web is Born (1989‚Äì1991) - **1989**: Tim Berners-Lee submits *"Information Management: A Proposal"* at CERN. - **1990**: First Web browser (called *WorldWideWeb*), first Web server (`info.cern.ch`), first Web page. - **1991**: WWW goes **public** outside CERN. - **1993**: **Mosaic** browser (NCSA) ‚Äî first graphical browser. - **1994**: **Netscape Navigator** released; **W3C** founded at MIT. ### üîπ Era 4 ‚Äî Commercialization & Beyond (1995 ‚Üí Today) - **1995**: NSFNET decommissioned; Internet fully commercialized. - **2004**: **Web 2.0** ‚Äî user-generated content, AJAX, social media. - **2008**: **HTML5** work begins. - **2014**: **HTML5** standardized (W3C Recommendation). - **2015+**: **Web 3.0** ‚Äî semantic web, decentralization, blockchain. --- ## 2.2 Evolution of the WWW ‚Äî Web 1.0 ‚Üí 2.0 ‚Üí 3.0 | Generation | Period | Characteristic | Key Technologies | User Role | |------------|--------|----------------|------------------|-----------| | **Web 1.0** | 1991‚Äì2004 | *Read-Only* ‚Äî static HTML pages | HTML, Basic CGI, Tables, Frames | **Passive Consumer** | | **Web 2.0** | 2004‚Äì2014 | *Read-Write* ‚Äî interactive, social | AJAX, XML/JSON, jQuery, CSS3 | **Active Contributor** | | **Web 3.0** | 2014‚ÄìPresent | *Read-Write-Execute* ‚Äî semantic, decentralized | HTML5, RDF/OWL, Blockchain, AI/ML, SPARQL | **Owner & Curator** | --- ## 2.3 Anatomy of a URL A URL is the *address* of a resource. It is parsed into **seven** parts (per RFC 3986): ``` scheme://userinfo@host:port/path?query#fragment ``` | Part | Example | Meaning | |------|---------|---------| | **scheme** | `https` | Protocol to use | | **userinfo** | `admin:1234` | Optional credentials | | **host** | `www.ktu.edu.in` | Domain name (resolves to IP) | | **port** | `443` | TCP port (default 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS) | | **path** | `/exam/results` | Resource location on server | | **query** | `?sem=5&year=2024` | Parameters sent to resource | | **fragment** | `#section2` | Client-side sub-resource | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Default Ports (Board-Exam Hotspot):** > - HTTP ‚Üí **80** > - HTTPS ‚Üí **443** > - FTP ‚Üí **21** > - SSH ‚Üí **22** --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Module 1) | # | Concept | One-Line Definition | Inventor / Year | KTU Weightage | |---|---------|---------------------|------------------|---------------| | 1 | ARPANET | First packet-switching network | ARPA / 1969 | High | | 2 | TCP/IP | Internet's core protocol suite | Cerf & Kahn / 1974 | High | | 3 | DNS | Translates domain ‚Üí IP | Mockapetris / 1983 | Medium | | 4 | WWW | Hypertext system on the Internet | T. Berners-Lee / 1989 | Very High | | 5 | URL | Address of a web resource | T. Berners-Lee / 1991 | Very High | | 6 | HTTP | Request-response protocol | T. Berners-Lee / 1991 | Very High | | 7 | HTML | Markup language for web pages | T. Berners-Lee / 1991 | Very High | | 8 | First Browser | `WorldWideWeb` | T. Berners-Lee / 1990 | Medium | | 9 | First Graphical Browser | `NCSA Mosaic` | E. Bina & M. Andreessen / 1993 | Medium | | 10 | W3C | Web standards body | Founded 1994 at MIT | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > Understanding this evolution is the *foundation* for every later KTU module ‚Äî REST APIs, server-side scripting, AJAX, and even security (HTTPS) trace their lineage directly to these 1990s innovations. # üìò SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown ## 3.1 How a Web Page Loads ‚Äî The Full Request-Response Cycle This is the **single most important KTU Module-1 analytical process.** Walk through it step-by-step (board-exam favourite for 7-mark derivations). > **Scenario:** A user types `https://www.ktu.edu.in/index.html` in Chrome. ### Step 1 ‚Äî URL Parsing (Browser-side) The browser's URL parser decomposes the input string into the seven parts (scheme, host, port, path, etc.). It detects: - **Scheme** = `https` ‚Üí use TLS + HTTP - **Host** = `www.ktu.edu.in` - **Path** = `/index.html` ### Step 2 ‚Äî DNS Resolution The browser asks the OS, which contacts the **DNS Resolver** to map: `www.ktu.edu.in ‚ü∂ 103.0.0.X (IP address)` Process: 1. Check browser cache. 2. Check OS cache. 3. Query **recursive resolver** (ISP). 4. Query **root server** ‚Üí `.in` TLD server ‚Üí `ktu.edu.in` authoritative server. 5. Return A (IPv4) or AAAA (IPv6) record. ### Step 3 ‚Äî TCP Three-Way Handshake A reliable connection is established on port 443: - Client ‚Üí `SYN` - Server ‚Üí `SYN-ACK` - Client ‚Üí `ACK` Connection is now open. ### Step 4 ‚Äî TLS Handshake (because scheme is `https`) - Client sends `ClientHello` (supported ciphers, random). - Server replies `ServerHello` + **digital certificate** (X.509). - Key exchange ‚Üí symmetric session key derived. - All subsequent traffic is **encrypted**. ### Step 5 ‚Äî HTTP Request The browser sends an **HTTP request message** to the server: ``` GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Host: www.ktu.edu.in User-Agent: Chrome/126.0 Accept: text/html Connection: keep-alive ``` ### Step 6 ‚Äî Server Processing The web server (e.g., Apache, Nginx) processes the request: - If static ‚Üí reads file from disk. - If dynamic ‚Üí invokes PHP / Node / Python handler. ### Step 7 ‚Äî HTTP Response The server sends back a **status line** + **headers** + **body**: ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Content-Length: 5432 ‚Ä¶ ``` ### Step 8 ‚Äî Browser Rendering Engine The browser's **rendering engine** (Blink / Gecko / WebKit): 1. Parses HTML ‚Üí builds **DOM tree**. 2. Parses CSS ‚Üí builds **CSSOM tree**. 3. Combines ‚Üí **Render Tree**. 4. Calculates layout (reflow). 5. Paints pixels to screen. ### üìä Analytical Summary Matrix | Stage | Component | Protocol | Time Order | |-------|-----------|----------|------------| | 1 | URL Parser | ‚Äî | t‚ÇÄ | | 2 | DNS Resolver | DNS (UDP 53) | t‚ÇÅ | | 3 | TCP Handshake | TCP | t‚ÇÇ | | 4 | TLS Handshake | TLS 1.3 | t‚ÇÉ | | 5 | HTTP Request | HTTP/S | t‚ÇÑ | | 6 | Server Handler | App logic | t‚ÇÖ | | 7 | HTTP Response | HTTP/S | t‚ÇÜ | | 8 | Rendering | HTML/CSS/JS | t‚Çá | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total Round-Trip Time (RTT) approximation:** > > $$T_{total} \;\approx\; T_{DNS} + 2RTT_{TCP} + 2RTT_{TLS} + T_{request} + T_{transfer} + T_{render}$$ > > For a typical broadband connection: **T_total ‚âà 100 ms ‚Äì 3 s** depending on payload size and server location. --- ## 3.2 Mathematical Model ‚Äî Hop Count & Latency (for analytical questions) In a packet-switched network, the **end-to-end delay** for a request of size $L$ bits across $N$ links is: $$T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} \;=\; N \cdot \left( T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} \right) + T_{queue}$$ Where the variables are defined as: | Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Definition | |--------|----------|------|------------| | $T_{prop}$ | Propagation delay | seconds | $\dfrac{d}{s}$, where $d$ = link length, $s$ ‚âà 2 √ó 10‚Å∏ m/s in fibre | | $T_{trans}$ | Transmission delay | seconds | $\dfrac{L}{R}$, where $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) | | $T_{proc}$ | Processing delay | seconds | Time spent at each router (lookup, error check) | | $T_{queue}$ | Queuing delay | seconds | Waiting time in router buffers | | $N$ | Number of hops | unitless | Number of routers traversed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Mini-Example (Board Style):** > Suppose a packet of size **L = 1200 bytes** traverses **N = 8** links, each of bandwidth **R = 1 Mbps**, propagation speed **s = 2 √ó 10‚Å∏ m/s**, link length **d = 1000 km**, and processing delay **T_proc = 1 ms**. Neglect queuing. Compute T_total. > > Step 1 ‚Äî Transmission delay: > $$T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1200 \times 8}{10^6} = 9.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 9.6\,\text{ms}$$ > > Step 1 (cont.) ‚Äî Propagation delay: > $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 5\,\text{ms}$$ > > Step 1 (cont.) ‚Äî Per-hop total: > $$T_{hop} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} = 5 + 9.6 + 1 = 15.6\,\text{ms}$$ > > Step 2 ‚Äî End-to-end total: > $$T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = N \cdot T_{hop} = 8 \times 15.6 = 124.8\,\text{ms}$$ > > **Answer: ‚âà 124.8 ms** ‚úÖ # üìò SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Internet & WWW ‚Äî Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[1969 ARPANET
4 Nodes Packet Switching] N2[1973 TCP IP Paper
Cerf and Kahn] N3[1983 ARPANET switches
to TCP IP Birth of Internet] N4[1989 Tim Berners Lee
Proposal at CERN] N5[1990 First Web Server
info.cern.ch] N6[1991 WWW Public
First Browser] N7[1993 Mosaic Browser
Graphical Web] N8[2004 Web 2.0
AJAX and Social] N9[2014 HTML5
Standard] N10[2020 plus Web 3.0
Semantic and Decentralized] N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5 --> N6 --> N7 --> N8 --> N9 --> N10 style N1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N2 fill:#2a4d7a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N3 fill:#356095,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N4 fill:#4073b0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N5 fill:#4a86cb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N6 fill:#5a99d9,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff style N7 fill:#6fade7,color:#0a2540,stroke:#ffffff style N8 fill:#84bef0,color:#0a2540,stroke:#ffffff style N9 fill:#9acff5,color:#0a2540,stroke:#ffffff style N10 fill:#b0dffa,color:#0a2540,stroke:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.2 Web Architecture ‚Äî Client / Server / Internet Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT_SIDE A1[User Browser
Chrome or Firefox] A2[Rendering Engine
Blink or Gecko] A3[HTML CSS JS Parser] end subgraph NETWORK_SIDE B1[DNS Resolver] B2[Internet Backbone
Routers and ISP] end subgraph SERVER_SIDE C1[Web Server
Apache or Nginx] C2[Application Layer
PHP or Node or Python] C3[Database
MySQL or MongoDB] end A1 -- Step 1 URL Parse --> A2 A2 -- Step 2 DNS Query --> B1 B1 -- Step 3 IP Returned --> A1 A1 -- Step 4 HTTP GET --> B2 B2 -- Step 5 Forward Request --> C1 C1 -- Step 6 Invoke App --> C2 C2 -- Step 7 Query --> C3 C3 -- Step 8 Data Return --> C2 C2 -- Step 9 HTML Response --> C1 C1 -- Step 10 HTTP Response --> B2 B2 -- Step 11 Delivery --> A1 A1 -- Step 12 Render --> A3 A3 -- Step 13 Paint to Screen --> A1 style A1 fill:#ffd966,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style C1 fill:#6fa8dc,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style B1 fill:#93c47d,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style C2 fill:#6fa8dc,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style C3 fill:#6fa8dc,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Web 1.0 / 2.0 / 3.0 Comparison ‚Äî Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph WEB1 [Web 1.0 - Read Only - 1991 to 2004] W1A[Static HTML Pages] W1B[Server Push Only] W1C[User Role Passive Consumer] end subgraph WEB2 [Web 2.0 - Read Write - 2004 to 2014] W2A[AJAX and Dynamic HTML] W2B[Social Media Platforms] W2C[User Role Active Contributor] end subgraph WEB3 [Web 3.0 - Semantic - 2014 to Present] W3A[HTML5 and APIs] W3B[AI and Blockchain] W3C[User Role Owner and Curator] end WEB1 --> WEB2 --> WEB3 style W1A fill:#cccccc,color:#000000 style W1B fill:#cccccc,color:#000000 style W1C fill:#cccccc,color:#000000 style W2A fill:#9fc5e8,color:#000000 style W2B fill:#9fc5e8,color:#000000 style W2C fill:#9fc5e8,color:#000000 style W3A fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000000 style W3B fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000000 style W3C fill:#b6d7a8,color:#000000 ``` # üìò SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## üìù PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model]** **Differentiate between the Internet and the World Wide Web. Mention the inventor of WWW.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Internet | World Wide Web | |--------|----------|----------------| | **Definition** | Global network of networks using TCP/IP | Information system of hypertext resources accessed over the Internet | | **Nature** | Infrastructure / hardware + protocol stack | Application / software layer | | **Content Carried** | Web, Email, FTP, VoIP, etc. | Only Web resources (HTML, CSS, JS) | | **Inventor** | Vint Cerf & Bob Kahn (TCP/IP) | **Sir Tim Berners-Lee** (1989, CERN) | | **Year** | 1983 (ARPANET ‚Üí TCP/IP cutover) | 1989 (proposal) / 1991 (public release) | > **Mark Split:** Definition 1 + Difference Table 1.5 + Inventor 0.5 --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model]** **With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a URL. List any two default port numbers.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** uniquely identifies a web resource. Generic syntax: ``` scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment ``` **Example:** ``` https://www.ktu.edu.in:443/results2024/index.html?sem=5#section2 ``` | Part | Value in Example | |------|------------------| | scheme | `https` | | host | `www.ktu.edu.in` | | port | `443` | | path | `/results2024/index.html` | | query | `?sem=5` | | fragment | `#section2` | **Default Ports (any two):** - HTTP ‚Üí **80** - HTTPS ‚Üí **443** - FTP ‚Üí **21** > **Mark Split:** Structure 1 + Example 1 + Ports 1 --- ## üìù PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **üõë KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** "Internet" and "WWW" ‚Äî they are *not* synonyms. Examiners *deduct 2 marks* for this. > 2. **Always mention the year** alongside the inventor's name (e.g., "Tim Berners-Lee, 1989"). A bare name costs 0.5 marks. > 3. **Skip the role of DNS or TCP handshake** in process questions, and the examiner's *most-hated* loss-of-marks is guaranteed. > 4. **Diagrams must be hand-drawn** (in the exam) but **labelled**. An unlabelled diagram = 0 marks. > 5. **URL parts** ‚Äî write the *general syntax* first, then the example. Reverse order = ‚Äì1 mark. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **evolution of the Internet** from ARPANET to the modern Internet, highlighting at least **six major milestones** with their respective years and inventors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Client‚ÄìServer architecture** of the WWW with a labelled diagram. Explain **HTTP request and response** message formats. **(7 marks)** --- #### ‚úÖ MODEL SOLUTION ‚Äî Question A ### Part (a) ‚Äî Evolution of the Internet (7 marks) | # | Year | Milestone | Inventor / Organization | Significance | |---|------|-----------|--------------------------|--------------| | 1 | **1969** | **ARPANET** commissioned | **ARPA** (DoD, USA) | First packet-switching network, 4 nodes | | 2 | **1972** | **Email** invented | **Ray Tomlinson** | Introduced `@` in email addresses | | 3 | **1973/74** | **TCP/IP protocol** designed | **Vint Cerf & Bob Kahn** | Universal language of the Internet | | 4 | **1983** | **ARPANET switches to TCP/IP** (Jan 1) | ARPANET team | Official **birth of the modern Internet** | | 5 | **1983** | **DNS** introduced | **Paul Mockapetris** | Human-readable names replace raw IPs | | 6 | **1989** | **WWW** proposed | **Tim Berners-Lee (CERN)** | Hypertext system on the Internet | | 7 | **1991** | WWW released to public | CERN | First browser `WorldWideWeb` | > **Award:** 1 mark per correct milestone (year + inventor + significance). 6 milestones = 6 marks + 1 mark for crisp intro/conclusion. ### Part (b) ‚Äî Client‚ÄìServer Architecture & HTTP (7 marks) **Architecture (4 marks):** The WWW uses a **Client‚ÄìServer model**: - **Client** = Web browser (Chrome, Firefox) ‚Äî issues HTTP requests. - **Server** = Web server (Apache, Nginx) + Application layer (PHP, Node) + Database (MySQL). - Communication is over the **Internet** using **HTTP/HTTPS** on **port 80/443**. ``` ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê HTTP Request ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê ‚îÇ Client ‚îÇ ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∂ ‚îÇ Server ‚îÇ ‚îÇ Browser ‚îÇ ‚óÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ ‚îÇ Apache ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò HTTP Response ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò ``` **HTTP Request Format (1.5 marks):** ``` GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n Host: www.ktu.edu.in\r\n User-Agent: Chrome/126\r\n Accept: text/html\r\n \r\n [Optional Body] ``` **HTTP Response Format (1.5 marks):** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n Content-Type: text/html\r\n Content-Length: 1024\r\n \r\n ... ``` > **Mark Split:** Architecture 2 + Diagram 2 + HTTP Request 1.5 + HTTP Response 1.5 --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **evolution of the World Wide Web** from Web 1.0 to Web 3.0, comparing their characteristics, technologies, and user roles. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a detailed note on **Web Browsers and Web Servers**. Compare any **two popular web servers** (Apache vs Nginx) on at least four parameters. **(7 marks)** --- #### ‚úÖ MODEL SOLUTION ‚Äî Question B ### Part (a) ‚Äî Evolution of WWW (7 marks) | Feature | **Web 1.0** | **Web 2.0** | **Web 3.0** | |---------|-------------|-------------|-------------| | **Period** | 1991‚Äì2004 | 2004‚Äì2014 | 2014‚ÄìPresent | | **Paradigm** | Read-Only | Read-Write | Read-Write-Execute | | **Content** | Static HTML | Dynamic, user-generated | Semantic, AI-driven | | **Technologies** | HTML, Tables, Frames | AJAX, XML, JSON, jQuery | HTML5, RDF, OWL, Blockchain, SPARQL | | **User Role** | Passive Consumer | Active Contributor | Owner & Curator | | **Examples** | Yahoo Directory, Britannica | Facebook, YouTube, Wikipedia | Siri, Wolfram Alpha, dApps | | **Architecture** | One-to-Many | Many-to-Many | Peer-to-Peer, decentralized | > **Mark Split:** Intro 1 + 3 rows √ó 2 marks each = 6 + crisp conclusion = full 7. ### Part (b) ‚Äî Web Browsers & Web Servers (7 marks) **Web Browser (2 marks):** - A **Web Browser** is a software application used to locate, retrieve, and display Web content. - **Examples:** Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Microsoft Edge, Safari, Opera. - **Functions:** URL parsing, DNS lookup, HTTP communication, HTML/CSS/JS rendering, caching, security sandboxing. **Web Server (2 marks):** - A **Web Server** is hardware/software that stores Web content and delivers it to clients over HTTP/HTTPS. - **Examples:** Apache HTTP Server, Nginx, Microsoft IIS, LiteSpeed. - **Functions:** Listens on port 80/443, processes requests, executes server-side scripts, returns responses. **Comparison: Apache vs Nginx (3 marks):** | Parameter | **Apache HTTP Server** | **Nginx** | |-----------|------------------------|-----------| | **Architecture** | Process-driven (one process/thread per request) | Event-driven, asynchronous (single-threaded, non-blocking) | | **Performance under load** | Slower with many concurrent connections | Excellent ‚Äî handles 10k+ concurrent connections easily | | **Static content** | Moderate speed | **Extremely fast** (designed for static content & reverse proxy) | | **Configuration** | `.htaccess` per-directory overrides (flexible) | Centralized config (no per-directory overrides) | | **Module support** | Dynamic module loading, very rich ecosystem | Modules must be compiled in (less flexible) | | **Best suited for** | Dynamic content, complex apps, shared hosting | High-traffic sites, reverse proxy, load balancing | | **License** | Apache License 2.0 | BSD-like (2-clause) | | **Year / Author** | 1995, Apache Group / NCSA | 2004, Igor Sysoev (Russia) | > **Mark Split:** Browser def 1 + examples 1 + Server def 1 + examples 1 + Apache vs Nginx 3 + table 1 = full 7 --- ## üßÝ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ‚úÖ **Internet** = network of networks using **TCP/IP**; **WWW** = hypertext system **on top of** the Internet. - ‚úÖ **Tim Berners-Lee** invented the **WWW** at **CERN** in **1989**; publicly released in **1991**. - ‚úÖ **ARPANET** (1969, 4 nodes) ‚Üí **TCP/IP** (Cerf & Kahn, 1974) ‚Üí **DNS** (Mockapetris, 1983) ‚Üí **WWW** (TBL, 1989). - ‚úÖ **January 1, 1983** is the **official birthday** of the modern Internet (ARPANET TCP/IP cutover). - ‚úÖ A **URL** has 7 parts: `scheme://userinfo@host:port/path?query#fragment`. - ‚úÖ **Default Ports:** HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, FTP = 21, SSH = 22. - ‚úÖ **Web 1.0 = Read-Only** (Static) | **Web 2.0 = Read-Write** (AJAX, Social) | **Web 3.0 = Semantic + Decentralized** (AI, Blockchain). - ‚úÖ **First Browser** = `WorldWideWeb` (TBL, 1990) | **First Graphical Browser** = `NCSA Mosaic` (1993). - ‚úÖ **W3C** (World Wide Web Consortium) was founded in **1994** at **MIT** to maintain Web standards. - ‚úÖ **HTTP** is *stateless*, *request-response*, *text-based* (line-oriented) on **port 80**. - ‚úÖ **HTTPS** = HTTP + **TLS/SSL** encryption on **port 443** ‚Äî used for secure transactions. - ‚úÖ **Client** = Browser (issues request) | **Server** = Web/App server (returns response). - ‚úÖ **Apache** = process-based, `.htaccess`, rich modules | **Nginx** = event-driven, fast for static & reverse proxy. - ‚úÖ **End-to-end delay formula** (for analytical problems): $$T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = N \cdot (T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc}) + T_{queue}$$ - ‚úÖ **First email** by **Ray Tomlinson** (1972), introduced the **`@`** sign. - ‚úÖ **HTML** = HyperText Markup Language ‚Äî the *document format* of the Web. - ‚úÖ Always remember the **Request-Response cycle**: URL Parse ‚Üí DNS ‚Üí TCP ‚Üí TLS ‚Üí HTTP Request ‚Üí Server ‚Üí HTTP Response ‚Üí Render. > **üéØ Final KTU Tip:** In the exam, always write the *year* beside every inventor's name and *draw* at least one diagram per 14-mark question ‚Äî KTU examiners award **1‚Äì2 grace marks** for well-labelled diagrams.

topics.title: URIs and URL - MIME
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** URIs and URL - MIME # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > A **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** is a compact sequence of characters that identifies an abstract or physical resource on the World Wide Web. It is the **superset** that defines how resources are named and addressed, governed by **IETF RFC 3986**. A URI is essentially a **string of characters** that serves as a unique identifier for *any* resource ‚Äî a document, an image, a service, a book, or even a person. Think of it as the *identity card* of every web resource. ### üéØ Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Postal Address" System Imagine India's postal system: | Web Concept | Real-World Postal Analogy | |---|---| | **Resource** | A house or office | | **URI** | The full formatted address (Pin + State + City + Street + House No.) | | **URL** | A complete address with a *specific delivery method* (Speed Post, Courier) | | **URN** | A name-only reference (e.g., "Taj Mahal") ‚Äî locates by *name*, not by location | Every URL is a URI, but not every URI is a URL. **A URI is the broad category; URL and URN are its two specialized subsets.** --- ## 1.2 URLs (Uniform Resource Locators) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** is a specific type of URI that not only identifies a resource but also provides the **means of accessing it** by describing its primary access mechanism (protocol) and its network location. A URL answers two questions: 1. **What** is the resource? (Name/Identifier) 2. **How** do I get it? (Protocol + Location) ### General Syntax of a URL The general format follows the convention: ``` scheme://authority/path?query#fragment ``` Where each component has a strict meaning: - **`scheme`** ‚Üí Protocol used (http, https, ftp, file) - **`authority`** ‚Üí `[userinfo@]host[:port]` ‚Äî server identity - **`path`** ‚Üí Hierarchical location on the server - **`query`** ‚Üí Parameters passed to the resource (after `?`) - **`fragment`** ‚Üí Sub-resource locator (after `#`) --- ## 1.3 URNs (Uniform Resource Names) > [!NOTE] > **URN** is a URI that identifies a resource by **name in a particular namespace**, without implying its location or how to retrieve it. > Example: `urn:isbn:9788120346548` uniquely identifies a book by its ISBN, regardless of where the book is stored. --- ## 1.4 MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **MIME** is an internet standard originally defined in **RFC 2045 (1996)** by Nathaniel Borenstein and Ned Freed. It extends the format of email messages to support text in character sets other than ASCII, non-text attachments (audio, video, images, programs), and multiple parts within a single message. In modern Web Programming, MIME is the **standard mechanism for indicating the type and format of a document** transmitted over HTTP. ### üéØ Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Product Label" System Imagine every package delivered to your house has a **sticker label** saying: - *"Fragile ‚Äî Glass"* - *"This side up ‚Äî Liquid"* - *"Refrigerated ‚Äî Dairy"* MIME types do exactly this for **data packets on the web**. They tell the browser (or receiver): *"Hey, the following data is of type X ‚Äî handle it accordingly."* Without MIME, a browser receiving data wouldn't know whether to display it as an HTML page, render it as an image, save it as a PDF, or hand it off to a plugin. --- ## 1.5 The Relationship: URI vs URL vs URN vs MIME The following hierarchical relationship clarifies these concepts: ``` ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê ‚îÇ URI ‚îÇ (Identity, broad category) ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò ‚îÇ ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê ‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê ‚îÇ URL ‚îÇ ‚îÇ URN ‚îÇ ‚îÇ (Locator) ‚îÇ ‚îÇ (Name) ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò MIME ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∂ A separate standard describing CONTENT FORMAT (used in HTTP Content-Type header) ``` > [!NOTE] > **MIME is *orthogonal* to URI/URL/URN.** URI identifies *where/what* a resource is; MIME describes *what kind* of data the resource contains. Both work together in HTTP transactions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a URI ‚Äî RFC 3986 Decomposition According to **RFC 3986**, a URI is composed of hierarchical components. Below is the rigorous structural breakdown used in KTU-level questions: | Component | Symbol | Description | Example (from URL below) | |---|---|---|---| | **Scheme** | `scheme:` | Protocol identifier ‚Äî must start with a letter, followed by letters/digits/+/-/. | `https` | | **Authority** | `//authority` | Optional. Contains userinfo, host, and port. | `//www.ktu.edu.in:8080` | | **Userinfo** | `userinfo@` | Optional. Username and (rarely) password. | `admin@` | | **Host** | `host` | Domain name or IP address. | `www.ktu.edu.in` | | **Port** | `:port` | Optional. Defaults to scheme's standard port. | `:8080` | | **Path** | `/path` | Hierarchical path to the resource. | `/results/publish` | | **Query** | `?query` | Non-hierarchical data (key=value pairs). | `?dept=CSE&sem=5` | | **Fragment** | `#fragment` | Sub-resource identifier (client-side only). | `#section2` | ### Example ‚Äî Complete Decomposition Given the URL: ``` https://admin@www.ktu.edu.in:8080/results/publish?dept=CSE&sem=5#timetable ``` The decomposition is: - **Scheme** ‚Üí `https` - **Authority** ‚Üí `//admin@www.ktu.edu.in:8080` - **Userinfo** ‚Üí `admin` - **Host** ‚Üí `www.ktu.edu.in` - **Port** ‚Üí `8080` - **Path** ‚Üí `/results/publish` - **Query** ‚Üí `?dept=CSE&sem=5` - **Fragment** ‚Üí `#timetable` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The fragment (`#...`) is **never sent to the server** during an HTTP request. It is processed purely by the *client-side* browser to scroll to a specific section. --- ## 2.2 Classification of URIs ### A. Absolute vs Relative URI | Type | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Absolute URI** | Contains a scheme. Self-contained. | `https://ktu.edu.in/syllabus.pdf` | | **Relative URI** | Referenced against a base URI. No scheme. | `/syllabus.pdf` or `../docs/syllabus.pdf` | ### B. Opaque vs Hierarchical URI - **Opaque URI**: Scheme followed by scheme-specific data. Example: `mailto:student@ktu.edu.in` - **Hierarchical URI**: Follows the `//authority/path` structure. Example: `http://example.com/a/b` ### C. URL Encoding (Percent-Encoding) > [!IMPORTANT] > URIs may only contain a limited set of ASCII characters. Any character outside this set must be encoded using **percent-encoding**, where the byte value is represented as `%XX` in hexadecimal. The encoding formula used by the browser is: $$ \text{Encoded Character} = \% \;\; \text{followed by} \;\; \text{Hex(Byte Value)} $$ **Example:** A space character (ASCII 32 = `0x20`) is encoded as `%20`. | Original Char | ASCII Code | Encoded Form | |---|---|---| | Space | 32 | `%20` | | `@` | 64 | `%40` | | `#` | 35 | `%23` | | `&` | 38 | `%26` | | `+` | 43 | `%2B` | --- ## 2.3 MIME Type Architecture ‚Äî Deep Dive MIME types follow a strict two-part hierarchical structure: $$ \text{type} \;\big/ \;\text{subtype} $$ The official IANA registry categorizes MIME types into **9 top-level discrete types** and **multipart composite types**: ### The 9 Discrete (Top-Level) MIME Types | # | Type | Meaning | Example Subtypes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `application` | Binary data, executable streams | `application/pdf`, `application/json`, `application/octet-stream` | | 2 | `audio` | Sound data | `audio/mpeg`, `audio/wav`, `audio/ogg` | | 3 | `example` | Reserved for examples | `example/foo` | | 4 | `font` | Font data | `font/ttf`, `font/woff`, `font/otf` | | 5 | `image` | Static visual data | `image/jpeg`, `image/png`, `image/gif`, `image/svg+xml` | | 6 | `model` | 3D / structured data | `model/3mf`, `model/gltf+json` | | 7 | `text` | Human-readable text | `text/plain`, `text/html`, `text/css`, `text/csv` | | 8 | `video` | Moving visual + audio | `video/mp4`, `video/webm`, `video/quicktime` | | 9 | `*` | Wildcard ‚Äî matches any type | `*/*` | ### Multipart MIME Types Used when a single message contains multiple independent body parts (commonly used in form submissions and emails): | Multipart Type | Use Case | |---|---| | `multipart/form-data` | HTML form submission with file uploads | | `multipart/mixed` | Email with attachments | | `multipart/byteranges` | HTTP 206 Partial Content responses | | `multipart/alternative` | Same content in different formats (e.g., plain + HTML email) | --- ## 2.4 How MIME Works Inside HTTP When a web server responds to a browser request, it includes MIME information in two key headers: ### A. `Content-Type` Header Tells the browser **what kind of data** is being sent. ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Content-Length: 1024 ``` ### B. `Content-Disposition` Header Tells the browser **how to handle** the data ‚Äî display inline or trigger a download. ``` Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.pdf" ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The default MIME type in HTTP is `application/octet-stream`, which means *"unknown binary data"* ‚Äî the browser will always offer it as a download rather than rendering it. --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility ‚Äî Why Study URI & MIME? | Concept | Real-World Web Application | |---|---| | **URI Design** | REST API endpoints (e.g., `/api/v1/students/{id}`) | | **Query Strings** | Search filters, pagination, analytics tracking | | **URL Encoding** | Sending user-supplied input safely in URLs | | **MIME Types** | File upload validation, content negotiation, CDN caching | | **Content-Type** | Browser rendering decisions, security headers (X-Content-Type-Options) | | **Multipart/form-data** | File upload forms, document submission portals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** MIME-type confusion attacks (e.g., uploading a `.html` file as `image/jpeg`) are a real security vulnerability class ‚Äî proper MIME validation is essential in server-side web programming. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of URI Resolution ‚Äî Relative to Absolute Conversion When a browser receives a relative URI, it must resolve it against a **Base URI**. This follows **RFC 3986 Section 5 ‚Äî Reference Resolution Algorithm**. ### Step-by-Step Resolution Procedure Given: - **Base URI:** `https://ktu.edu.in/api/v1/students/index.html` - **Relative Reference:** `../courses/cs501.html` **Step 1:** Decompose both URIs into their hierarchical components. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Base URI Components:} \quad & \text{scheme} = \text{https} \\ & \text{authority} = \text{ktu.edu.in} \\ & \text{path} = \text{/api/v1/students/index.html} \\ \text{Relative Ref:} \quad & \text{path} = \text{../courses/cs501.html} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the "merge" base directory by removing the last path segment from the base URI's path. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Base Dir} &= \text{path of base URI with last segment removed} \\ &= \text{/api/v1/students/index.html} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{/api/v1/students/} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Apply the **Remove Dot Segments** algorithm (RFC 3986 ¬ß5.2.4) to the merge result. Each `.` removes the current segment; each `..` removes the parent segment. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input Path:} \quad & \text{/api/v1/students/} \;+\; \text{../courses/cs501.html} \\ &= \text{/api/v1/students/../courses/cs501.html} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply each dot-segment transformation in sequence. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Raw concatenated path:} \quad & \text{/api/v1/students/../courses/cs501.html} \end{aligned} $$ Reading left to right: - `/api/` ‚Üí keep - `v1/` ‚Üí keep - `students/` ‚Üí keep - `../` ‚Üí remove the immediately preceding segment (`students/`) - `courses/cs501.html` ‚Üí keep $$ \begin{aligned} \text{After resolution:} \quad & \text{/api/v1/courses/cs501.html} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Reattach the scheme and authority from the base URI to form the final absolute URI. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Final Resolved URI} \quad &= \text{scheme} + \text{"://"} + \text{authority} + \text{resolved path} \\ &= \text{https} + \text{"://"} + \text{ktu.edu.in} + \text{/api/v1/courses/cs501.html} \\ &= \text{https://ktu.edu.in/api/v1/courses/cs501.html} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Percent-Encoding Algorithm When encoding any unsafe character into a URI-safe form: **Input:** Character with ASCII code $C$ (an integer between 0 and 127). **Step 1:** Verify $C$ is NOT in the unreserved character set: $$ \text{Unreserved} = \{A\text{-}Z,\; a\text{-}z,\; 0\text{-}9,\; -,\; .,\; \_\;,\; \sim\} $$ **Step 2:** If unsafe, convert $C$ into two hexadecimal digits: $$ \text{High Hex Digit} = \lfloor C / 16 \rfloor \quad \text{(base 16 representation)} $$ $$ \text{Low Hex Digit} = C \;\bmod\; 16 $$ **Step 3:** Construct the encoded triplet. $$ \text{Encoded Output} \;=\; \text{"\%"} \;+\; \text{HighHex} \;+\; \text{LowHex} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example **Encode** the space character, ASCII code $C = 32$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{High Hex Digit} &= \lfloor 32 / 16 \rfloor = \lfloor 2.0 \rfloor = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"2"} \\ \text{Low Hex Digit} &= 32 \;\bmod\; 16 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"0"} \\ \text{Encoded Output} &= \text{"\%"} + \text{"2"} + \text{"0"} = \text{"\%20"} \end{aligned} $$ **Encode** the `@` character, ASCII code $C = 64$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{High Hex Digit} &= \lfloor 64 / 16 \rfloor = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"4"} \\ \text{Low Hex Digit} &= 64 \;\bmod\; 16 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"0"} \\ \text{Encoded Output} &= \text{"\%"} + \text{"4"} + \text{"0"} = \text{"\%40"} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî MIME Type Lookup Table This is the **high-yield reference matrix** for KTU Web Programming answers: | HTTP Context | Content-Type Value | Browser Action | |---|---|---| | Static HTML page | `text/html; charset=UTF-8` | Render as HTML | | Plain text log | `text/plain` | Render as monospaced text | | CSS stylesheet | `text/css` | Parse and apply as CSS | | JavaScript file | `application/javascript` | Execute as JS | | JSON API response | `application/json` | Parse and consume as JSON | | Form submission (no files) | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | Parse key=value pairs | | Form submission (with files) | `multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundaryXYZ` | Parse multipart payload | | PNG image | `image/png` | Render using image viewer | | JPEG image | `image/jpeg` | Render using image viewer | | SVG vector | `image/svg+xml` | Render as scalable vector | | PDF document | `application/pdf` | Open in PDF viewer plugin | | ZIP archive | `application/zip` | Trigger download dialog | | Generic binary | `application/octet-stream` | Trigger download dialog | | MP4 video | `video/mp4` | Play with video player | | MP3 audio | `audio/mpeg` | Play with audio player | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example ‚Äî Constructing a Multipart MIME Message Consider an HTML form uploading two files: a profile picture and a CV PDF. **Step 1:** The browser constructs the boundary string. A common format is: $$ \text{boundary} = \text{"------WebKitFormBoundary"} + \text{randomHex} $$ **Step 2:** The browser sends the following multipart body: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{--}\langle \text{boundary} \rangle & \\ \text{Content-Disposition: form-data;} \quad \text{name="profile"}; \quad \text{filename="photo.jpg"} & \\ \text{Content-Type: image/jpeg} & \\ \langle \text{binary JPEG data} \rangle & \\ \text{--}\langle \text{boundary} \rangle & \\ \text{Content-Disposition: form-data;} \quad \text{name="cv"}; \quad \text{filename="resume.pdf"} & \\ \text{Content-Type: application/pdf} & \\ \langle \text{binary PDF data} \rangle & \\ \text{--}\langle \text{boundary} \rangle \text{--} & \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** The HTTP request header is set to: $$ \text{Content-Type} = \text{multipart/form-data}; \quad \text{boundary}=\langle \text{boundary} \rangle $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The trailing double hyphen `--` after the final boundary marks the end of the multipart message. A single hyphen `--` separates individual parts. --- ## 3.5 Quick Algorithm ‚Äî Classifying Any URI For any candidate URI string $S$: $$ \text{Type}(S) = \begin{cases} \text{URL} & \text{if } S \text{ contains scheme} + \text{"://"} \text{ and a host} \\ \text{URN} & \text{if } S \text{ begins with } \text{"urn:"} \text{ and has no retrieval mechanism} \\ \text{OpAQUE URI} & \text{if } S \text{ has a scheme but no } \text{"//"} \text{ authority} \\ \text{RELATIVE REF} & \text{if } S \text{ has no scheme and no authority} \\ \text{INVALID} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 URI/URL/URN Hierarchy (RFC 3986 Venn-style) ```mermaid graph TD uriNode["URI - Uniform Resource Identifier
Identifies any resource"] urlNode["URL - Uniform Resource Locator
Identifies AND locates via protocol"] urnNode["URN - Uniform Resource Name
Identifies by name in a namespace"] opaque["Opaque URI
e.g. mailto:user at domain.com"] relref["Relative Reference
e.g. ../docs/file.pdf"] uriNode --> urlNode uriNode --> urnNode uriNode --> opaque uriNode --> relref urlNode --> urlEx1["http://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus"] urlNode --> urlEx2["https://api.ktu.edu.in:8080/v1/students?id=42"] urnNode --> urnEx["urn:isbn:9788120346548"] opaque --> opaqueEx["tel:+91-471-2598000"] relref --> relEx["../students/list.html"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Anatomy of a URL ‚Äî Component Decomposition Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A["Scheme
https"] --> B["://"] B --> C["Userinfo
admin"] C --> D["@"] D --> E["Host
www.ktu.edu.in"] E --> F[":Port
8080"] F --> G["Path
/results/publish"] G --> H["?Query
dept=CSE&sem=5"] H --> I["#Fragment
timetable"] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000 style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 MIME Type Classification Tree ```mermaid graph TD root["MIME Type
type/subtype"] root --> discrete["Discrete Types
9 Top-Level Categories"] root --> multipart["Multipart Types
Composite Messages"] discrete --> app["application
Binary / Programmatic"] discrete --> audio["audio
Sound Files"] discrete --> image["image
Static Visuals"] discrete --> text["text
Human-Readable"] discrete --> video["video
Moving Visuals"] discrete --> font["font
Typography"] discrete --> model["model
3D Structures"] app --> appEx["application/pdf
application/json
application/javascript
application/octet-stream"] text --> textEx["text/html
text/plain
text/css
text/csv"] image --> imgEx["image/jpeg
image/png
image/gif
image/svg+xml"] multipart --> mpart["multipart/form-data
multipart/mixed
multipart/byteranges
multipart/alternative"] style root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style discrete fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style multipart fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 HTTP Request/Response Flow ‚Äî Role of MIME ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant B as Browser Client participant S as Web Server participant FS as File System B->>S: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.ktu.edu.in
Accept: text/html, application/json Note over S: Server reads request
Determines MIME type from
file extension or config S->>FS: Read /var/www/html/index.html FS-->>S: HTML bytes S-->>B: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 4096
[HTML Body] Note over B: Browser reads Content-Type
Renders as HTML B->>S: POST /upload HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----XYZ
[Multipart body with files] Note over S: Server parses MIME boundary
Extracts each part
Validates per-file Content-Type S-->>B: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
{"status":"uploaded"} ``` --- ## 4.5 URL Encoding Pipeline ‚Äî Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR inp["Raw Input String
Hello World & More"] sep["Tokenizer
Splits into characters"] check{"Is char in
Unreserved Set?"} keep["Keep ASCII
as-is"] enc["Percent-Encode
%HH Hex form"] out["URL-Safe String
Hello%20World%20%26%20More"] inp --> sep --> check check -- Yes --> keep --> out check -- No --> enc --> out style inp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style check fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## üìò Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **Differentiate between URI, URL, and URN with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > A **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** is the generic superset of all resource identifiers on the web. A **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** is a URI that not only identifies a resource but also specifies *how to access it* (scheme + location). A **URN (Uniform Resource Name)** is a URI that identifies a resource by *name within a namespace*, independent of location. > > - URI example: `https://ktu.edu.in/syllabus` (also acts as URL) > - URL example: `http://www.example.com/index.html` > - URN example: `urn:isbn:9788120346548` > > Every URL and every URN is a URI, but the reverse is not always true. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` **What is MIME? Explain its structure with two examples.** **Model Answer:** > **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** is an internet standard (originally RFC 2045) used to indicate the **nature and format of a document, file, or data stream** transmitted over the internet. In HTTP, MIME types are conveyed through the `Content-Type` header. > > MIME types follow the hierarchical structure: **`type/subtype`** > > **Examples:** > 1. `text/html` ‚Üí `text` is the type (human-readable); `html` is the subtype (HTML markup). > 2. `image/jpeg` ‚Üí `image` is the type (static visual); `jpeg` is the subtype (JPEG format). > > MIME enables browsers to correctly render, display, or download received data based on its declared type. --- ## üìï Part B ‚Äî Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **omit the scheme** when listing URL components ‚Äî always include it. > - Confusing **URI with URL** in answers loses 1‚Äì2 marks; be precise. > - For MIME questions, students forget the **`charset` parameter** in `Content-Type` headers ‚Äî mention it when relevant. > - Don't confuse **URN** with URL; URNs do NOT contain retrieval mechanism. > - For percent-encoding, show the **conversion calculation** (decimal ‚Üí hex) ‚Äî don't skip steps. --- ### üî∑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **general syntax of a URI** as per RFC 3986. Identify and describe each component using the example `https://admin@www.ktu.edu.in:8080/results?dept=CSE#timetable`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **MIME types** in detail. Discuss the top-level discrete types and multipart types with examples. Describe how MIME is used in HTTP responses. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ‚úÖ Solution to Question A (a) **URI General Syntax (RFC 3986):** The generic URI syntax as defined in RFC 3986 is: $$ \text{URI} = \text{scheme} \;:\; \text{hier{-}part} \;[\; \text{?} \; \text{query} \;] \;[\; \# \; \text{fragment} \;] $$ Where: $$ \text{hier{-}part} = \text{"//"} \; \text{authority} \; \text{path{-}abempty} \;\;\big/\;\; \text{path{-}absolute} \;\;\big/\;\; \text{path{-}rootless} \;\;\big/\;\; \text{path{-}empty} $$ **Component Breakdown of the given URI:** | Component | Value Extracted | Description | |---|---|---| | Scheme | `https` | Protocol ‚Äî secure HTTP | | Authority | `admin@www.ktu.edu.in:8080` | Server identification + optional credentials | | Userinfo | `admin` | Username portion before `@` | | Host | `www.ktu.edu.in` | Domain name of the server | | Port | `8080` | Non-default TCP port (default for https is 443) | | Path | `/results` | Hierarchical location of the resource on the server | | Query | `dept=CSE` | Key-value parameter passed to the resource | | Fragment | `timetable` | Client-side anchor reference | > The **fragment** is processed entirely by the browser and is **never sent to the server** during the HTTP request. --- #### ‚úÖ Solution to Question A (b) **MIME Types ‚Äî Detailed Explanation:** MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions), standardized in **RFC 2045 and RFC 2046**, is the mechanism used to indicate the type of data carried in an internet message or HTTP transaction. The format is: $$ \text{MIME} = \text{type} \; / \; \text{subtype} \; [\;\text{;} \; \text{parameter}=\text{value}\;] $$ **Top-Level Discrete Types (9 Categories):** 1. `application` ‚Äî Binary/programmatic data (e.g., `application/pdf`, `application/json`) 2. `audio` ‚Äî Sound files (e.g., `audio/mpeg`, `audio/wav`) 3. `example` ‚Äî Placeholder for documentation 4. `font` ‚Äî Font files (e.g., `font/woff2`) 5. `image` ‚Äî Static images (e.g., `image/jpeg`, `image/png`) 6. `model` ‚Äî 3D model files (e.g., `model/gltf+json`) 7. `text` ‚Äî Plain text (e.g., `text/html`, `text/plain`, `text/css`) 8. `video` ‚Äî Video files (e.g., `video/mp4`) 9. `*` ‚Äî Wildcard match (`*/*`) **Multipart Composite Types:** Multipart types combine multiple body parts into one message, separated by a boundary string. Examples include: - `multipart/form-data` ‚Äî HTML form uploads - `multipart/mixed` ‚Äî Email with attachments - `multipart/byteranges` ‚Äî Partial HTTP responses - `multipart/alternative` ‚Äî Same content in multiple formats **Use in HTTP:** The server includes the `Content-Type` header in its HTTP response: ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 ``` The browser uses this to decide rendering. The default fallback is `application/octet-stream`, which prompts a download dialog. --- ### üî∑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 ‚Äî Alternative Choice]` **(a)** What is **URL encoding**? Explain the percent-encoding mechanism with two examples. Show the encoding of the string `"Kerala & Co"` step-by-step. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **classification of URIs** into URL, URN, Opaque URI, and Relative Reference. Discuss the role of **Content-Type and Content-Disposition** HTTP headers in MIME communication. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ‚úÖ Solution to Question B (a) **URL Encoding (Percent-Encoding):** URL encoding is the mechanism for converting characters that are not allowed in a URI into a universally compatible ASCII format. It is governed by **RFC 3986**. **Mechanism:** 1. Identify the unsafe or reserved character. 2. Convert its ASCII decimal value to hexadecimal. 3. Prefix with the `%` symbol. **Example 1:** Encode space (ASCII 32 = `0x20`) ‚Üí `%20` **Example 2:** Encode `&` (ASCII 38 = `0x26`) ‚Üí `%26` **Step-by-step encoding of `"Kerala & Co"`:** | Character | ASCII Decimal | Hex Value | Encoded Form | |---|---|---|---| | `K` | 75 | `4B` | `K` (safe, unreserved) | | `e` | 101 | `65` | `e` (safe) | | `r` | 114 | `72` | `r` (safe) | | `a` | 97 | `61` | `a` (safe) | | `l` | 108 | `6C` | `l` (safe) | | `a` | 97 | `61` | `a` (safe) | | space | 32 | `20` | `%20` | | `&` | 38 | `26` | `%26` | | space | 32 | `20` | `%20` | | `C` | 67 | `43` | `C` (safe) | | `o` | 111 | `6F` | `o` (safe) | $$ \text{Final Encoded String} = \text{Kerala\%20\%26\%20Co} $$ --- #### ‚úÖ Solution to Question B (b) **Classification of URIs:** ``` URI ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê URL URN Opaque URI Relative Reference (Locates) (Names) (Scheme-specific) (No scheme) ``` - **URL** ‚Üí Identifies AND locates a resource. Example: `http://example.com/page` - **URN** ‚Üí Identifies a resource by name in a namespace. Example: `urn:isbn:9781234567890` - **Opaque URI** ‚Üí Has a scheme but no `//authority`. Example: `mailto:user@ktu.edu.in`, `tel:+91-471-1234` - **Relative Reference** ‚Üí Resolved against a base URI. Example: `../docs/file.html` **Content-Type and Content-Disposition Headers:** **`Content-Type`** declares the MIME type of the body so the browser knows how to interpret it: ``` Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 ``` **`Content-Disposition`** controls presentation ‚Äî either inline display or attachment download: ``` Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.pdf" ``` Together, these headers form the **core MIME communication contract** between server and client in HTTP transactions. --- ## üîÅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ‚úÖ **URI is the superset; URL and URN are subsets.** Not all URIs are URLs, but all URLs are URIs. - ‚úÖ **General URL syntax:** `scheme://userinfo@host:port/path?query#fragment` - ‚úÖ **Fragment (`#...`) is NEVER sent to the server** ‚Äî it is purely client-side. - ‚úÖ **Percent-encoding** converts unsafe characters to `%XX` hex form. - ‚úÖ **MIME = `type/subtype`** structure, defined in RFC 2045/2046. - ‚úÖ **9 discrete top-level types:** application, audio, example, font, image, model, text, video, * (wildcard). - ‚úÖ **Multipart types** combine multiple body parts using boundary delimiters (used in `multipart/form-data` uploads). - ‚úÖ **HTTP `Content-Type` header** carries MIME info; default is `application/octet-stream`. - ‚úÖ **`Content-Disposition`** controls inline display vs file download behavior. - ‚úÖ **URN example to remember:** `urn:isbn:9788120346548` - ‚úÖ **Email-like URIs are *opaque*:** `mailto:student@ktu.edu.in` has scheme but no `//` authority. - ‚úÖ **Security caveat:** Always validate MIME type server-side to prevent MIME-confusion attacks.

topics.title: Introduction to HTML5: Structuring and Editing an HTML5 Document
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to HTML5: Structuring and Editing an HTML5 Document # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of HTML5 **HTML5** is the fifth and current major version of the **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** standard, which is the foundational language used to structure and present content on the World Wide Web. As per the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** Recommendation published on **28 October 2014**, HTML5 is a markup language designed to deliver rich internet applications without the need for additional proprietary plugins (such as Flash). It extends, improves, and rationalizes the markup available for documents, and introduces **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)** and **Document Object Model (DOM)** features that facilitate complex web applications. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCNT503 ‚Äì Module 1):** > *"Introduction to HTML5: Structuring and editing an HTML5 document."* > This topic directly maps to **CO1**: *"Apply HTML5 and CSS3 to design and develop responsive, semantic web pages."* For KTU examinations, students must remember that HTML5 is **not** owned by a single body ‚Äî it is jointly maintained by **WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)** as a "Living Standard" and **W3C**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "Building a House" Think of an HTML5 document as the **blueprint of a house**: | HTML5 Component | Real-World House Analogy | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `` | The building permit/plan approval notice | Tells the browser which version of HTML rules to follow | | `` | The plot of land (boundary wall) | The root container of the entire document | | `` | The architect's office (not visible to guests) | Holds metadata, title, links to CSS/JS files | | `` | The nameplate on the building | Shown on the browser tab | | `<body>` | The actual rooms, furniture, and decor | All visible content rendered on screen | | `<meta charset="UTF-8">` | The language translator on the door | Ensures characters display correctly | | `<meta viewport>` | The architectural scale of the door/window | Controls how page scales on mobile devices | Just as a house **must** have a foundation, walls, and roof in a specific order, an HTML5 document **must** have its tags arranged in a strict hierarchical structure to render correctly. --- ## 1.3 Evolution Timeline ‚Äî How HTML5 Came to Be $$\text{HTML 1.0 (1993)} \rightarrow \text{HTML 2.0 (1995)} \rightarrow \text{HTML 3.2 (1997)} \rightarrow \text{HTML 4.01 (1999)} \rightarrow \text{XHTML 1.0 (2000)} \rightarrow \text{HTML5 (2014 W3C / 2004 WHATWG)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Historical Fact for Exams:** > - HTML5 was first drafted by **WHATWG** in **2004**. > - It became a **W3C Recommendation on 28 October 2014**. > - HTML5 replaced both **HTML 4.01, XHTML 1.0, and XHTML 2.0**. > - It uses the **MIME type `text/html`** (unlike XHTML which used `application/xhtml+xml`). --- ## 1.4 Salient Features of HTML5 The salient features every KTU student must memorize: 1. **Semantic Elements** ‚Äî `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, `<footer>` provide meaning to structure. 2. **Native Multimedia** ‚Äî `<audio>` and `<video>` tags eliminate the need for Flash/QuickTime. 3. **Graphics APIs** ‚Äî `<canvas>` for 2D/3D bitmaps, `<svg>` for vector graphics. 4. **Form Enhancements** ‚Äî New input types (`date`, `email`, `url`, `number`, `range`, `color`). 5. **Offline Storage** ‚Äî `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, `IndexedDB`. 6. **Geolocation API** ‚Äî Detects user's geographical position. 7. **Drag and Drop API** ‚Äî Native drag/drop operations. 8. **Web Workers** ‚Äî Background JavaScript threads. 9. **Cross-document Messaging** ‚Äî Secure cross-origin communication. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exam:** *"Some Native Graphics Form Offline Geolocation Drag Cross"* ‚Äî covers all 9 key HTML5 features above. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics While HTML is a markup (not physics) language, certain **standard numeric values** are fixed by W3C and examiners love asking them: | Standard Constant | Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | DOCTYPE keyword | `html` (lowercase) | Shortest valid doctype in HTML5 | | Default Character Set | **UTF-8** | Universal Unicode encoding | | Viewport default width | **980 pixels** (mobile fallback) | CSS pixels before meta viewport | | `<canvas>` default size | **300 √ó 150** pixels | HTML5 Canvas default dimensions | | HTML5 video max frame rate | No fixed limit (browser-dependent) | Modern codecs support 60fps | | Minimum HTML5 document | **131 bytes** (theoretically) | Doctype + html + head + title + body | --- ## 1.6 Editing an HTML5 Document ‚Äî Definition **Editing an HTML5 document** refers to the process of **authoring, modifying, validating, and saving** the markup text using software tools that range from simple plain-text editors to advanced **Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)**. The editing process typically involves four stages: 1. **Authoring** ‚Äî Writing tags and content in a text editor. 2. **Saving** ‚Äî Storing with `.html` or `.htm` extension. 3. **Rendering** ‚Äî Opening in a browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) to view output. 4. **Validating** ‚Äî Using the **W3C Markup Validation Service** (`https://validator.w3.org/`) to check conformance. <!-- SECTION_1_END --> <!-- SECTION_2_START --> # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an HTML5 Document ‚Äî The Five Mandatory Building Blocks Every valid HTML5 document MUST contain exactly these five elements in this order: $$\text{DOCTYPE} \rightarrow \langle html \rangle \rightarrow \langle head \rangle \rightarrow \langle title \rangle \rightarrow \langle body \rangle$$ ### Block 1: The DOCTYPE Declaration ```html <!DOCTYPE html> ``` **Why it exists:** Before HTML5, doctypes were long and confusing: - HTML 4.01 Strict: `<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/strict.dtd">` - XHTML 1.0: `<!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Strict//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-strict.dtd">` **How it works:** The DOCTYPE is **case-insensitive** but conventionally written in uppercase for readability. It triggers **standards mode** in browsers instead of **quirks mode** (legacy IE5 behavior). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse DOCTYPE with an HTML tag. It is **NOT a tag** ‚Äî it is a *declaration*. It does **not** have a closing tag and contains **no attributes** in HTML5. ### Block 2: The Root `<html>` Element ```html <html lang="en"> ``` **Why:** Acts as the root container for all other elements. The `lang` attribute (language identifier, e.g., `en`, `fr`, `ml`) helps screen readers and search engines. ### Block 3: The `<head>` Section Contains **machine-readable metadata** about the document. Common child elements: | Element | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `<title>` | Browser tab text, bookmark name, SEO | `<title>My Page` | | `` | Character encoding declaration | `` | | `` | Responsive design control | `` | | `` | SEO snippet in search results | `` | | `` | External resource (CSS, favicon) | `` | | `` | | `` | | `` | Base URL for relative links | `` | ### Block 4: The `` Element **Crucial rule:** A document MUST have exactly ONE `<title>` element inside `<head>`. It is required and cannot be omitted. ### Block 5: The `<body>` Section Contains all visible content. The browser renders everything inside `<body>`. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ‚Äî Document Structure | Element | Type | Required? | Self-Closing? | Where It Lives | |---|---|---|---|---| | `<!DOCTYPE html>` | Declaration (not a tag) | YES | N/A | Before `<html>` | | `<html>` | Root element | YES | NO | Wraps everything | | `<head>` | Sectioning root | YES | NO | Inside `<html>` | | `<title>` | Metadata | YES | NO | Inside `<head>` | | `<meta>` | Void element | NO | YES (Self-Closing) | Inside `<head>` | | `<link>` | Void element | NO | YES | Inside `<head>` | | `<body>` | Sectioning root | YES | NO | Inside `<html>` (after `<head>`) | | `<script>` | Metadata/Content | NO | NO | Anywhere (deferred/async) | | `<style>` | Metadata | NO | NO | Inside `<head>` | > [!NOTE] > **Void Elements** in HTML5 are those that cannot have content or a closing tag. They are written as `<tag>` or `<tag />` (the slash is optional in HTML5 but **mandatory** in XHTML). Complete list of void elements: `area, base, br, col, embed, hr, img, input, link, meta, param, source, track, wbr`. --- ## 2.3 Editing an HTML5 Document ‚Äî Tools Hierarchy ### Level 1: Plain Text Editors - **Examples:** Notepad (Windows), TextEdit (Mac), gedit (Linux). - **Pros:** Lightweight, no learning curve. - **Cons:** No syntax highlighting, no auto-completion, error-prone. ### Level 2: Source Code Editors (Most Popular for KTU Labs) - **Examples:** **Visual Studio Code (VS Code)**, Sublime Text, Atom, Brackets, Notepad++. - **Pros:** Syntax highlighting, IntelliSense, extensions, integrated terminal. - **Cons:** Lightweight but not full IDE. ### Level 3: Full IDEs - **Examples:** WebStorm, Adobe Dreamweaver, Eclipse with Web Tools. - **Pros:** Project management, debugging, FTP deployment. - **Cons:** Heavy, paid licenses. > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Favorite Question:** *"Which editor is recommended for HTML5 development?"* > **Answer:** Visual Studio Code (VS Code) due to its free nature, built-in Emmet abbreviations, live preview, and extensive extension marketplace. ### Level 4: Online Editors - **Examples:** CodePen, JSFiddle, JSBin, Replit, Glitch. - **Use Case:** Quick prototyping, sharing snippets, collaboration. --- ## 2.4 The Editing Workflow ‚Äî 6-Step Process $$\text{1. Create} \rightarrow \text{2. Edit} \rightarrow \text{3. Save (.html)} \rightarrow \text{4. Open in Browser} \rightarrow \text{5. Validate (W3C)} \rightarrow \text{6. Debug/Refine}$$ ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Semantic HTML5** improves **SEO (Search Engine Optimization)** for e-commerce. - **Viewport meta tag** is critical for **mobile-first responsive design** in modern web apps. - **`<canvas>` and `<svg>`** power data visualization dashboards in **IoT and Industry 4.0** monitoring systems. - **Offline storage (localStorage)** enables **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)** used in field-engineering tools where internet connectivity is unreliable. --- ## 2.5 Common HTML5 Editor Extensions (VS Code Specific) | Extension | Purpose | |---|---| | Live Server | Auto-refresh browser on save | | Prettier | Code formatting | | HTML CSS Support | IntelliSense for HTML/CSS | | Auto Rename Tag | Synchronously renames opening/closing tags | | Emmet | Built-in shorthand for HTML5 (e.g., `!` + Tab = full HTML5 skeleton) | --- ## 2.6 Naming Conventions and Best Practices 1. **File extensions:** Use `.html` (preferred) over `.htm` (legacy Windows). 2. **Folder names:** Lowercase, no spaces (use hyphens), e.g., `web-project-2024`. 3. **Homepage file:** Must be named `index.html` ‚Äî browsers automatically look for this. 4. **Tag case:** Always use **lowercase** (HTML5 standard). 5. **Attribute quotes:** Use **double quotes** `"value"` consistently. 6. **Indentation:** 2 or 4 spaces (avoid tabs in shared projects). 7. **Encoding:** Always include `<meta charset="UTF-8">` as the **first** child of `<head>`. <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Build-Up of an HTML5 Document ‚Äî From Zero to Full We will construct an HTML5 document **incrementally**, showing what happens at each stage. Every step includes the code added and an explanation of *why* it is needed. ### STEP 1: Start with an Empty File ```text [Empty file] ``` **Explanation:** Any text editor can serve as an HTML editor at this stage. The file contains nothing. --- ### STEP 2: Add the DOCTYPE Declaration ```html <!DOCTYPE html> ``` **Explanation:** - Tells the browser: *"This is an HTML5 document, render in standards mode."* - Without this, the browser enters **quirks mode**, emulating old browser bugs for backward compatibility. - It must be the **very first line** with no preceding whitespace (though some browsers tolerate whitespace). --- ### STEP 3: Open the Root `<html>` Element ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> ``` **Explanation:** - `lang="en"` declares English as the primary language. - This helps: 1. **Screen readers** pronounce words correctly. 2. **Search engines** classify content language. 3. **Browsers** select appropriate fonts for the script. --- ### STEP 4: Add the `<head>` Section ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> </head> ``` **Explanation:** - `<head>` contains non-visible metadata. - Indentation of 2 spaces improves readability (not required by HTML5). --- ### STEP 5: Add the Mandatory `<meta charset>` Tag ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> </head> ``` **Explanation:** - `UTF-8` encodes all Unicode characters (covers every language, emoji, symbols). - Must appear within the **first 1024 bytes** of the document for safety. - Without this, special characters (e.g., ‚Çπ, √©, ‰∏≠) display as garbled `√¢‚Ç¨` symbols (**mojibake**). --- ### STEP 6: Add the Viewport Meta Tag (for Mobile Responsiveness) ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> </head> ``` **Explanation:** - `width=device-width` makes the page width match the device's screen width. - `initial-scale=1.0` sets the initial zoom level to 100%. - Without this, mobile browsers render the page at **980px width** and shrink it ‚Äî breaking responsive design. --- ### STEP 7: Add the Required `<title>` Element ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> <title>KTU Web Programming - HTML5 Introduction ``` **Explanation:** - The title appears in the browser tab, history, bookmarks, and search results. - Must be human-readable (max ~60 characters for SEO). - Only one `` per document is allowed. --- ### STEP 8: Add Optional `<meta>` Tags for SEO ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> <title>KTU Web Programming - HTML5 Introduction ``` **Explanation:** - `description`: Used by Google as the snippet under the page title. - `keywords`: Largely ignored by modern search engines but historically important. - `author`: Identifies the page creator. --- ### STEP 9: Close the `` and Open `` ```html KTU Web Programming - HTML5 Introduction ``` **Explanation:** - The `` follows `` strictly ‚Äî they are siblings inside ``. - Order matters: `head` first, `body` second. --- ### STEP 10: Add Visible Content to `` ```html KTU Web Programming - HTML5 Introduction

Welcome to HTML5

This is my first HTML5 document structured for KTU Module 1.

Visit KTU Official Website ``` **Explanation:** - `

` is the most important heading (only one per page recommended). - `

` defines a paragraph. - `` is an anchor (hyperlink). - All visible content lives between `` and ``. --- ### STEP 11: Close the Root `` Element The `` close tag must be the **very last line** of every HTML5 document. ```html ``` --- ## 3.2 Final Complete HTML5 Document ‚Äî Minimal Valid Skeleton ```html Minimal HTML5

Hello, KTU!

``` **Verification Checklist (KTU Lab Exam):** | Check | Status | |---|---| | DOCTYPE on first line | ‚úî | | `` attribute present | ‚úî | | `` before `` | ‚úî | | Exactly one `` | ‚úî | | Charset declared within first 1024 bytes | ‚úî | | All opening tags have matching closing tags | ‚úî | | Lowercase tag names | ‚úî | | File saved as `index.html` | ‚úî | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî HTML4 vs HTML5 vs XHTML | Feature | HTML 4.01 | XHTML 1.0 | HTML5 | |---|---|---|---| | DOCTYPE length | Long DTD reference | Long DTD reference | `<!DOCTYPE html>` | | Case sensitivity | Insensitive | **Sensitive** (lowercase only) | Insensitive (lowercase recommended) | | Attribute quoting | Optional | **Mandatory** double quotes | Optional but recommended | | Self-closing void tags | Allowed | **Mandatory** `/` | Optional `/` | | MIME type | `text/html` | `application/xhtml+xml` | `text/html` | | Video/Audio | Plugin-dependent (Flash) | Plugin-dependent | **Native support** | | Semantic tags | None | None | **`<header>`, `<nav>`, etc.** | | Form input types | Limited | Limited | `email, date, number, range` | | Error tolerance | Tolerant | Strict (XML rules) | Tolerant | | Year standardized | 1999 | 2000 | **2014** | --- ## 3.4 Editing Process ‚Äî Detailed Step-by-Step Using VS Code ### Step 1: Install VS Code - Download from `https://code.visualstudio.com/`. - Free, open-source (MIT License), cross-platform. ### Step 2: Install Live Server Extension - Open Extensions panel (`Ctrl+Shift+X`). - Search "Live Server" by Ritwick Dey. - Click Install. ### Step 3: Create Working Directory - Create folder `ktu-html5-pccnt503` on Desktop. ### Step 4: Create New File - In VS Code: `File ‚Üí New File` or `Ctrl+N`. - Type the HTML5 skeleton (or type `!` and press Tab for Emmet auto-generation). ### Step 5: Save with `.html` Extension - `Ctrl+S`, name as `index.html`. ### Step 6: Run Live Server - Right-click in editor ‚Üí "Open with Live Server". - Browser opens at `http://127.0.0.1:5500/`. - Any saved change auto-refreshes the browser. ### Step 7: Validate at W3C - Copy rendered HTML to `https://validator.w3.org/#validate_by_input`. - W3C returns pass/fail with line-by-line error report. ### Step 8: Debug Using Browser DevTools - `F12` or `Ctrl+Shift+I` opens DevTools. - Inspect element, view console errors, test responsive viewports. --- ## 3.5 Mathematical Representation ‚Äî Document Object Model (DOM) Tree The DOM tree for the minimal HTML5 document is: $$ T_{\text{DOM}} = \begin{cases} \text{root: } & \texttt{Document} \\ \text{children: } & [\texttt{DOCTYPE}, \texttt{<html>}] \\ \texttt{<html>} \rightarrow & [\texttt{<head>}, \texttt{<body>}] \\ \texttt{<head>} \rightarrow & [\texttt{<meta>}, \texttt{<title>}] \\ \texttt{<body>} \rightarrow & [\texttt{<h1>}] \\ \texttt{<h1>} \rightarrow & [\text{TextNode: "Hello, KTU!"}] \end{cases} $$ This tree structure enables **JavaScript** to traverse and manipulate every node programmatically. <!-- SECTION_3_END --> <!-- SECTION_4_START --> # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî HTML5 Document Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[DOCTYPE Declaration] --> B[Root html Element lang=en] B --> C[Head Section] B --> D[Body Section] C --> C1[meta charset UTF-8] C --> C2[meta viewport] C --> C3[title Element] C --> C4[meta description] C --> C5[link rel stylesheet] C --> C6[script src app.js] D --> D1[h1 Heading] D --> D2[p Paragraph] D --> D3[a Anchor Link] D --> D4[img Void Element] D --> D5[div Container] D5 --> D5a[header] D5 --> D5b[nav] D5 --> D5c[main] D5 --> D5d[footer] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** - The **yellow** node represents the DOCTYPE declaration. - **Blue** node is the root HTML container. - **Green** cluster contains head metadata (invisible to users). - **Pink** cluster contains body content (visible to users). - **Purple** sub-tree shows semantic HTML5 structural elements. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart ‚Äî HTML5 Editing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Open Text Editor]) --> Step1[Step 1: Write DOCTYPE html] Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Add html root lang attribute] Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Build head section] Step3 --> Step3a[Add meta charset UTF-8] Step3a --> Step3b[Add meta viewport] Step3b --> Step3c[Add title element] Step3c --> Step4[Step 4: Build body section] Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Add semantic content] Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Save as index.html] Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Open in browser] Step7 --> Step8{Did it render correctly?} Step8 -->|No| Step9[Press F12 Inspect Console] Step9 --> Step10[Fix errors] Step10 --> Step7 Step8 -->|Yes| Step11[Validate at validator.w3.org] Step11 --> Step12{Validation Passed?} Step12 -->|No| Step13[Review W3C error report] Step13 --> Step10 Step12 -->|Yes| Finish([Done: HTML5 Document Ready]) style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Finish fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Step8 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Step12 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation of the flowchart:** - **Green** start and **Pink** end nodes mark workflow boundaries. - **Yellow** decision diamonds represent validation gates. - **Blue** rectangles are sequential editing steps. - The loop back from "Fix errors" to "Open in browser" represents the iterative debugging cycle. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ‚Äî Browser Parsing of an HTML5 Document ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant FS as File System participant B as Browser participant P as HTML5 Parser participant DOM as DOM Tree participant R as Render Engine U->>FS: 1. Request index.html FS-->>B: 2. Send HTML source B->>P: 3. Parse DOCTYPE P-->>B: 4. Trigger standards mode B->>P: 5. Parse html root element P->>DOM: 6. Create document node B->>P: 7. Parse head children P->>DOM: 8. Add meta title link nodes B->>P: 9. Parse body children P->>DOM: 10. Add h1 p a img nodes DOM-->>R: 11. Send complete tree R->>U: 12. Render pixels on screen Note over B,R: Standards mode ensures CSS3 and JS behave per W3C spec ``` **Interpretation:** - The browser performs a **tokenization** phase (lexing the HTML into tokens). - Then **tree construction** (building the DOM). - Finally, **layout and paint** (rendering pixels). --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture Diagram ‚Äî Editing Tool Stack ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Source[Authoring Layer] A1[VS Code] --> A2[Live Server] A1 --> A3[Emmet Abbreviations] A1 --> A4[Prettier Formatter] end subgraph File[File System Layer] B1[index.html] B2[style.css] B3[script.js] end subgraph Render[Rendering Layer] C1[Chrome Engine Blink] C2[Firefox Engine Gecko] C3[Safari Engine WebKit] end subgraph Validate[Validation Layer] D1[W3C Markup Validator] D2[Browser DevTools F12] D3[Lighthouse Audit] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> C3 C1 --> D1 C1 --> D2 C1 --> D3 style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** - The **blue** layer is where developers write code. - The **yellow** layer is file storage. - The **pink** layer is where HTML gets converted to pixels (each browser uses a different rendering engine). - The **green** layer ensures quality through validation. <!-- SECTION_4_END --> <!-- SECTION_5_START --> # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## üìù PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define HTML5. List any four salient features of HTML5. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **HTML5 Definition (1 Mark):** HTML5 is the fifth and latest major version of the HyperText Markup Language, standardized by the W3C as a Recommendation on 28 October 2014.
