It is a markup language used for structuring and presenting content on the World Wide Web, with native support for multimedia, semantic elements, and advanced APIs without requiring third-party plugins. **Four Salient Features (2 Marks ‚Äî 0.5 each):** 1. **Semantic Elements:** Provides meaningful tags such as `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, and `<footer>` that describe the purpose of content. 2. **Native Multimedia Support:** Built-in `<audio>` and `<video>` elements eliminate the need for external plugins like Adobe Flash or QuickTime. 3. **Offline Storage:** Provides `localStorage` (persistent) and `sessionStorage` (per-tab) APIs, allowing web applications to function without an active internet connection. 4. **Canvas and SVG Graphics:** The `<canvas>` element enables dynamic 2D/3D bitmap rendering via JavaScript, while `<svg>` supports scalable vector graphics ‚Äî both useful for data visualization and animations. --- ### Question 2: Explain the role of the `<head>` section in an HTML5 document with examples. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The `<head>` section (1 Mark) of an HTML5 document acts as a **metadata container** that holds machine-readable information about the document. It does not render any visible content on the browser but provides essential data to browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies. **Examples of `<head>` children (2 Marks):** 1. **`<title>`** ‚Äî Defines the document title displayed on the browser tab. ```html <title>KTU Web Programming ``` 2. **``** ‚Äî Declares the character encoding to ensure universal character display. 3. **``** ‚Äî Controls the layout on mobile browsers for responsive design. 4. **``** ‚Äî Links an external CSS file for styling. The `` must precede `` and may contain ``, `

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HTML Graphics** refers to the native capabilities of HTML5 (and its companion technologies) that allow web developers to create, manipulate, and render visual content directly within a web browser without relying on external plugins (like Flash or Silverlight). Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, HTML Graphics encompasses two primary, browser-native rendering technologies:

1. **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** ‚Äî A declarative, XML-based, vector graphics language defined by the W3C.
2. **Canvas** ‚Äî An HTML5 element that provides a procedural, bitmap-based drawing surface controlled via the JavaScript Canvas API (2D Context).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must understand that HTML Graphics is *not* a single API. It is a **dual-paradigm ecosystem**: SVG (declarative / DOM-resident) versus Canvas (procedural / pixel-buffer). Exam questions frequently test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **whiteboard in a conference room**:

- **SVG** behaves like a whiteboard where every shape you draw (a circle, a line, a polygon) is a **physical, labeled sticky note** stuck to the board. You can later grab a sticky note, move it, resize it, or read its label (its properties). The board is **document-oriented**.
- **Canvas** behaves like a **chalkboard**. Once chalk is on the board, the individual strokes blend into one continuous picture. To change anything, you must **erase and redraw** the entire affected region. The board is **pixel-oriented**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:**
> - **SVG** = *What you see is what is in the DOM.* (Reactive, accessible, scalable.)
> - **Canvas** = *What you see is what was last painted.* (Fast, imperative, pixel-based.)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reserved Vocabulary (Bolded)

- **Resolution Independence:** SVG drawings remain crisp at any zoom level because they are described by mathematical equations, not a fixed grid of pixels.
- **Pixel Buffer:** Canvas maintains an internal raster (bitmap) buffer whose dimensions are measured in **CSS pixels** by default.
- **DOM (Document Object Model):** SVG elements are first-class citizens of the DOM tree, making them queryable via `document.getElementById`, `querySelector`, and CSS selectors.
- **Context Object:** Canvas requires a **rendering context** (commonly `2d`) obtained via `canvas.getContext('2d')` to access drawing methods.
- **W3C Standard:** SVG is a W3C recommendation; HTML5 Canvas is a WHATWG HTML Living Standard.

---

## 1.4 Learning Outcomes (Mapped to KTU COs)

Upon mastering this topic, the learner will be able to:
- **CO1:** Differentiate between vector and raster graphics paradigms in a web context.
- **CO2:** Construct SVG drawings using primitive shapes and attributes.
- **CO3:** Implement Canvas-based drawings using the 2D rendering context.
- **CO4:** Evaluate the suitability of SVG vs. Canvas for a given application scenario.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Two Paradigms

### A. SVG ‚Äî Scalable Vector Graphics

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Declaration:** SVG is declared inline within HTML using the `<svg>` root element or referenced as an external file (`.svg`).
2. **XML Structure:** The document is parsed as XML; every shape is a child node (e.g., `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<path>`, `<text>`, `<line>`, `<polyline>`, `<polygon>`).
3. **Attribute-Driven Rendering:** Visual properties (position, size, fill, stroke) are passed as XML attributes, e.g., `fill="red"`, `stroke="black"`, `stroke-width="2"`.
4. **DOM Integration:** Because SVG is part of the DOM, CSS can style it, JavaScript can attach event listeners, and screen readers can read it (accessibility).
5. **Resolution Independence:** Coordinates are in the SVG **user coordinate system**, mapped to the viewport at render time.

**Key SVG Primitives:**

- `<rect>` ‚Äî Rectangle (x, y, width, height, rx, ry)
- `<circle>` ‚Äî Circle (cx, cy, r)
- `<ellipse>` ‚Äî Ellipse (cx, cy, rx, ry)
- `<line>` ‚Äî Line (x1, y1, x2, y2)
- `<polyline>` ‚Äî Connected line segments
- `<polygon>` ‚Äî Closed shape
- `<path>` ‚Äî Arbitrary shape using `d` attribute commands (M, L, C, Q, A, Z)
- `<text>` ‚Äî Vector text
- `<g>` ‚Äî Group element for transforms/styles

### B. Canvas ‚Äî HTML5 Procedural Drawing

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Element Placement:** The `<canvas>` element is placed in the DOM with `width` and `height` attributes defining the **drawing buffer size**.
2. **Context Acquisition:** JavaScript obtains a 2D rendering context: `const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');`
3. **State Stack:** The context maintains a **drawing state** (fillStyle, strokeStyle, lineWidth, transformations). State can be saved/restored using `save()` and `restore()`.
4. **Path-Based Drawing:** Shapes are constructed via a path API (`beginPath`, `moveTo`, `lineTo`, `arc`, `closePath`) and then committed with `fill()` or `stroke()`.
5. **Pixel Commitment:** Once rendered, the canvas becomes a static raster; re-rendering requires explicit code invocation.

**Key Canvas 2D Methods (API Cheat Sheet):**

- **Rectangles:** `fillRect()`, `strokeRect()`, `clearRect()`
- **Paths:** `beginPath()`, `moveTo()`, `lineTo()`, `arc()`, `arcTo()`, `quadraticCurveTo()`, `bezierCurveTo()`, `closePath()`
- **Committing:** `fill()`, `stroke()`, `clip()`
- **Text:** `fillText()`, `strokeText()`, `measureText()`
- **Transforms:** `translate()`, `rotate()`, `scale()`, `setTransform()`, `transform()`
- **State:** `save()`, `restore()`
- **Images:** `drawImage()`

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| **Aspect** | **SVG** | **Canvas** |
|---|---|---|
| **Rendering Model** | Declarative (XML) | Procedural (JavaScript) |
| **Output Type** | Vector (resolution-independent) | Raster (pixel-buffer) |
| **DOM Presence** | Yes ‚Äî each shape is a node | No ‚Äî only the `<canvas>` element exists in DOM |
| **CSS Styling** | Yes (via attributes or CSS) | No (only via the 2D context API) |
| **Event Handling** | Native per-element event listeners | Manual hit-testing via coordinates |
| **Performance (Few Large Objects)** | Slower (DOM overhead) | Faster |
| **Performance (Many Small Objects)** | Better with `<g>` grouping | Slower without manual optimizations |
| **Accessibility** | Excellent (text, ARIA) | Poor (raster is opaque to AT) |
| **Save / Export** | `.svg` file (text/XML) | `canvas.toDataURL()` or `.toBlob()` |
| **Animation** | CSS, SMIL, JS, Web Animations API | `requestAnimationFrame` loop |
| **Use Case Examples** | Logos, icons, charts, maps, UI | Games, image processing, particle systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SVG is dominant in **data visualization** (D3.js output), **responsive UI design**, and **print-quality web assets**. Canvas dominates in **real-time game engines**, **video processing**, and **physically-based simulations** rendered per frame.

## 2.3 The SVG Coordinate System

SVG uses a left-handed coordinate system where:
- The origin `(0,0)` is at the **top-left** of the viewport.
- The **x-axis** increases to the right.
- The **y-axis** increases **downward** (this is different from Cartesian math).
- The viewport is defined by the `viewBox` attribute: `viewBox="min-x min-y width height"`.

## 2.4 The Canvas Coordinate System

Canvas follows the same convention as SVG:
- Origin at **top-left `(0,0)`**.
- **x** ‚Üí right, **y** ‚Üí down.
- Coordinates map directly to pixel positions in the drawing buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU exam trap: students forget that the **y-axis points downward** in both SVG and Canvas (unlike Cartesian math). Questions involving rotation or arc commands frequently test this.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 SVG Implementation ‚Äî Drawing a Face

Below is a step-by-step construction of a simple smiley face using SVG primitives. Each step introduces a new concept.

```html
<svg width="200" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <!-- Step 1: Yellow face background (circle) -->
  <circle cx="100" cy="100" r="90" fill="gold" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"/>

  <!-- Step 2: Left eye (circle) -->
  <circle cx="70" cy="80" r="10" fill="black"/>

  <!-- Step 3: Right eye (circle) -->
  <circle cx="130" cy="80" r="10" fill="black"/>

  <!-- Step 4: Smile (path with arc command) -->
  <path d="M 60 130 Q 100 160 140 130" 
        stroke="black" stroke-width="4" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round"/>
</svg>
```

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. The `<svg>` root establishes a 200√ó200 user-space viewport.
2. The first `<circle>` creates a 90-pixel-radius yellow disc centered at (100,100).
3. Two smaller black circles form the eyes at symmetric coordinates.
4. The `<path>` uses a **quadratic B√©zier curve** (`Q` command): start at (60,130), control point at (100,160), end at (140,130). This produces a downward-curving smile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Mini-Cheat Sheet:**
> - `M x y` ‚Äî Move to (start a new subpath).
> - `L x y` ‚Äî Line to.
> - `H x` ‚Äî Horizontal line to.
> - `V y` ‚Äî Vertical line to.
> - `Q cx cy x y` ‚Äî Quadratic B√©zier.
> - `C c1x c1y c2x c2y x y` ‚Äî Cubic B√©zier.
> - `A rx ry x-axis-rotation large-arc-flag sweep-flag x y` ‚Äî Arc.
> - `Z` ‚Äî Close path.

## 3.2 Canvas Implementation ‚Äî Same Smiley Face

The equivalent Canvas implementation requires JavaScript to imperatively construct the same scene.

```html
<canvas id="myCanvas" width="200" height="200" 
        style="border:1px solid black;"></canvas>
<script>
  const canvas = document.getElementById('myCanvas');
  const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');

  // Step 1: Draw the yellow face
  ctx.beginPath();
  ctx.arc(100, 100, 90, 0, Math.PI * 2);
  ctx.fillStyle = 'gold';
  ctx.fill();
  ctx.strokeStyle = 'black';
  ctx.lineWidth = 3;
  ctx.stroke();

  // Step 2 & 3: Draw the eyes
  ctx.fillStyle = 'black';
  ctx.beginPath();
  ctx.arc(70, 80, 10, 0, Math.PI * 2);
  ctx.fill();

  ctx.beginPath();
  ctx.arc(130, 80, 10, 0, Math.PI * 2);
  ctx.fill();

  // Step 4: Draw the smile using a quadratic curve
  ctx.beginPath();
  ctx.moveTo(60, 130);
  ctx.quadraticCurveTo(100, 160, 140, 130);
  ctx.lineWidth = 4;
  ctx.lineCap = 'round';
  ctx.stroke();
</script>
```

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. Acquire the 2D context ‚Äî this is the *only* object exposing drawing methods.
2. `arc(x, y, radius, startAngle, endAngle)` uses **radians**; the full circle spans `0` to `2œÄ` (i.e., `Math.PI * 2`).
3. `fill()` commits the current path as a filled region; `stroke()` outlines it.
4. The `quadraticCurveTo(cpx, cpy, x, y)` method mirrors the SVG `Q` command exactly.
5. Note: there is **no DOM node** for the smile ‚Äî once the script ends, the pixels are baked into the buffer.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SVG vs Canvas Decision Logic

The following table is a high-yield tool for KTU 14-mark questions that ask you to *justify* a technology choice for a given scenario.

| **Scenario** | **Recommended** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| A logo that must scale from a 16√ó16 favicon to a 4K billboard | **SVG** | Vector-based resolution independence. |
| A real-time multiplayer game with 1000+ particles | **Canvas** | Per-frame redraw performance; SVG DOM would bottleneck. |
| An interactive bar chart with hover tooltips | **SVG** | Native event listeners per bar; CSS transitions. |
| A photo filter app (Instagram-style) | **Canvas** | Direct pixel access via `getImageData` / `putImageData`. |
| A complex map with thousands of pannable markers | **SVG or Canvas** | Trade-off ‚Äî SVG if accessibility matters; Canvas if >5k markers. |
| A printed report header requiring crisp text | **SVG** | Vector text remains sharp at any zoom / DPI. |
| A heatmap generated from a million data points | **Canvas** | Direct pixel manipulation (`ImageData` buffer). |

## 3.4 The Canvas State Stack ‚Äî Formal Behavior

Canvas maintains a **state stack**. Each call to `save()` pushes a snapshot of the current drawing state onto the stack; each call to `restore()` pops it. The preserved properties are:

$$
\text{State} = \begin{cases}
\text{Transformation matrix (translate, rotate, scale)} \\
\text{Clipping region} \\
\text{Stroke and fill styles} \\
\text{Line width, line cap, line join, miter limit} \\
\text{Font, textAlign, textBaseline} \\
\text{Global alpha, globalCompositeOperation} \\
\text{Shadow offsets, blur, color}
\end{cases}
$$

This is essential for **isolated sub-drawings** ‚Äî you can transform, draw, then restore the original state without polluting subsequent code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most common student error:** Forgetting that `restore()` pops the *last* `save()`. Mismatched pairs cause subtle rendering bugs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVG Architecture ‚Äî DOM-Tree Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HTML Document] --> B[svg root element]
    B --> C[g group element]
    C --> D[rect primitive]
    C --> E[circle primitive]
    C --> F[path primitive]
    C --> G[text element]
    D --> H[style: fill stroke]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    B --> I[defs reusable defs]
    I --> J[linearGradient]
    I --> K[pattern]
    I --> L[clipPath]
    B --> M[viewBox viewport mapping]
    M --> N[user coordinate system]
    N --> O[browser pixel render]
```

## 4.2 Canvas Architecture ‚Äî Procedural Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[HTML canvas element] --> Q[getContext 2d call]
    Q --> R[2D Rendering Context]
    R --> S[Drawing State Stack]
    R --> T[Path Construction API]
    R --> U[Transformation API]
    R --> V[Pixel Buffer]
    T --> W[beginPath moveTo lineTo arc]
    W --> X[fill or stroke commit]
    X --> V
    U --> Y[translate rotate scale]
    Y --> V
    S --> Z[save restore]
    Z --> R
    V --> AA[Browser Compositor]
    AA --> AB[Screen Pixels]
```

## 4.3 SVG vs Canvas ‚Äî Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need to draw graphics on web] --> Q1{Need per-element events?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SVGPath[Use SVG]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{More than 1000 dynamic objects?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CanvasPath[Use Canvas]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need pixel-level manipulation?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> CanvasPath
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need crisp scaling and print?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> SVGPath
    Q4 -- No --> CanvasPath
    SVGPath --> SVGResult[Vector DOM-based solution]
    CanvasPath --> CanvasResult[Raster buffer solution]
```

## 4.4 Canvas Drawing Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    StepA[Acquire canvas element] --> StepB[Get 2d context]
    StepB --> StepC[Configure state styles]
    StepC --> StepD[Begin new path]
    StepD --> StepE[Add path commands]
    StepE --> StepF[Fill or stroke]
    StepF --> StepG{More shapes?}
    StepG -- Yes --> StepD
    StepG -- No --> StepH[Render complete]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between SVG and HTML5 Canvas. List any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Rendering Model:** SVG is a *declarative* XML-based vector format; Canvas is a *procedural* bitmap API controlled by JavaScript.
2. **DOM Presence:** SVG elements are part of the DOM and can be styled with CSS and respond to event listeners individually; Canvas renders to a single pixel buffer with no per-shape DOM nodes.
3. **Scalability:** SVG is resolution-independent and remains crisp at any zoom; Canvas becomes pixelated when scaled beyond its buffer size.
4. **Use Case:** SVG is preferred for static/semi-static illustrations, icons, and charts; Canvas is preferred for real-time animations, games, and pixel-level image manipulation.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the role of `getContext('2d')` in HTML5 Canvas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `getContext('2d')` method is called on a `<canvas>` element to obtain a **2D rendering context object**, which is the only interface that exposes Canvas's drawing methods (e.g., `fillRect`, `arc`, `fillText`, `drawImage`). Without this context, the canvas element is merely an empty bitmap container. Internally, the context holds the **drawing state** (fill style, stroke style, transformations, clipping region) and the **current path**. Calling `getContext('2d')` on the same canvas always returns the *same* context instance, preserving continuity of state.

---

## Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **HTML5 SVG**. List at least **five SVG primitive shapes** with their key attributes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete HTML program that uses SVG to draw a **rectangle, a circle, a line, and a text label** inside a 400√ó400 viewport. Each shape should be visually distinct (different fill/stroke). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture & Primitives (7 marks):**

SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) is a W3C-standard, XML-based language for describing 2D vector graphics. Its architecture consists of:

- **`<svg>` root element:** defines the viewport (`width`, `height`) and the user coordinate system via `viewBox`.
- **Shape primitives:** child elements that describe geometric objects.
- **Styling layer:** presentation attributes (fill, stroke) and/or external CSS.
- **Grouping & transformation:** `<g>` element supports `transform="translate(...) rotate(...) scale(...)"`.
- **Reusable definitions:** `<defs>` block holds reusable elements such as gradients, patterns, and clip paths.
- **DOM integration:** every SVG element is a live DOM node.

**Five SVG primitive shapes:**

| **Element** | **Key Attributes** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|
| `<rect>` | x, y, width, height, rx, ry | Axis-aligned rectangle (optionally rounded). |
| `<circle>` | cx, cy, r | Circle of radius r. |
| `<ellipse>` | cx, cy, rx, ry | Ellipse with two radii. |
| `<line>` | x1, y1, x2, y2 | Straight line segment. |
| `<polygon>` | points (e.g., "10,10 50,5 20,30") | Closed multi-vertex shape. |
| `<polyline>` | points | Open multi-vertex path. |
| `<path>` | d (path-data string) | Arbitrary shape via M/L/C/Q/A/Z commands. |
| `<text>` | x, y, font-size, fill | Vector text. |

**(b) Program (7 marks):**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>SVG Demo</title>
</head>
<body>
  <svg width="400" height="400" 
       xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" 
       style="border:1px solid #333">

    <!-- Rectangle: red with black dashed border -->
    <rect x="40" y="40" width="120" height="80"
          fill="#e74c3c" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"
          stroke-dasharray="6 3"/>

    <!-- Circle: blue with no stroke -->
    <circle cx="300" cy="100" r="50"
            fill="#3498db"/>

    <!-- Line: thick green diagonal -->
    <line x1="40" y1="300" x2="360" y2="200"
          stroke="#27ae60" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>

    <!-- Text label -->
    <text x="100" y="370" font-family="Arial" 
          font-size="24" fill="#2c3e50">
      HTML Graphics with SVG
    </text>
  </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation of each shape:**

- The `<rect>` has `x="40", y="40"` placing its top-left at (40,40). The `stroke-dasharray="6 3"` creates a 6-pixel dash followed by a 3-pixel gap.
- The `<circle>` is centered at (300,100) with radius 50, so it spans from (250,50) to (350,150).
- The `<line>` runs from (40,300) to (360,200); `stroke-linecap="round"` gives it rounded ends.
- The `<text>` element renders at baseline y=370 with a 24-pixel font.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Canvas 2D Context API**. Describe the role of the **drawing state stack** and the `save()`/`restore()` methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete HTML program that uses JavaScript and Canvas to draw **a green rectangle, a red circle, and a blue diagonal line** on a 300√ó300 canvas. The rectangle must have a 4-pixel black border, and the circle must be centered at (150,150) with radius 60. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Canvas 2D Context API & State Stack (7 marks):**

The **Canvas 2D Context** (`CanvasRenderingContext2D`) is the procedural drawing interface returned by `canvas.getContext('2d')`. It provides methods organized into functional groups:

- **Rectangle methods:** `fillRect`, `strokeRect`, `clearRect`.
- **Path methods:** `beginPath`, `moveTo`, `lineTo`, `arc`, `arcTo`, `quadraticCurveTo`, `bezierCurveTo`, `closePath`, `rect`, `ellipse`.
- **Path commit:** `fill`, `stroke`, `clip`, `isPointInPath`.
- **Text:** `fillText`, `strokeText`, `measureText`.
- **Images & pixels:** `drawImage`, `getImageData`, `putImageData`, `createImageData`.
- **Transformations:** `translate`, `rotate`, `scale`, `setTransform`, `transform`, `resetTransform`.
- **State management:** `save`, `restore`.
- **Compositing & styling:** `globalAlpha`, `globalCompositeOperation`, `fillStyle`, `strokeStyle`, `shadow*` properties.

**Drawing State Stack:**

The context maintains a **stack of saved states**. When `save()` is called, the *current* state of all style and transformation properties is **pushed** onto the stack. When `restore()` is called, the **most recently saved** state is **popped** and reapplied. The saved state includes:

- The current **transformation matrix**.
- The **clipping region**.
- The current values of `strokeStyle`, `fillStyle`, `globalAlpha`, `lineWidth`, `lineCap`, `lineJoin`, `miterLimit`, `lineDashOffset`, `shadowOffsetX`, `shadowOffsetY`, `shadowBlur`, `shadowColor`, `globalCompositeOperation`, `font`, `textAlign`, `textBaseline`, `imageSmoothingEnabled`.

**Why it matters:** Without `save`/`restore`, transformations leak into subsequent drawing code. The standard idiom is:

```javascript
ctx.save();
ctx.translate(100, 100);
ctx.rotate(Math.PI / 4);
// draw something transformed
ctx.restore();   // back to original transform
```

**(b) Program (7 marks):**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Canvas Demo</title>
</head>
<body>
  <canvas id="c" width="300" height="300"
          style="border:1px solid #333"></canvas>

  <script>
    const canvas = document.getElementById('c');
    const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');

    // Green rectangle with 4-pixel black border
    ctx.fillStyle = 'green';
    ctx.fillRect(30, 30, 100, 60);

    ctx.strokeStyle = 'black';
    ctx.lineWidth = 4;
    ctx.strokeRect(30, 30, 100, 60);

    // Red circle centered at (150,150), radius 60
    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.arc(150, 150, 60, 0, Math.PI * 2);
    ctx.fillStyle = 'red';
    ctx.fill();
    ctx.strokeStyle = 'darkred';
    ctx.lineWidth = 2;
    ctx.stroke();

    // Blue diagonal line
    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(20, 280);
    ctx.lineTo(280, 20);
    ctx.strokeStyle = 'blue';
    ctx.lineWidth = 3;
    ctx.stroke();
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation:**

- `fillRect(30, 30, 100, 60)` paints a 100√ó60 green rectangle with its top-left at (30,30).
- `strokeRect` with `lineWidth=4` draws the 4-pixel black border **inside** the rectangle's bounds (Canvas strokes are centered on the edge by default; setting `lineWidth=4` means 2px on each side).
- `arc(150, 150, 60, 0, 2œÄ)` draws a full circle using **radians** (0 to `2œÄ`).
- The diagonal line uses `moveTo` to set the starting pixel and `lineTo` to extend to the ending pixel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Radian vs Degree trap:** Canvas `arc()` and `arcTo()` use **radians**, not degrees. A circle is `2 * Math.PI`, not `360`. Students writing `arc(150, 150, 60, 0, 360)` will silently produce **no arc** (since 360 rad ‚âà 57 full circles, the line may self-intersect and look like a full circle, but subsequent `fill()` calls may fail). Always write `Math.PI * 2`.
> 2. **Path bleeding:** Failing to call `beginPath()` between distinct shapes will cause every subsequent path to be appended to the previous one, producing merged geometry and styling bugs. This is the **#1 cause** of lost marks in Canvas coding questions.
> 3. **SVG namespace:** Inline SVG *inside* an HTML5 document still requires `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"` on the root `<svg>` element. Omitting it causes the browser to fall back to HTML parsing, and shapes may not render.
> 4. **Coordinate direction:** Y-axis grows **downward** in both SVG and Canvas. A "north" arrow pointing to (0,-50) in math terms is written as `(x, y-50)` in screen terms (i.e., *subtract* from y).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ **SVG** = declarative, XML, vector, DOM-resident, CSS-styleable, accessible, scalable.
- ‚úÖ **Canvas** = procedural, JS, raster buffer, fast for many dynamic objects, pixel-manipulable.
- ‚úÖ **`<svg>` root attributes:** `width`, `height`, `viewBox`, `xmlns`.
- ‚úÖ **Five core SVG primitives:** `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<ellipse>`, `<line>`, `<polygon>` (plus `<path>`, `<text>`, `<polyline>`, `<g>`).
- ‚úÖ **Path commands:** `M` (move), `L` (line), `H/V` (horizontal/vertical), `Q` (quadratic B√©zier), `C` (cubic B√©zier), `A` (arc), `Z` (close).
- ‚úÖ **Canvas mandatory pattern:** acquire element ‚Üí `getContext('2d')` ‚Üí `beginPath()` ‚Üí path commands ‚Üí `fill()`/`stroke()`.
- ‚úÖ **Always call `beginPath()`** before constructing a new shape; otherwise paths bleed.
- ‚úÖ **Angles in Canvas are in radians:** full circle = `Math.PI * 2`.
- ‚úÖ **State stack:** `ctx.save()` snapshots, `ctx.restore()` pops; preserves transform, styles, clip.
- ‚úÖ **Coordinate origin:** top-left of the viewport, **y-axis grows downward**.
- ‚úÖ **SVG event handling:** `element.addEventListener('click', handler)` works on individual SVG shapes.
- ‚úÖ **Canvas event handling:** requires manual hit-testing ‚Äî get `event.offsetX/offsetY` and test against shapes yourself.
- ‚úÖ **Exporting:** SVG saves as `.svg` text files; Canvas exports via `canvas.toDataURL('image/png')` or `canvas.toBlob()`.
- ‚úÖ **Common use-case pairing:** SVG ‚Üí logos, charts, icons, maps; Canvas ‚Üí games, image filters, real-time data viz, simulations.
- ‚úÖ **Accessibility edge:** SVG `<text>` is indexable by search engines and screen readers; Canvas text is rendered as pixels and is **invisible** to assistive technologies (mitigate with parallel DOM text).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HTML APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** HTML APIs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **HTML5 APIs (Application Programming Interfaces)** are a set of standardized browser-native interfaces, objects, and methods exposed by modern web browsers that allow client-side JavaScript to interact with the underlying host system, manipulate the Document Object Model (DOM), store data locally, handle multimedia, perform asynchronous communication, and access device hardware ‚Äî **without requiring third-party plugins, browser extensions, or external libraries**. The W3C and WHATWG specifications define these APIs as part of the **HTML Living Standard**. Each API provides a programmable surface (set of properties, methods, and events) that bridges JavaScript with a specific browser capability. Common categories include: | Category | Example APIs | |---|---| | **Data Storage** | Web Storage API, IndexedDB | | **Communication** | Fetch API, WebSockets, Server-Sent Events | | **Device Access** | Geolocation, Camera/MediaDevices, Battery | | **User Interaction** | Drag and Drop, Fullscreen, Clipboard | | **Graphics/Media** | Canvas, WebGL, Audio/Video | | **Performance** | Web Workers, Service Workers | | **File Handling** | File API, FileReader, Blob, FileList | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Swiss Army Knife" of the Browser > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a modern web browser like a **smartphone**. A decade ago, to make a phone call, send an SMS, or use GPS, you needed specialized hardware-software add-ons. Today, the phone comes with built-in "apps" (the camera app, the maps app, the messaging app) ‚Äî you simply call them. **HTML5 APIs are exactly that for the browser**: built-in, ready-to-use "capabilities" that any web page can summon with a few lines of JavaScript, instead of relying on Flash, Java applets, or bulky external libraries. - **API ‚âà A restaurant menu**: Your JavaScript code is the *customer*. The browser is the *kitchen*. The API (menu) tells you what you can order (methods), what info you must provide (parameters/options), and what you'll get back (return values/promises). - **W3C Standardization**: All major browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) implement these APIs according to shared specifications, ensuring your code works everywhere. ## 1.3 Why HTML APIs Matter ‚Äî Syllabus Highlights > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:** Module 1 of PCCNT503 (Web Programming) covers client-side fundamentals. HTML5 APIs are explicitly listed in the KTU syllabus under *HTML5 advanced features*. Mastering them is essential for: (1) building progressive web apps, (2) implementing offline-capable pages, (3) creating responsive, interactive UIs, and (4) performing client-side validation and persistence. **Core principle to remember:** *An HTML API exposes browser power to your JavaScript through well-defined methods and events.* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Most-Asked HTML APIs in KTU Exams Based on KTU 2024 Scheme examination patterns and the prescribed syllabus, the following three APIs are **high-yield** (frequently tested): ### 2.1.1 Geolocation API Provides the geographic location (latitude, longitude, altitude, heading) of the user's device. It requires **explicit user permission** and is served over **HTTPS** in modern browsers. **Core Methods:** - `navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(success_cb, error_cb, options)` ‚Äî One-time location fetch. - `navigator.geolocation.watchPosition(success_cb, error_cb, options)` ‚Äî Continuous tracking; returns a watch ID. - `navigator.geolocation.clearWatch(watchId)` ‚Äî Stops tracking. **Returned Object (Position):** - `coords.latitude` (decimal degrees, WGS84 datum) - `coords.longitude` - `coords.accuracy` (meters) - `coords.altitude`, `coords.altitudeAccuracy` - `coords.heading` (degrees, `NaN` if device is stationary) - `coords.speed` (meters/second) - `timestamp` (DOMTimeStamp in milliseconds) ### 2.1.2 Web Storage API Provides key-value persistent storage in the browser. It is **synchronous** and offers two scopes: | Property | Scope | Lifetime | Capacity (typical) | |---|---|---|---| | `localStorage` | Per origin (protocol + domain + port) | Permanent until explicitly cleared | **~5‚Äì10 MB** | | `sessionStorage` | Per origin + per tab/window | Until tab/window closes | **~5 MB** | **Core Methods (both objects share the same interface):** - `setItem(key, value)` ‚Äî Stores a string (values are auto-converted to strings). - `getItem(key)` ‚Äî Returns the value or `null`. - `removeItem(key)` - `clear()` ‚Äî Removes **all** keys. - `key(index)` ‚Äî Returns the key at the given index. - `length` ‚Äî Number of stored items. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Storage Event:** The `storage` event fires on **other** tabs/windows of the same origin when `localStorage` is modified, enabling cross-tab synchronization. It does **not** fire in the tab that made the change. ### 2.1.3 Drag and Drop API Allows any DOM element to be made *draggable* and to act as a *drop target* using native browser events. **Required Steps:** 1. Set the `draggable="true"` attribute on the source element. 2. Handle the `dragstart` event on the source (set `dataTransfer` payload). 3. Handle `dragover` on the target (call `e.preventDefault()` to allow drop). 4. Handle `drop` on the target (read the payload). 5. Optional: `dragenter`, `dragleave`, `dragend`, `drop`. **Key Object: `DataTransfer`** - `setData(format, data)` ‚Äî e.g., `setData("text/plain", "hello")`. - `getData(format)` ‚Äî Retrieves the payload. - `effectAllowed`, `dropEffect` ‚Äî Control visual cursor feedback (`copy`, `move`, `link`, `none`). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Although HTML APIs are declarative/imperative JavaScript interfaces (not physics formulas), the table below summarizes the **canonical method signatures and key properties** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce verbatim. | API | Method / Property | Purpose | Return Type / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Geolocation | `navigator.geolocation` | Root object (read-only) | Object exposing position methods | | Geolocation | `getCurrentPosition(success, error, options)` | Fetch current position | Calls `success(Position)` async | | Geolocation | `coords.latitude` | Angular distance N/S of equator | Decimal degrees (`-90` to `+90`) | | Geolocation | `coords.longitude` | Angular distance E/W of prime meridian | Decimal degrees (`-180` to `+180`) | | Geolocation | `coords.accuracy` | 68% confidence radius | Meters | | Web Storage | `localStorage.setItem(k, v)` | Persist a string pair | `undefined` | | Web Storage | `localStorage.getItem(k)` | Retrieve by key | `string \vert null` | | Web Storage | `localStorage.length` | Number of entries | Non-negative integer | | Web Storage | `sessionStorage` | Per-tab storage | Same API as `localStorage` | | Drag & Drop | `e.dataTransfer.setData(fmt, data)` | Attach payload to drag | Format string (e.g., `"text/plain"`) | | Drag & Drop | `e.dataTransfer.getData(fmt)` | Read payload on drop | String (empty if not set) | | Drag & Drop | `e.preventDefault()` (in `dragover`) | **Required** to allow drop | Without this, drop is rejected | | File API | `input.files` | Selected files from `` | `FileList` (array-like) | | File API | `file.name, file.size, file.type` | File metadata | String, number (bytes), MIME string | | History | `history.pushState(state, title, url)` | Add history entry without reload | Manipulates address bar | | Workers | `new Worker("script.js")` | Spawn background thread | `Worker` instance | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Geolocation API** ‚Üí Used in ride-sharing apps (Uber, Ola), asset tracking, geo-tagging in social media, location-based marketing. - **Web Storage API** ‚Üí Saves user preferences, JWT tokens, shopping cart state, form drafts ‚Äî replacing deprecated cookies for non-sensitive data. - **Drag & Drop API** ‚Üí Powers Kanban boards (Trello), file upload UIs, sortable lists, image re-ordering in CMS dashboards. - **File API** ‚Üí Underpins modern image uploaders, PDF previewers, CSV importers in web applications. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners often award partial credit (1‚Äì2 marks) if you correctly mention **where** the API is used in real systems. Always tie theory to a real-world scenario. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > In this section, "derivations" refer to the **step-by-step construction of working code**, the **logical flow of API calls**, and the **analytical breakdown of each event/handler** ‚Äî exactly what the KTU board expects when a question says "Write a program to demonstrate..." or "Explain with a suitable example...". --- ## 3.1 Derivation #1 ‚Äî Geolocation API: From Click to Coordinates **Problem statement (typical KTU Part B sub-question):** *Write an HTML page that, on clicking a button, displays the user's current latitude and longitude using the Geolocation API.* ### Step-by-step analytical breakdown: **Step 1 ‚Äî Verify API availability** Before calling the API, check that the browser actually supports it. This is a defensive programming practice expected by KTU examiners. ```javascript if (!navigator.geolocation) { document.getElementById("out").innerHTML = "Geolocation not supported."; throw new Error("Geolocation API unavailable"); } ``` **Step 2 ‚Äî Define the success callback** The browser invokes this callback **asynchronously** with a `Position` object once the location is determined. We extract `coords.latitude` and `coords.longitude` and inject them into the DOM. ```javascript function showPosition(position) { const lat = position.coords.latitude; const lon = position.coords.longitude; document.getElementById("out").innerHTML = "Latitude: " + lat + "
Longitude: " + lon; } ``` **Step 3 ‚Äî Define the error callback** If the user denies permission, GPS is off, or the request times out, this handler is invoked with a `PositionError` object whose `code` is `1` (permission denied), `2` (position unavailable), or `3` (timeout). ```javascript function showError(error) { switch(error.code) { case error.PERMISSION_DENIED: msg = "User denied the request."; break; case error.POSITION_UNAVAILABLE: msg = "Location info unavailable."; break; case error.TIMEOUT: msg = "Request timed out."; break; case error.UNKNOWN_ERROR: msg = "Unknown error."; break; } document.getElementById("out").innerHTML = msg; } ``` **Step 4 ‚Äî Invoke the API on user gesture** The call must be made in response to a user action (button click) ‚Äî never on page load ‚Äî to satisfy browser security policies. ```javascript function getLocation() { navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(showPosition, showError, { enableHighAccuracy: true, timeout: 10000, maximumAge: 0 }); } ``` **Step 5 ‚Äî Complete HTML structure** The full file ties it all together. ```html Geolocation Demo

Find My Location

``` **Analytical reasoning matrix (why each step is necessary):** | Step | Why It Is Required | |---|---| | Feature detection (`if (!navigator.geolocation)`) | Older browsers / private modes may lack the API; avoids runtime crash. | | User-gesture invocation | Browser security policy forbids silent geolocation; click satisfies the policy. | | Asynchronous callback | Location lookup is I/O-bound (GPS/Wi-Fi triangulation); blocking would freeze the page. | | Error handling | Permission denial is common; graceful UX feedback is mandatory. | --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 ‚Äî Web Storage API: Counter Persistence **Problem statement:** *Create a page with two buttons ‚Äî "Increment" and "Reset" ‚Äî that maintain a counter across page reloads using `localStorage`.* ### Step-by-step analytical breakdown: **Step 1 ‚Äî Initialize the value on first load** We must guard against `null` (returned by `getItem` when the key is absent). Use the nullish-coalescing-style fallback. ```javascript let count = Number(localStorage.getItem("counter")) || 0; ``` **Step 2 ‚Äî Render the value** Update the DOM whenever the value changes. ```javascript function render() { document.getElementById("display").innerText = "Count: " + count; } ``` **Step 3 ‚Äî Increment handler** Read, modify, write back. Note that `localStorage` only stores **strings**, so we explicitly use `Number(...)` and `String(...)` conversions. ```javascript function increment() { count += 1; localStorage.setItem("counter", String(count)); render(); } ``` **Step 4 ‚Äî Reset handler** ```javascript function reset() { localStorage.removeItem("counter"); count = 0; render(); } ``` **Step 5 ‚Äî Full HTML** ```html Persistent Counter

Count: 0

``` **Analytical reasoning matrix:** | Concern | Resolution | Reason | |---|---|---| | Why `Number(...)` on read? | `getItem` returns string or null. | Arithmetic requires numeric type. | | Why `String(count)` on write? | `setItem` implicitly converts, but explicit is best practice. | Avoids type-coercion ambiguity. | | Why `removeItem` instead of `setItem("counter", "0")`? | Cleaner; treats absence as fresh state. | Semantically "reset" ‚âÝ "set to zero". | | Why does this work after closing the tab? | `localStorage` persists per origin indefinitely. | Replaces server-side session in simple cases. | --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3 ‚Äî Drag and Drop API: Image Reordering **Problem statement:** *Implement a list where users can drag items to reorder them.* ### Step-by-step analytical breakdown: **Step 1 ‚Äî Make source draggable** Set the HTML attribute `draggable="true"` and tag each item with a unique identifier. ```html
  • Item 1
  • Item 2
  • Item 3
``` **Step 2 ‚Äî Attach `dragstart` on each item** Store the dragged item's ID in the `DataTransfer` object. Without `setData`, the `drop` event will not fire. ```javascript document.querySelectorAll("#list li").forEach(item => { item.addEventListener("dragstart", e => { e.dataTransfer.setData("text/plain", e.target.dataset.id); e.dataTransfer.effectAllowed = "move"; }); }); ``` **Step 3 ‚Äî Allow drops on the list** The `dragover` handler **must** call `e.preventDefault()`; without this, the browser rejects the drop by default. ```javascript const list = document.getElementById("list"); list.addEventListener("dragover", e => { e.preventDefault(); e.dataTransfer.dropEffect = "move"; }); ``` **Step 4 ‚Äî Handle `drop`** Read the ID, find the original element, and re-insert it before the drop target. ```javascript list.addEventListener("drop", e => { e.preventDefault(); const id = e.dataTransfer.getData("text/plain"); const dragged = document.querySelector(`[data-id="${id}"]`); const target = e.target.closest("li"); if (target && dragged !== target) { list.insertBefore(dragged, target); } }); ``` **Analytical reasoning matrix:** | Event | Why It Is Needed | |---|---| | `dragstart` | Initiates the drag; sets payload. | | `dragover` + `preventDefault` | Authorizes the drop zone. | | `drop` | Performs the reordering. | | `e.target.closest("li")` | Handles drops on text nodes inside `
  • `. | --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix ‚Äî All Three APIs | Dimension | Geolocation | Web Storage | Drag & Drop | |---|---|---|---| | Asynchronous? | **Yes** (callback/Promise) | **No** (synchronous) | **Event-driven** | | User permission? | **Yes** (mandatory) | **No** | **No** | | Data persistence? | No (transient) | **Yes** (local/session) | No | | Primary data structure | `Position` object | Key-value string pairs | `DataTransfer` object | | Common exam trap | Forgetting error callback | Forgetting `Number()` cast | Forgetting `preventDefault()` in `dragover` | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of an HTML5 API Call The block diagram below illustrates the **runtime flow** when JavaScript invokes a typical HTML API. This is the exact mental model the KTU examiner expects you to draw (or describe) in Part B answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User Action Click or Load Event"] --> B["JavaScript Code invokes API method"] B --> C{"Browser API Implementation exists"} C -- No --> D["Feature Detection fails Throw error or fallback"] C -- Yes --> E["Browser asks User Permission if required"] E -- Denied --> F["Error Callback invoked PositionError or similar"] E -- Granted --> G["Browser performs underlying operation GPS read Disk read Drag capture"] G --> H["Data returned to JavaScript Position object Storage value DataTransfer"] H --> I["Success Callback executes Updates DOM Triggers side effects"] F --> J["Error Handler updates DOM User feedback message"] I --> K["Page State updated UI reflects new data"] J --> K ``` **How to read this diagram in an exam answer:** 1. The cycle starts with a **user gesture** (click, drag, submit). 2. JavaScript calls the API; the browser checks for **feature support**. 3. If permission is required (Geolocation, Camera, Notifications), the browser shows a prompt. 4. On success, data is returned via a callback or promise; on failure, the error handler runs. 5. The DOM is finally updated to reflect the new state. --- ## 4.2 Geolocation API Method-Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["navigator.geolocation"] --> B["getCurrentPosition"] A --> C["watchPosition"] A --> D["clearWatch"] B --> E["Success Position object"] B --> F["Error PositionError"] C --> G["watchId number"] G --> D E --> H["coords latitude, longitude, accuracy, altitude, heading, speed"] E --> I["timestamp DOMTimeStamp"] F --> J["code 1 PERMISSION_DENIED"] F --> K["code 2 POSITION_UNAVAILABLE"] F --> L["code 3 TIMEOUT"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Web Storage API Object Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["window object"] --> B["localStorage Storage"] A --> C["sessionStorage Storage"] B --> D["setItem key value"] B --> E["getItem key"] B --> F["removeItem key"] B --> G["clear"] B --> H["length property"] C --> D C --> E C --> F C --> G C --> H D -- triggers --> I["storage event on other tabs of same origin"] ``` **Key insight:** Both `localStorage` and `sessionStorage` expose the **same method interface**; the only difference is *lifetime* and *scope*. This is a high-yield factoid for KTU short-answer questions. --- ## 4.4 Drag and Drop Event Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Source Element participant DT as DataTransfer participant T as Target Element participant U as User U->>S: mousedown + drag begins S->>S: dragstart fires S->>DT: setData format data U->>T: dragover fires repeatedly T->>T: preventDefault to allow drop U->>T: drop fires T->>DT: getData format DT-->>T: returns payload string S->>S: dragend fires ``` > [!TIP] > The `dragover` event fires **continuously** (every few milliseconds) while the cursor is over the target. The `preventDefault()` call inside `dragover` is what unlocks the `drop` event. **Forgetting it is the #1 reason drag-and-drop code "doesn't work" ‚Äî and a guaranteed KTU mark-deduction trigger.** --- ## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram ‚Äî Storage vs. Cookies ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Client-Side Storage Options"] --> B["localStorage"] A --> C["sessionStorage"] A --> D["Cookies document.cookie"] A --> E["IndexedDB"] B -- capacity --> F["5 to 10 MB per origin"] C -- capacity --> G["approximately 5 MB per tab"] D -- capacity --> H["approximately 4 KB per cookie"] E -- capacity --> I["typically 50 percent of disk quota per origin"] B -- expiry --> J["never until cleared"] C -- expiry --> K["tab close"] D -- expiry --> L["set by max-age or expires"] E -- expiry --> M["never until cleared"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]` **Define HTML5 Geolocation API. List any four properties of the `coords` object returned by `getCurrentPosition()`.** **Model Answer:** > The **HTML5 Geolocation API** is a browser-native interface that allows JavaScript to obtain the geographic position of the user's device (laptop, smartphone, tablet) by querying GPS hardware, Wi-Fi triangulation, or IP-based geolocation. It is accessed via the read-only `navigator.geolocation` object and requires **explicit user permission** for security reasons. **Four properties of the `coords` object:** 1. **`coords.latitude`** ‚Äî Geographic latitude in **decimal degrees** (range: `-90` to `+90`, WGS84 datum). 2. **`coords.longitude`** ‚Äî Geographic longitude in **decimal degrees** (range: `-180` to `+180`). 3. **`coords.accuracy`** ‚Äî Accuracy level of the latitude/longitude fix in **meters** (95% confidence interval). 4. **`coords.altitude`** ‚Äî Height above the WGS84 ellipsoid in **meters** (`null` if not supported). 5. *(Optional fifth)* **`coords.speed`** ‚Äî Instantaneous ground speed in **meters/second** (`null` if stationary). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write `position.latitude` instead of `position.coords.latitude`. The `coords` sub-object is **mandatory** ‚Äî losing 1 mark for this is common. --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between `localStorage` and `sessionStorage` with respect to scope, lifetime, and capacity.** **Model Answer:** | Property | `localStorage` | `sessionStorage` | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Shared across **all tabs/windows** of the same origin | Restricted to the **current browser tab/window** only | | **Lifetime** | **Permanent** ‚Äî persists until explicitly deleted by code or the user clears browser data | **Transient** ‚Äî cleared automatically when the tab/window is closed | | **Capacity** | Approximately **5‚Äì10 MB** per origin (browser-dependent) | Approximately **5 MB** per tab | | **Storage Event** | Fires on **other** tabs of same origin when modified | Does **not** fire across tabs (since it's per-tab) | | **Use Case** | "Remember Me" preferences, theme, JWT tokens | Wizard-step data, temporary form drafts | --- ## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU End-Semester Examinations for a 14-mark Part B question typically follow a **two-sub-part** structure: **part (a) for 7 marks** and **part (b) for 7 marks**. Each sub-part is a self-contained conceptual/programming problem. --- ### PART B ‚Äî Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Geolocation API** in HTML5 with its key methods. Write a complete HTML page that, on a button click, displays the user's latitude and longitude with proper error handling. **Model Solution:** **Key Methods of the Geolocation API:** 1. **`navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(success, error, options)`** ‚Äî Retrieves the current geographic position of the device **once** and invokes the success callback with a `Position` object. 2. **`navigator.geolocation.watchPosition(success, error, options)`** ‚Äî Continuously tracks the position, invoking the success callback whenever a new position is determined. Returns a numeric `watchId`. 3. **`navigator.geolocation.clearWatch(watchId)`** ‚Äî Cancels an ongoing watch started by `watchPosition`, using the ID returned earlier. **Complete HTML Code:** ```html Geolocation Demo

    My Location

    ``` **Explanation of key features:** - The `options` object passed to `getCurrentPosition` enables `enableHighAccuracy` (uses GPS if available), sets a 10-second `timeout`, and `maximumAge: 0` (always fetch a fresh fix). - Error handling uses the `PositionError.code` field with the constants `PERMISSION_DENIED`, `POSITION_UNAVAILABLE`, and `TIMEOUT`. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Web Storage API**. Write a program to store and retrieve a user's name and email using `localStorage`, and show how `sessionStorage` would differ in the same scenario. **Model Solution:** **Web Storage API Overview:** The Web Storage API provides two key-value storage objects, both accessible via the `window` object: - `localStorage` ‚Äî Persistent, per-origin, with no expiration. - `sessionStorage` ‚Äî Per-tab/per-window, cleared on tab close. Both expose the same interface: `setItem(k, v)`, `getItem(k)`, `removeItem(k)`, `clear()`, `key(i)`, and the `length` property. **Complete HTML Program:** ```html Web Storage Demo

    User Registration

    Name:

    Email:

    ``` **How `sessionStorage` Differs:** If we replace every occurrence of `localStorage` with `sessionStorage` in the code above: - The data is **only visible in the current tab** ‚Äî opening a new tab and clicking "Load Saved Data" will display *"Not set"*. - The data is **automatically destroyed** when the tab is closed. - It is ideal for **multi-step form wizards** where the user should not carry data across browsing sessions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Forgetting `e.preventDefault()` in the `dragover` event (if a related question appears) **deducts 2 marks** ‚Äî the drop never fires. > - Writing `localStorage.getItem` without handling the `null` return value (e.g., on first load) **deducts 1 mark** ‚Äî use `|| "Not set"`. > - In Geolocation code, omitting the `error` callback parameter **deducts 1 mark** ‚Äî silent failures are not accepted by KTU evaluators. > - Writing `position.latitude` instead of `position.coords.latitude` **deducts 1 mark** ‚Äî examiners are strict about the nested object. --- ### PART B ‚Äî Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Drag and Drop API** in HTML5. List the events involved and write a program to drag a `
    ` from one container to another. **Model Solution:** **Events in the Drag and Drop API:** | Event | Fired On | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `dragstart` | Source element | Drag begins; set `dataTransfer` payload | | `drag` | Source element | Continuously while dragging | | `dragend` | Source element | Drag operation ends (success or cancel) | | `dragenter` | Target element | Dragged element enters the target | | `dragover` | Target element | Dragged element is over the target (must call `preventDefault`) | | `dragleave` | Target element | Dragged element leaves the target | | `drop` | Target element | Dragged element is released (must call `preventDefault` in `dragover`) | **Complete HTML Program:** ```html
    Source
    Drag Me
    Destination
    ``` **Explanation:** - The `draggable="true"` attribute enables dragging on the green `
    `. - `setData("text/plain", id)` stores the element's ID in the `DataTransfer` object. - `allowDrop` calls `preventDefault()` on `dragover` to permit dropping. - `drop` retrieves the ID and moves the element via `appendChild`. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **File API** in HTML5. Write a program to read a text file selected by the user and display its content on the page without uploading to a server. **Model Solution:** **File API Overview:** The HTML5 File API enables web applications to read the **contents of files** selected via `` directly in the browser, **without uploading** to a server. It is exposed through: - The `files` property of file inputs, returning a `FileList`. - The `FileReader` object, providing asynchronous read methods: - `readAsText(file)` ‚Äî read as plain text - `readAsDataURL(file)` ‚Äî read as base64 data URL - `readAsArrayBuffer(file)` ‚Äî read as binary buffer **Complete HTML Program:** ```html File Reader Demo

    Local Text File Reader

    

  


```

**Explanation:**
- `e.target.files[0]` retrieves the first selected file.
- `new FileReader()` creates an asynchronous reader.
- `reader.onload` fires when reading completes; `event.target.result` contains the file's text.
- The `accept=".txt"` attribute hints to the browser to filter the file picker.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the KTU exam:**

- ‚≠ê **Geolocation API** is accessed via `navigator.geolocation` ‚Äî never instantiated with `new`.
- ‚≠ê The position object is **nested**: always use `position.coords.latitude`, **not** `position.latitude`.
- ‚≠ê Geolocation requires **HTTPS** in production and a **user gesture** to start ‚Äî silent calls on page load are blocked.
- ‚≠ê `localStorage` and `sessionStorage` share an **identical method interface**; they differ only in **scope** and **lifetime**.
- ‚≠ê `localStorage` persists across tabs and browser restarts; `sessionStorage` is per-tab.
- ‚≠ê The `storage` event fires on **other tabs only** (not the originating tab).
- ‚≠ê Drag-and-drop requires `draggable="true"`, `dragstart` ‚Üí `setData`, `dragover` ‚Üí `preventDefault`, `drop` ‚Üí `getData`.
- ‚≠ê The most common drag-and-drop bug: **forgetting `preventDefault()` in `dragover`**.
- ‚≠ê The `File API` uses `FileReader` for asynchronous file reading ‚Äî never use `fs.readFileSync` (Node-only).
- ‚≠ê HTML APIs are **browser-native** ‚Äî no installation or `

topics.title: Introduction to CSS - Basic Syntax and Structure - Inline Styles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to CSS - Basic Syntax and Structure - Inline Styles ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** is a **style sheet language** used to describe the **presentation and visual formatting** of a document written in a markup language such as **HTML** or **XML**. CSS is a cornerstone technology of the World Wide Web, alongside HTML and JavaScript, and is officially maintained by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** > *"CSS is a rule-based language that allows web developers to apply styling (colors, fonts, spacing, layouts) to HTML elements through selectors, properties, and values, separating content (HTML) from presentation (CSS)."* CSS enables **separation of concerns** ‚Äî a key software engineering principle where content structure (HTML) is kept distinct from visual presentation (CSS). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Intuition Imagine you are decorating a newly built house: | Component | Real-World Analogy | Web Equivalent | |-----------|-------------------|----------------| | **House Structure** (walls, rooms, doors) | The skeleton/blueprint | **HTML** (defines content) | | **Paint, Wallpaper, Furniture** | Aesthetic appearance | **CSS** (defines presentation) | | **Electrical Wiring / Smart Devices** | Interactive behavior | **JavaScript** (defines behavior) | - **HTML** says: *"There is a heading here, and a paragraph there."* - **CSS** says: *"Make the heading blue, 32px in size, with red background."* - **JavaScript** says: *"When the user clicks, hide the heading."* ### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Web Development > [!NOTE] > **The Three Core Web Technologies:** > 1. **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** ‚Äî Content & Structure > 2. **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)** ‚Äî Presentation & Styling > 3. **JavaScript** ‚Äî Behavior & Interactivity ### 1.4 Why CSS? ‚Äî The Problem It Solves Before CSS existed (in older HTML versions like HTML 3.2), formatting attributes like `` and `
` were embedded directly inside HTML tags. This caused three major problems: 1. **Code Bloating** ‚Äî Every page became huge because styling was repeated. 2. **Maintenance Nightmare** ‚Äî Changing a brand color across 100 pages meant editing 100 files. 3. **No Consistency** ‚Äî Same logical element looked different on different pages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CSS solves this by introducing the "One Source, Many Pages" rule** ‚Äî define style once, apply everywhere. ### 1.5 Introduction to Inline Styles An **Inline Style** is a CSS rule applied **directly to a single HTML element** using the **`style` attribute**. It is the most specific (highest priority) way to apply CSS, and overrides styles defined in `

Hello KTU

``` **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify all four CSS sources applying to `

`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Source}_1 &: \text{Element selector} \quad p \rightarrow \text{blue} \\ \text{Source}_2 &: \text{Class selector} \quad .highlight \rightarrow \text{green} \\ \text{Source}_3 &: \text{ID selector} \quad \#special \rightarrow \text{orange} \\ \text{Source}_4 &: \text{Inline style} \quad style="color: red;" \rightarrow \text{red} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the Specificity Hierarchy. $$ \text{Specificity Order} = \text{Element} < \text{Class} < \text{ID} < \text{Inline} $$ In numeric specificity terms (inline styles are not counted in the (a, b, c) tuple; they are computed as a separate, higher tier): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Element } p &: (0, 0, 0, 1) \\ \text{Class } .highlight &: (0, 0, 1, 0) \\ \text{ID } \#special &: (0, 1, 0, 0) \\ \text{Inline } style &: (1, 0, 0, 0, 0) \quad \text{(tier above ID)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Pick the highest. The inline style has the highest tier. $$ \boxed{\text{Final color} = \text{red}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ‚Äî Common Syntax Error Identification **Problem:** Identify the errors in the following inline style: ```html

Error Demo

``` **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Check colon usage ‚Äî `color: blue` ‚úì (correct). **Step 2:** Check separator after value ‚Äî `blue font-size` ‚úó (missing semicolon). **Step 3:** Check second declaration ‚Äî `font-size: 16px background` ‚úó (missing semicolon after `16px`). **Step 4:** Check third declaration ‚Äî `background: yellow` ‚úì correct, but final semicolon is good practice. **Step 5:** The corrected version: ```html

Error Demo

$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU practical exams, missing semicolons in inline styles cause the browser to **ignore subsequent properties silently** ‚Äî there is no error message. This is a classic debugging question in 3-mark problems. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Property Categories | Category | Common Properties | Example Values | Effect | |----------|------------------|----------------|--------| | **Color & Background** | `color`, `background`, `background-color` | `red`, `#FF0000`, `rgb(255,0,0)` | Text/element color | | **Typography** | `font-size`, `font-family`, `font-weight`, `font-style` | `16px`, `Arial`, `bold`, `italic` | Text appearance | | **Text Layout** | `text-align`, `text-decoration`, `line-height` | `center`, `underline`, `1.5` | Text positioning | | **Box Model** | `margin`, `padding`, `border`, `width`, `height` | `10px`, `2px solid black` | Element spacing | | **Display** | `display`, `visibility` | `block`, `inline`, `none` | Element flow | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Anatomy of an Inline CSS Style ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HTML Opening Tag
e.g. h1 p div"] --> B["Style Attribute
style=..."] B --> C["Declaration Block
Inside quotes"] C --> D1["Property 1: Value 1;"] C --> D2["Property 2: Value 2;"] C --> D3["Property 3: Value 3;"] D1 --> E["Semicolon Separators"] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F["HTML Content
Visible Text"] F --> G["Closing Tag
e.g. /h1 /p /div"] G --> H["Rendered in Browser
With Applied Styles"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî CSS Cascade Priority Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Multiple CSS Rules
Applied to One Element"] --> Q1{"Is there an
!important flag?"} Q1 -- Yes --> I["Highest Priority
!important wins"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is there an
inline style?"} Q2 -- Yes --> IS["Inline Style Wins
Highest Specificity"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is there an
ID selector?"} Q3 -- Yes --> ID["ID Selector Applied"] Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is there a
class selector?"} Q4 -- Yes --> CL["Class Selector Applied"] Q4 -- No --> Q5{"Is there an
element selector?"} Q5 -- Yes --> EL["Element Selector Applied"] Q5 -- No --> BR["Browser Default
Used"] IS --> Final["Final Computed Style
Rendered to User"] ID --> Final CL --> Final EL --> Final BR --> Final I --> Final ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Block-Level Architecture of CSS Application Methods ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HTML_Document["HTML Document"] direction TB HEAD["HEAD Section"] BODY["BODY Section"] end HEAD --> INT["Internal CSS
style tag inside head"] HEAD --> EXT["External CSS
link rel stylesheet href .css"] BODY --> INL["Inline CSS
style attribute on elements"] INT --> APPL["Applied to Single Page"] EXT --> APPL2["Applied to Multiple Pages"] INL --> APPL3["Applied to Single Element"] APPL --> REND["Browser Renders
Cascaded Final Style"] APPL2 --> REND APPL3 --> REND ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Inline Style Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Need to Style an Element?"] --> Q1{"Is the style
unique to this
one element?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is the style
truly one-time
or for testing?"} Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use Internal or
External CSS"] Q2 -- Yes --> INL["Use Inline Style
style attribute"] Q2 -- No --> Q3 INL --> Warn{"Avoid for
production websites?"} Warn -- Yes --> Q3 Warn -- Note --> Note["Note: Inline is acceptable
for email templates and
dynamic JS-generated styles"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]** *Define CSS. List any three advantages of using CSS in web development.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is a style sheet language used to describe the presentation of HTML or XML documents, separating content from design. **Three Advantages (2 Marks ‚Äî 1 mark each, or bundled):** 1. **Separation of Concerns** ‚Äî HTML handles content, CSS handles presentation, leading to cleaner code. 2. **Reusability** ‚Äî A single CSS file can style thousands of HTML pages; change once, update everywhere. 3. **Consistency** ‚Äî Uniform look and feel across all pages of a website. 4. **Reduced File Size** ‚Äî External CSS reduces HTML bloat, improving page load speed. 5. **Easy Maintenance** ‚Äî Style updates are centralized. *(Any three valid points accepted)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]** *Write the general syntax of an inline CSS style and explain its components.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Syntax (1 Mark):** ```html Content ``` **Explanation (2 Marks):** - **``** (¬Ω mark) ‚Äî The HTML element being styled (e.g., `

`, `

`). - **`style` attribute** (¬Ω mark) ‚Äî The HTML attribute that holds CSS declarations. - **`property: value;`** (¬Ω mark) ‚Äî Each declaration specifies a style attribute and its assigned value, terminated by a semicolon. - **Scope** (¬Ω mark) ‚Äî Affects only that single element, overriding all other style sources due to highest specificity. --- ### 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the basic structure and syntax of a CSS rule with a suitable example. Discuss the role of selector, property, and value with reference to at least five common CSS properties. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What are inline styles in CSS? Explain with a complete HTML program demonstrating inline styles applied to at least four different HTML elements. Discuss two advantages and two disadvantages of inline styling. **(7 Marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer ‚Äî 7 Marks:** **Basic CSS Rule Structure (3 Marks):** A CSS rule consists of three parts: ```css selector { property: value; property: value; } ``` - **Selector** (1 Mark) ‚Äî Identifies the HTML element(s) to be styled. - **Property** (1 Mark) ‚Äî The style attribute to be changed. - **Value** (1 Mark) ‚Äî The setting for that property. **Five Common CSS Properties Table (2 Marks):** | Property | Purpose | Example Value | |----------|---------|---------------| | `color` | Text color | `red`, `#FF0000` | | `background-color` | Element background | `yellow` | | `font-size` | Size of text | `20px` | | `text-align` | Horizontal alignment | `center` | | `font-family` | Typeface | `Arial` | **Example Code Block (2 Marks):** ```css h1 { color: navy; text-align: center; font-size: 32px; background-color: lightyellow; font-family: Georgia, serif; } ``` This rule applies navy, centered, 32px text with a light yellow background in Georgia font to all `

` elements. --- **(b) Model Answer ‚Äî 7 Marks:** **Definition of Inline Styles (2 Marks):** Inline styles are CSS declarations applied directly to an HTML element using the `style` attribute. The CSS rules are written inside the opening HTML tag and affect only that specific element. **Complete HTML Program (3 Marks):** ```html Inline Styles Demo

KTU Web Programming

This paragraph uses green text in Arial font.

This div has a gray background with 10px padding.
``` **Advantages (1 Mark ‚Äî ¬Ω each):** 1. **High Specificity** ‚Äî Inline styles override internal and external stylesheets, useful for one-off overrides. 2. **Quick Testing** ‚Äî Easy to test style changes without modifying CSS files. **Disadvantages (1 Mark ‚Äî ¬Ω each):** 1. **Violates Separation of Concerns** ‚Äî Mixes structure and presentation. 2. **Hard to Maintain** ‚Äî Changing a style across many elements requires editing each one individually. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Compare the three methods of applying CSS to an HTML document: Inline, Internal, and External. Create a comparison table and explain when each method is most appropriate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a complete HTML document that uses inline CSS to design a simple student bio-data card containing a heading, a paragraph, an image, and a styled table. The card should have a colored background, bordered table, and centered heading. **(7 Marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer ‚Äî 7 Marks:** **Three Methods of CSS Application (3 Marks):** 1. **Inline CSS** ‚Äî Written inside the `style` attribute of an HTML element. 2. **Internal CSS** ‚Äî Written inside a `

topics.title: Embedded Style Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Embedded Style Sheets ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Embedded Style Sheet** is a CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) implementation technique in which style declarations are written **inside the `` section of an HTML document**, enclosed within the `

APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University

This paragraph uses a class selector.

This paragraph uses an ID selector.

This is a normal paragraph using element defaults.

``` **Step-by-Step Annotation:** - **Line 5:** `` ensures proper character rendering. - **Line 7:** `` | `` | | **Specificity** | Highest $(1,0,0,0)$ | Medium | Lowest (barring `!important`) | | **Reusability** | None | Single page | Multi-page | | **Maintainability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | | **Page Load Speed** | Slower (HTML bloat) | Moderate | Fastest (cached) | | **Best Use Case** | One-off overrides | Single-page styling | Multi-page sites | | **KTU Exam Weight** | High | High | High | --- ### 3.4 Cascade Conflict Resolution Algorithm When two CSS rules target the same element with the same property, the browser follows this **5-step resolution order**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{Check for } !\text{important flag} \\ \text{Step 2: } & \text{Compare origin (author > user > browser)} \\ \text{Step 3: } & \text{Compare specificity scores} \\ \text{Step 4: } & \text{If tied, later source order wins} \\ \text{Step 5: } & \text{If still tied, property is inherited from parent} \end{aligned} $$ **Visualization of Cascade Priority:** $$\boxed{\text{!important User} > \text{!important Author} > \text{Inline} > \text{Embedded/External IDs} > \text{Classes} > \text{Elements}}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 HTML Document Architecture with Embedded Style Sheet ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HTML Document] --> B[HEAD Section] A --> C[BODY Section] B --> D[meta charset UTF-8] B --> E[title KTU Embedded] B --> F[style type text/css] F --> F1[Selector: body] F --> F2[Selector: h1] F --> F3[Selector: .class] F --> F4[Selector: #id] F1 --> G1[Property-Value Pairs] F2 --> G2[Property-Value Pairs] F3 --> G3[Property-Value Pairs] F4 --> G4[Property-Value Pairs] C --> H1[h1 heading] C --> H2[p class highlight] C --> H3[p id special] C --> H4[p normal] F1 -.->|targets| C F2 -.->|targets| H1 F3 -.->|targets| H2 F4 -.->|targets| H3 G1 --> Z[Final Rendered Page] G2 --> Z G3 --> Z G4 --> Z H1 --> Z H2 --> Z H3 --> Z H4 --> Z ``` --- ### 4.2 CSS Cascade Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Multiple CSS Rules Target Same Element] --> Q{Is there !important flag} Q -->|Yes| R[Apply !important rule with highest specificity] Q -->|No| S[Compare Specificity Scores] S --> T{Are specificities different} T -->|Yes| U[Apply higher specificity rule] T -->|No| V[Check Source Order] V --> W{Which rule appears later} W -->|Rule X| X[Apply Rule X] W -->|Rule Y| Y[Apply Rule Y] R --> OUT[Final Computed Style] U --> OUT X --> OUT Y --> OUT ``` --- ### 4.3 Specificity Calculation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CSS Selector String] --> B[Tokenize Selectors] B --> C[Count IDs] B --> D[Count Classes] B --> E[Count Elements] C --> F1[Position b: ID count] D --> F2[Position c: Class count] E --> F3[Position d: Element count] F1 --> G[Build Specificity Tuple] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Compare tuples left to right] H --> I[Highest tuple wins] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** **Define embedded style sheet. List any two of its advantages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** An embedded style sheet is a CSS technique in which style rules are placed inside the `` section of an HTML document using the ` ``` The `type` attribute specifies the MIME type of the stylesheet, which is `text/css` (default in HTML5). **Example Code (2 marks):** ```html

KTU Web Programming

Embedded style sheets are page-scoped.

``` **Advantages (1 mark ‚Äî any 2):** - No need to create or link a separate CSS file. - Allows page-specific customization. - Reduces code redundancy within a single page. **Disadvantages (1 mark ‚Äî any 2):** - Styles cannot be reused across multiple pages. - Increases HTML file size. - Maintenance becomes difficult for large websites. --- ### ‚úÖ Model Solution for Q.A (b) **Specificity Rules Explanation (3 marks):** CSS specificity is a four-part tuple $(a, b, c, d)$: - $a$ = number of inline `style` attributes - $b$ = number of ID selectors - $c$ = number of class, attribute, and pseudo-class selectors - $d$ = number of element and pseudo-element selectors **Example (2 marks):** ```css #header .nav li.active a { color: red; } /* Specificity: (0, 1, 2, 2) */ .nav a { color: green; } /* Specificity: (0, 0, 1, 1) */ ``` **Cascade Resolution Algorithm (2 marks):** 1. **!important** declarations override normal rules. 2. **Inline styles** outrank embedded/external styles. 3. **Higher specificity score** wins. 4. When specificity is tied, **later rule in source** wins. 5. If still unresolved, the property is **inherited from the parent**. In the example above, the first rule wins because its specificity $(0, 1, 2, 2)$ is greater than $(0, 0, 1, 1)$. --- #### üìó Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** What are the three methods of applying CSS to an HTML document? Compare them based on specificity, reusability, and use case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an HTML program that uses an embedded style sheet to format a page containing a heading, two paragraphs (one styled with a class, one with an ID), and an unordered list. Show the output. **(7 marks)** --- ### ‚úÖ Model Solution for Q.B (a) **Three Methods (3 marks):** 1. **Inline Style** ‚Äî `style="color:red"` directly in HTML tags. 2. **Embedded Style Sheet** ‚Äî `

Web Programming Module 2

This paragraph is styled using a class selector, which can be reused on multiple elements.

This paragraph uses an ID selector, which is unique to a single element.

  • HTML Structure
  • Embedded CSS
  • Cascade & Specificity
``` **Output Description (2 marks):** - The page displays a centered dark-blue heading "WEB PROGRAMMING MODULE 2". - A grey paragraph (intro) is followed by a crimson bolded note with a left border. - A square-bulleted green unordered list appears at the bottom. - The body uses a serif font on a light grey background with 30px margins. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Specificity comparison is tuple-wise, NOT arithmetic.** $(0, 1, 0, 0)$ is **NOT** equal to $(0, 0, 5, 0)$ ‚Äî the ID position always wins. > - **Do not confuse** the `` - ‚úÖ **Three CSS Application Methods:** Inline $(1,0,0,0)$ > Embedded > External. - ‚úÖ **Specificity Tuple:** $(a, b, c, d)$ ‚Äî Inline, ID, Class, Element. - ‚úÖ **Comparison Rule:** Compare tuples **left-to-right**; the first non-equal position decides. - ‚úÖ **Cascade Order:** `!important` > Inline > Specificity > Source Order > Inheritance. - ‚úÖ **Advantages:** No extra HTTP request, page-specific styling, reduces inline repetition. - ‚úÖ **Disadvantages:** Not reusable, increases file size, harder to maintain for multi-page sites. - ‚úÖ **Best Practice:** Use external style sheets for production sites, embedded for prototypes or single-page customizations. - ‚úÖ **Selectors Recap:** Element (`h1`), Class (`.cls`), ID (`#id`), Attribute (`[type="text"]`), Pseudo-class (`:hover`). - ‚úÖ **HTML5 Note:** `type="text/css"` is optional in HTML5; default is `text/css`. - ‚úÖ **Multiple `

topics.title: Conflict Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Conflict Resolution # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conflict Resolution** in the context of web development and version control refers to the systematic process of identifying, evaluating, and reconciling incompatible changes made by multiple developers working concurrently on the same set of files within a shared repository. It is a critical operation within **Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)** such as **Git**, which is the industry-standard tool mandated by the KTU 2024 Web Programming syllabus. A **conflict** (often called a *merge conflict*) arises when two or more branches modify the same line(s) of a file in mutually exclusive ways, or when one developer edits a file while another deletes it. The version control system (VCS) cannot automatically determine which change is "correct," so it halts the automated merge process and flags the file as **conflicted**.
