          width x height"] A --> B B --> C C --> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each layer wraps the next. The diagram reads from the **outermost** (margin) inward to the **content**. The element's total footprint = all four layers combined. --- ## 4.2 Position Property Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Need to position an element?"] --> Q1{"In normal flow?"} Q1 -->|Yes, with small shift| A["position: relative
          Space reserved"] Q1 -->|No, precise placement| B["position: absolute
          Removed from flow"] Q1 -->|Lock to viewport| C["position: fixed
          Doesn't scroll"] Q1 -->|Scrolls then sticks| D["position: sticky
          Hybrid behavior"] Q1 -->|Default, no offset| E["position: static
          Normal flow only"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Box Model Calculation Flow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR W["width: 200px"] --> TOTAL P["padding: 20px
          x 2 sides"] --> TOTAL B["border: 5px
          x 2 sides"] --> TOTAL M["margin: 15px
          x 2 sides"] --> TOTAL TOTAL["TOTAL HORIZONTAL SPACE
          = 200 + 2(20) + 2(5) + 2(15)
          = 280px"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Display Property Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["display property"] Root --> B1["BLOCK
          New line, full width
          div, p, h1"] Root --> I1["INLINE
          Flows with text, no size
          span, a, em"] Root --> IB["INLINE-BLOCK
          Flows with text, has size
          img, button"] Root --> H["HIDDEN
          Removed from layout
          display: none"] Root --> F["FLEX
          1D layout system"] Root --> G["GRID
          2D layout system"] ``` --- ## 4.5 Responsive Design Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MOBILE["MOBILE FIRST BASE STYLE"] M1["width: 100%"] M2["font-size: 14px"] M3["flex-direction: column"] end subgraph TABLET["@media min-width: 768px"] T1["width: 750px"] T2["font-size: 16px"] T3["flex-direction: row"] end subgraph DESKTOP["@media min-width: 1024px"] D1["width: 1170px"] D2["font-size: 18px"] D3["max-width: 3 columns"] end MOBILE --> TABLET --> DESKTOP ``` --- ## 4.6 Background Property Layer Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["Layer 1: background-color
          Base solid color"] L2["Layer 2: background-image
          Image / Gradient"] L3["Layer 3: background-repeat
          Tiling pattern"] L4["Layer 4: background-position
          Anchor point"] L5["Layer 5: background-size
          Scaling behavior"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 ``` --- ## 4.7 CSS Position Reference Frame Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD R["Reference Frame Logic"] R --> P1["position: static
          No reference frame
          Offsets ignored"] R --> P2["position: relative
          Reference: ITSELF
          Offsets work"] R --> P3["position: absolute
          Reference: Nearest positioned
          ancestor OR viewport"] R --> P4["position: fixed
          Reference: VIEWPORT
          Always"] R --> P5["position: sticky
          Reference: Nearest scrolling
          ancestor"] ``` --- ## 4.8 Div vs Span Architectural Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR DIV["DIV ELEMENT
          Display: block
          Takes full width
          Forces line break"] SPAN["SPAN ELEMENT
          Display: inline
          Takes content width
          Flows with text"] DIV --> D1["Used for: sections, cards,
          page layouts, containers"] SPAN --> S1["Used for: text highlights,
          inline icons, word styling"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define CSS Box Model with a labeled diagram. List its components. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **CSS Box Model** is a rectangular box representation that wraps every HTML element. It consists of four concentric layers (from innermost to outermost): 1. **Content** ‚Äî The actual text, image, or media (defined by `width` and `height`) 2. **Padding** ‚Äî Transparent space *inside* the border, surrounding the content 3. **Border** ‚Äî A visible line that surrounds the padding and content 4. **Margin** ‚Äî Transparent space *outside* the border, separating this element from others **Total horizontal space formula:** $$ \text{Total Width} = \text{width} + 2 \times (\text{padding} + \text{border} + \text{margin}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1) + 4 components listed (1.5) + Formula/diagram (0.5) --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between `
          ` and `` elements with examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | `
          ` | `` | |---|---|---| | Display type | Block-level | Inline | | New line | Yes (before & after) | No | | Width/Height | Respected | Ignored | | Use case | Page sections, layouts | Inline text styling | | Example | `
          ...
          ` | `This is important` | **Code Example:** ```html
          This is a div. This is a span inside it.
          ``` --- ## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks **Explain the CSS `position` property in detail. Compare `relative`, `absolute`, and `fixed` positioning with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** The `position` property in CSS specifies how an element is placed in the document. There are five values: `static`, `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, and `sticky`. **1. `position: relative`:** - The element is positioned **relative to its normal flow position** - Offsets (`top`, `left`, etc.) shift the element from where it *would* be - **Original space is reserved** ‚Äî no other element fills it - Acts as a positioning context for absolutely-positioned children ```css .rel-box { position: relative; top: 20px; left: 30px; background: lightblue; } ``` **2. `position: absolute`:** - The element is **removed from the document flow** - Positioned relative to the **nearest positioned ancestor** (or viewport if none) - The original space is **collapsed** ‚Äî other elements fill it - Useful for modals, tooltips, dropdowns ```css .abs-box { position: absolute; top: 50px; right: 20px; background: lightcoral; } ``` **3. `position: fixed`:** - The element is **removed from flow** and **locked to the viewport** - Does **not scroll** with the page - Always positioned relative to the browser window - Used for sticky navbars, chat widgets, "back to top" buttons ```css .fixed-nav { position: fixed; top: 0; width: 100%; background: #333; color: white; } ``` **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Relative | Absolute | Fixed | |---|---|---|---| | Reference frame | Itself | Nearest positioned ancestor | Viewport | | Original space | Preserved | Collapsed | Collapsed | | Scrolls with page | Yes (if no offset changes) | Yes | No | | Use case | Minor shifts, anchors | Modals, tooltips | Sticky nav, banners | --- #### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks **Describe the CSS Box Model. With a suitable example, show how `box-sizing: border-box` differs from `content-box`.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** The **CSS Box Model** describes the rectangular boxes generated for elements in the document tree. Each box has four areas: **content, padding, border, margin**. **Visual Representation:** ``` +-------------------------------+ ‚Üê margin (outside) |+-----------------------------+| ‚Üê border ||+---------------------------+|| ‚Üê padding ||| CONTENT (text/image) ||| ||+---------------------------+|| |+-----------------------------+| +-------------------------------+ ``` **Default: `box-sizing: content-box`** - `width` and `height` apply **only to the content area** - Padding and border are **added on top** of the declared width **Example:** ```css .box1 { box-sizing: content-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid #000; } ``` **Calculated total width:** $$ \text{Total} = 200 + 2(20) + 2(5) = 250 \text{ px} $$ **Actual content area:** `200px` (as declared) **Alternative: `box-sizing: border-box`** - `width` and `height` **include** content + padding + border - More predictable sizing, preferred in responsive design ```css .box2 { box-sizing: border-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid #000; } ``` **Calculated total width:** `200px` (as declared) **Actual content area:** $$ \text{Content} = 200 - 2(20) - 2(5) = 200 - 50 = 150 \text{ px} $$ **Practical Comparison:** | Property | `content-box` | `border-box` | |---|---|---| | Declared width | 200px | 200px | | Total rendered width | 250px | 200px | | Content area | 200px | 150px | | Predictability | Low (math required) | High (intuitive) | > [!NOTE] > **Best Practice:** Apply `box-sizing: border-box` globally using: > ```css > * { box-sizing: border-box; } > ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks **Explain the different CSS background properties with examples. What is the difference between `background-size: cover` and `contain`?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** CSS provides a comprehensive set of **background properties** for styling element backdrops: **1. `background-color`** Sets a solid color background. ```css .box { background-color: #f5f5f5; } ``` **2. `background-image`** Sets one or more background images (top-most listed first). ```css .box { background-image: url('pattern.png'); } ``` **3. `background-repeat`** Controls tiling behavior. - `repeat` (default) ‚Äî tiles both directions - `no-repeat` ‚Äî single instance - `repeat-x` / `repeat-y` ‚Äî tiles only horizontally/vertically **4. `background-position`** Sets the starting position of the background image. - Keywords: `top left`, `center`, `bottom right` - Percentages: `0% 0%` (top-left), `100% 100%` (bottom-right) - Pixels: `20px 30px` **5. `background-size`** Defines the dimensions of the background image. - `auto` (default) ‚Äî original size - `cover` ‚Äî scales to **fill container**, may crop - `contain` ‚Äî scales to **fit inside container**, may leave gaps - Length/percentage: `100% 50%` **6. `background-attachment`** Determines scroll behavior. - `scroll` (default) ‚Äî scrolls with the page - `fixed` ‚Äî stays fixed to viewport (parallax effect) - `local` ‚Äî scrolls with element's content **7. `background-origin`** Defines the reference box for positioning. - `border-box` / `padding-box` (default) / `content-box` **8. `background-clip`** Defines the painting area of the background. - `border-box` (default) / `padding-box` / `content-box` **The Shorthand:** ```css .hero { background: #000 url('hero.jpg') center/cover no-repeat fixed; } ``` **`cover` vs `contain` ‚Äî Detailed Comparison:** | Aspect | `cover` | `contain` | |---|---|---| | Behavior | Fills entire container | Fits entire image within container | | Image cropping | Yes (may crop) | No (always visible) | | Empty space | None | Yes (letterboxing) | | Aspect ratio | Maintained | Maintained | | Use case | Hero images, backgrounds | Thumbnails, logos, product images | | Visual effect | Immersive, edge-to-edge | Complete visibility, padded | **Example:** ```css .cover-bg { background: url('a.jpg') center/cover no-repeat; } .contain-bg { background: url('a.jpg') center/contain no-repeat; } ``` --- #### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks **What are Media Queries in CSS? Explain mobile-first responsive design with a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Media Queries** are CSS techniques that apply styles based on the **characteristics of the device** or viewport ‚Äî most commonly **width, height, resolution, and orientation**. They are the cornerstone of **Responsive Web Design (RWD)**. **Syntax:** ```css @media (condition) { /* CSS rules */ } ``` **Common Media Features:** - `width` / `height` ‚Äî viewport dimensions - `min-width` / `max-width` ‚Äî range queries - `orientation` ‚Äî `portrait` or `landscape` - `resolution` ‚Äî DPI/PPI for high-DPI screens - `hover` / `pointer` ‚Äî input capability detection **Media Query Logic:** - Use `and` to combine conditions - Use `not` to negate - Use `,` (comma) for OR logic ```css @media screen and (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px) { ... } @media not screen and (orientation: landscape) { ... } ``` **Mobile-First Design Philosophy:** In mobile-first, we write **base styles for the smallest screens** (mobile), then use `min-width` queries to **progressively enhance** the design for larger screens. **Why Mobile-First?** 1. **Performance** ‚Äî Mobile devices load minimal CSS first 2. **Simplicity** ‚Äî Avoids complex overrides common in desktop-first 3. **Google's preference** ‚Äî Mobile-first indexing (SEO benefit) 4. **Industry standard** ‚Äî Aligns with modern frameworks (Bootstrap 5, Tailwind) **Complete Example:** **HTML:** ```html
          Card 1
          Card 2
          Card 3
          ``` **CSS (Mobile-First):** ```css /* BASE = MOBILE (applies to all viewports) */ * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; } .container { width: 100%; padding: 10px; display: flex; flex-direction: column; /* Stack on mobile */ gap: 10px; } .card { background: #3498db; color: white; padding: 20px; text-align: center; border-radius: 8px; font-size: 14px; } /* TABLET ‚Äî 768px and up */ @media (min-width: 768px) { .container { flex-direction: row; /* Side by side */ max-width: 750px; margin: 0 auto; } .card { font-size: 16px; } } /* DESKTOP ‚Äî 1024px and up */ @media (min-width: 1024px) { .container { max-width: 1170px; gap: 20px; } .card { font-size: 18px; padding: 30px; } } ``` **Standard Breakpoints:** | Device | Min-Width | |---|---| | Small phones | 320px | | Large phones | 480px | | Tablets | 768px | | Laptops | 1024px | | Desktops | 1280px | | Large screens | 1440px+ | **Result Visualization:** - **Mobile (375px):** Cards stack vertically (1 column) - **Tablet (768px):** Cards in a row (3 columns, smaller padding) - **Desktop (1280px):** Cards in a row (larger padding and font) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bonus Tip:** Always include this in your HTML `` for proper mobile rendering: > ```html > > ``` --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing `position: relative` with `absolute`** ‚Äî Students write that relative removes the element from flow. **NO!** Only `absolute`/`fixed` remove from flow. Relative *preserves* the original space. > > 2. **Box Model Formula Mistakes** ‚Äî Writing `Total = width + padding + border + margin` (forgetting the **√ó2** multiplier for left and right sides). > > 3. **Forgetting the viewport meta tag** in responsive design questions ‚Äî Examiners **specifically check** for this in mobile-related answers. > > 4. **Wrong order in `background` shorthand** ‚Äî The `/` separator between position and size is **mandatory**; missing it costs marks. > > 5. **Confusing `cover` and `contain`** ‚Äî `cover` fills (crops), `contain` fits (pads). VTU/KTU examiners LOVE this question. > > 6. **Writing `@media (max-width)` for mobile-first** ‚Äî Mobile-first uses `min-width`. Mixing these up shows conceptual confusion. > > 7. **Missing `box-sizing: border-box`** in responsive examples ‚Äî Modern best practice; failing to mention it loses marks in 7-mark questions. > > 8. **Not labeling all 4 layers of the box model** in diagrams ‚Äî Padding, Border, Margin, Content must all be **clearly named**. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **üöÄ Quick Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):** ### **1. CSS Positioning (Relative)** - ‚≠ê `position: relative` ‚Üí **shifts element**, **preserves space** - ‚≠ê Offsets: `top`, `right`, `bottom`, `left` - ‚≠ê Acts as **containing block** for absolute children - ‚≠ê `z-index` works only on positioned elements ### **2. CSS Backgrounds** - ‚≠ê Shorthand order: `color image position/size repeat attachment origin clip` - ‚≠ê `/` separator is **mandatory** before `background-size` - ‚≠ê `cover` = fills (crops), `contain` = fits (pads) - ‚≠ê Multiple backgrounds are **comma-separated** (first = topmost) ### **3. List Styles** - ‚≠ê Three parts: `list-style-type`, `list-style-position`, `list-style-image` - ‚≠ê `none` removes markers completely - ‚≠ê `outside` (default) vs `inside` (affects text wrap) - ‚≠ê Types: `disc`, `circle`, `square`, `decimal`, `roman`, `alpha` ### **4. Element Dimensions** - ‚≠ê Units: `px`, `%`, `em`, `rem`, `vw`, `vh` - ‚≠ê `box-sizing: content-box` (default) vs `border-box` - ‚≠ê `min-width`/`max-width` for responsive constraints ### **5. Table Layouts** - ‚≠ê `table-layout: auto` (smart, slow) vs `fixed` (predictable, fast) - ‚≠ê `border-collapse: collapse` merges cell borders - ‚≠ê `border-spacing` works only with `border-collapse: separate` ### **6. Box Model & Text Flow** - ‚≠ê **Formula:** `Total = content + padding + border + margin` (√ó2 for both sides) - ‚≠ê `display: block` (new line) vs `inline` (flows) vs `inline-block` (hybrid) - ‚≠ê **Margin collapse:** vertical margins merge to `max(a, b)` - ‚≠ê `box-sizing: border-box` is modern best practice ### **7. Div and Span** - ‚≠ê `
          ` = **block-level** generic container - ‚≠ê `` = **inline** generic container - ‚≠ê Both are **semantically neutral** (no meaning) - ‚≠ê Use `
          ` for sections, `` for inline styling ### **8. Responsive CSS** - ‚≠ê **3 pillars:** fluid grids + flexible images + media queries - ‚≠ê Mobile-first uses `min-width`, desktop-first uses `max-width` - ‚≠ê **Mandatory:** `` - ‚≠ê Common breakpoints: `768px` (tablet), `1024px` (desktop) - ‚≠ê `@media (min-width: 768px) { ... }` is the **most-used syntax** ### **üßÝ One-Line Memory Hooks:** - **"Relative shifts, Absolute removes, Fixed freezes"** - **"Cover crops, Contain pads"** - **"Margin outside, Padding inside"** - **"Div breaks lines, Span flows inline"** - **"Mobile-first: start small, scale up"**

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **CSS Forms** refers to the systematic application of Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) to HTML form elements (`<form>`, `<input>`, `<textarea>`, `<select>`, `<button>`, `<label>`, `<fieldset>`, `<legend>`) in order to control their **visual presentation, spatial layout, interactive feedback, and accessibility behavior**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCNT503 - Web Programming, Module 2)**, CSS Forms encompasses three foundational pillars:

1. **Aesthetic Styling** ‚Äî colors, borders, backgrounds, typography, padding, and shadows applied to form controls.
2. **Structural Layout** ‚Äî arranging form controls using `display`, `flexbox`, and `grid` to produce responsive, multi-column, or single-column form schemas.
3. **State-Based Pseudo-Class Styling** ‚Äî using CSS pseudo-classes such as `:focus`, `:hover`, `:valid`, `:invalid`, `:checked`, `:disabled`, and `:required` to provide **dynamic user feedback** during interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to understand how CSS attribute selectors and pseudo-classes combine to deliver interactive form experiences, and how the **CSS Box Model** is applied to every form control to compute final dimensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **paper application form** at a government office counter. The form has:
- **Boxes to fill in** (input fields) ‚Äî these need to be large, clearly bordered, and easy to write in.
- **Labels** next to each box ‚Äî telling you what to write.
- **A submit button** at the bottom ‚Äî clearly visible, often colored differently to draw attention.
- **Sections grouped together** (e.g., "Personal Information", "Address Details") ‚Äî visually separated by boxes or shading.
- **Error highlights** when you fill something wrong ‚Äî a red border or marker.
- **Hover or focus effects** ‚Äî when you click on a box, the pen feels "active" because the box highlights.

In web development, **HTML** builds the paper form's structure, and **CSS** is the **graphic designer / stationery artist** who decides:
- The width and height of each box.
- The color of the button.
- What happens visually when the user clicks a field (focus).
- How errors are displayed when input is invalid.

> [!TIP]
> **Why CSS Forms Matter in KTU Exams:** Almost every web application (login pages, registration forms, search bars, checkout pages) uses CSS-styled forms. KTU examiners frequently test your ability to combine **attribute selectors**, **pseudo-classes**, and **box-model properties** to produce complete, working styled form snippets.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in CSS Forms

| Property / Concept | Standard Metric / Value |
|---|---|
| Default form font size | **16px** (browser default) |
| Recommended input padding | **8px to 12px** (vertical) / **10px to 16px** (horizontal) |
| Accessible minimum tap target | **44 √ó 44 px** (WCAG 2.1 recommendation) |
| Default `display` of `<div>` | **block** |
| Default `display` of `<span>` | **inline** |
| Default `display` of `<input>` | **inline-block** |
| Focus ring contrast requirement | **3:1** (WCAG 2.1) |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of an HTML Form

A typical HTML form is composed of the following elements, each of which is a **CSS-targetable element**:

| HTML Element | CSS Role | Common Pseudo-Classes |
|---|---|---|
| `<form>` | Outer container, layout parent | ‚Äî |
| `<label>` | Field caption, accessibility anchor | ‚Äî |
| `<input type="text">` | Single-line text capture | `:focus`, `:valid`, `:invalid`, `:disabled`, `:required` |
| `<input type="password">` | Masked text capture | `:focus`, `:valid`, `:invalid` |
| `<input type="email">` | Email with format validation | `:valid`, `:invalid` |
| `<input type="number">` | Numeric input | `:in-range`, `:out-of-range` |
| `<input type="checkbox">` | Boolean toggle | `:checked` |
| `<input type="radio">` | Mutually exclusive choice | `:checked` |
| `<input type="submit">` / `<button>` | Form submission trigger | `:hover`, `:active`, `:disabled` |
| `<textarea>` | Multi-line text capture | `:focus`, `:valid`, `:invalid` |
| `<select>` | Dropdown list | `:focus`, `:valid`, `:invalid` |
| `<fieldset>` | Logical grouping of fields | `:disabled` |
| `<legend>` | Caption for a fieldset | ‚Äî |

## 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind CSS Form Styling

### Why do we style forms?
1. **Usability** ‚Äî Users need to instantly recognize input fields, buttons, and labels.
2. **Accessibility** ‚Äî Focus indicators help keyboard users navigate forms.
3. **Validation Feedback** ‚Äî Color-coded borders (:valid/:invalid) guide users to fix errors.
4. **Branding Consistency** ‚Äî Forms must match the website's visual identity.
5. **Responsive Design** ‚Äî Forms must work on mobile, tablet, and desktop.

### How does CSS achieve this?
- **Attribute Selectors** target specific input types: `input[type="text"]`, `input[type="submit"]`.
- **Pseudo-Classes** target states: `:hover`, `:focus`, `:checked`, `:disabled`, `:valid`, `:invalid`, `:required`, `:optional`, `:read-only`, `:placeholder-shown`.
- **Box Model Properties** (`width`, `height`, `padding`, `border`, `margin`) control dimensions.
- **Display Properties** (`block`, `inline-block`, `flex`, `grid`) control layout.
- **Background and Color** provide visual feedback.
- **Transitions** smooth state changes: `transition: border-color 0.3s ease;`.

## 2.3 The CSS Box Model Applied to Forms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every form control is a **rectangular box** governed by the CSS Box Model. Understanding this is critical for KTU 14-mark questions involving form layout.

The **total width** of any form control is computed as:

$$
\text{Total Width} = \text{Width} + 2 \times \text{Padding} + 2 \times \text{Border} + 2 \times \text{Margin}
$$

Similarly, for height:

$$
\text{Total Height} = \text{Height} + 2 \times \text{Padding}_{\text{top/bottom}} + 2 \times \text{Border}_{\text{top/bottom}} + 2 \times \text{Margin}_{\text{top/bottom}}
$$

The **content area** (where typed text actually appears) is:

$$
\text{Content Area} = \text{Total Width} - 2(\text{Padding} + \text{Border})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If `box-sizing: border-box;` is set, the `width` property includes padding and border. Without it (default `content-box`), padding and border are *added* to the width, often causing unexpected overflow.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Selector Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Total box width | $\text{Width} + 2\text{Padding} + 2\text{Border} + 2\text{Margin}$ | Compute final occupied space |
| Border-box width | $\text{Width (includes padding and border)}$ | Predictable sizing |
| Attribute selector | `input[type="text"]` | Target specific input types |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî focus | `:focus` | Style when input is active |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî valid | `:valid` | Style valid form data |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî invalid | `:invalid` | Style invalid form data |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî checked | `:checked` | Style selected radio/checkbox |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî required | `:required` | Style mandatory fields |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî disabled | `:disabled` | Style inactive fields |
| Pseudo-class ‚Äî placeholder | `::placeholder` | Style hint text inside input |
| Flex form layout | `display: flex; flex-direction: column;` | Stack label and input vertically |
| Grid form layout | `display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr;` | Two-column label + input layout |
| Accessible tap target | $\text{min-height} \geq 44\text{px}$ | Mobile-friendly controls |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSS Form styling is not academic ‚Äî it powers every digital interaction:

- **E-commerce Checkout Forms** (Amazon, Flipkart) ‚Äî credit card inputs, address fields.
- **Banking Login Pages** (SBI, HDFC) ‚Äî secure password fields with focus highlights.
- **Healthcare Portals** ‚Äî patient registration forms with required-field indicators.
- **Government e-Forms** (Aadhaar, Passport) ‚Äî large input fields, validation feedback.
- **Search Bars** (Google, YouTube) ‚Äî rounded inputs with focus glow.

In KTU 2024 Scheme, students must demonstrate the ability to **build, style, and validate** such forms using HTML + CSS, often as a 14-mark lab/model question.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Styling a Complete Registration Form

Below is a **fully derived, step-by-step construction** of a CSS-styled registration form. Every transition from raw HTML to styled output is explained.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Base HTML Structure (Unstyled)

```html
<form class="reg-form">
  <label for="name">Full Name</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required>

  <label for="email">Email Address</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

  <label for="pwd">Password</label>
  <input type="password" id="pwd" name="pwd" required minlength="8">

  <label for="age">Age</label>
  <input type="number" id="age" name="age" min="18" max="100">

  <label>
    <input type="checkbox" name="terms"> I agree to the Terms
  </label>

  <input type="submit" value="Register">
</form>
```

### Step 2 ‚Äî Establish Layout Using Flexbox

**Logic:** We want labels above inputs (vertical stacking). Using `flex-direction: column` on the form container achieves this with a single rule.

```css
.reg-form {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  max-width: 400px;
  margin: 20px auto;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
}
```

**Reasoning of each property:**

- `display: flex` ‚Äî Activates flexbox layout model for the form container.
- `flex-direction: column` ‚Äî Forces children (labels and inputs) to stack vertically.
- `max-width: 400px` ‚Äî Caps form width for desktop readability.
- `margin: 20px auto` ‚Äî Centers the form horizontally on the page.
- `padding: 20px` ‚Äî Inner spacing between form border and its content.
- `border: 1px solid #ccc` ‚Äî Light gray border for visual definition.
- `border-radius: 8px` ‚Äî Rounded corners for modern aesthetics.
- `font-family` ‚Äî Establishes consistent typography.

### Step 3 ‚Äî Style Labels

**Logic:** Labels are small, bold, and spaced from their corresponding input.

```css
.reg-form label {
  margin-top: 12px;
  margin-bottom: 4px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-size: 14px;
  color: #333;
}
```

**Reasoning:**

- `margin-top: 12px` ‚Äî Vertical breathing room between previous input and new label.
- `margin-bottom: 4px` ‚Äî Tighter spacing between label and its input.
- `font-weight: bold` ‚Äî Visual hierarchy (labels are important).
- `font-size: 14px` ‚Äî Slightly smaller than input text for clarity.
- `color: #333` ‚Äî Dark gray, easier on the eyes than pure black.

### Step 4 ‚Äî Style All Text Inputs (Attribute Selector)

**Logic:** Use an attribute selector to target multiple text-based inputs in a single rule.

```css
.reg-form input[type="text"],
.reg-form input[type="email"],
.reg-form input[type="password"],
.reg-form input[type="number"] {
  width: 100%;
  padding: 10px 12px;
  border: 2px solid #ddd;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 15px;
  box-sizing: border-box;
  transition: border-color 0.3s ease, box-shadow 0.3s ease;
}
```

**Reasoning of each property:**

- `width: 100%` ‚Äî Stretches input to fill form width.
- `padding: 10px 12px` ‚Äî Generous inner space for user comfort.
- `border: 2px solid #ddd` ‚Äî Visible but neutral default border.
- `border-radius: 4px` ‚Äî Subtle rounding.
- `font-size: 15px` ‚Äî Readable input text size.
- `box-sizing: border-box` ‚Äî Ensures `width: 100%` accounts for padding and border.
- `transition` ‚Äî Smoothly animates the border color when state changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners often ask: *"Why is `box-sizing: border-box` important for form inputs?"* The answer: Without it, an input with `width: 100%; padding: 10px 12px;` will overflow its parent by 24px (12px left + 12px right), breaking the form layout.

### Step 5 ‚Äî Apply State-Based Pseudo-Classes

**Logic:** Provide visual feedback for user interaction and validation.

```css
/* Focus state ‚Äî when user clicks into the field */
.reg-form input[type="text"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="email"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="password"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="number"]:focus {
  border-color: #4A90E2;
  outline: none;
  box-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(74, 144, 226, 0.5);
}

/* Valid state ‚Äî only triggers after user interaction */
.reg-form input:valid {
  border-color: #2ecc71;
}

/* Invalid state */
.reg-form input:invalid {
  border-color: #e74c3c;
}

/* Required field indicator */
.reg-form input:required {
  background: linear-gradient(to right, #fff 95%, #e74c3c 95%);
}

/* Disabled field */
.reg-form input:disabled {
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  cursor: not-allowed;
}

/* Checked state for checkbox */
.reg-form input[type="checkbox"]:checked {
  outline: 2px solid #4A90E2;
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning of the focus rule:**

1. User clicks the input ‚Üí browser triggers `:focus` pseudo-class.
2. CSS rule `border-color: #4A90E2` changes the 2px border from gray to blue.
3. `outline: none` removes the default browser outline (we replace it with custom styling).
4. `box-shadow` creates a soft blue glow around the input.
5. The `transition` declared in Step 4 animates this change over 0.3 seconds.

### Step 6 ‚Äî Style the Submit Button

```css
.reg-form input[type="submit"] {
  margin-top: 20px;
  padding: 12px;
  background-color: #4A90E2;
  color: white;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 16px;
  font-weight: bold;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease;
}

.reg-form input[type="submit"]:hover {
  background-color: #357ABD;
}

.reg-form input[type="submit"]:active {
  background-color: #2A5F94;
}

.reg-form input[type="submit"]:disabled {
  background-color: #aaa;
  cursor: not-allowed;
}
```

**Reasoning:**

- `background-color: #4A90E2` ‚Äî Brand-consistent blue.
- `:hover` ‚Äî Darkens the button on mouseover for affordance feedback.
- `:active` ‚Äî Further darkens when clicked, simulating a "pressed" state.
- `:disabled` ‚Äî Grayed out when form is invalid or processing.

### Step 7 ‚Äî Style the Placeholder Text

```css
.reg-form input::placeholder {
  color: #999;
  font-style: italic;
  opacity: 1;
}
```

**Reasoning:** Placeholder hints (e.g., `"Enter your full name"`) appear in italicized gray. `opacity: 1` overrides Firefox's default placeholder opacity.

### Step 8 ‚Äî Placeholder-Shown Pseudo-Class (Advanced)

```css
.reg-form input:placeholder-shown {
  font-style: italic;
}
```

**Logic:** This rule applies *only* when the placeholder is currently visible (i.e., the input is empty). Once the user types, the italic style disappears.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pseudo-Class Selection Guide

| Pseudo-Class | Trigger Condition | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `:hover` | Mouse pointer is over the element | Buttons, links, interactive cards |
| `:focus` | Element is selected (clicked or tabbed to) | Inputs, textareas, selects |
| `:active` | Element is being clicked/pressed | Buttons (pressed state) |
| `:checked` | Radio or checkbox is selected | Custom toggle styling |
| `:disabled` | Element has `disabled` attribute | Grayed-out form controls |
| `:enabled` | Element does **not** have `disabled` | Default styling for active fields |
| `:required` | Element has `required` attribute | Asterisk indicators, bold labels |
| `:optional` | Element does **not** have `required` | Subtle styling for non-mandatory fields |
| `:valid` | Input passes HTML5 validation | Green border on correctly filled fields |
| `:invalid` | Input fails HTML5 validation | Red border on incorrectly filled fields |
| `:in-range` | Numeric value is within `min`/`max` | Valid age range |
| `:out-of-range` | Numeric value is outside `min`/`max` | Invalid age range |
| `:read-only` | Element has `readonly` attribute | Non-editable but selectable fields |
| `:placeholder-shown` | Placeholder text is currently visible | Conditional styling on empty inputs |

## 3.3 Layout Derivation: Two-Column Form (Grid)

For a professional **two-column form** where labels appear left and inputs appear right:

```css
.reg-form {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr;
  gap: 12px 16px;
  align-items: center;
  max-width: 600px;
  margin: 20px auto;
}

.reg-form label {
  text-align: right;
  font-weight: bold;
}

.reg-form input[type="submit"] {
  grid-column: 1 / -1;  /* Span full width */
}
```

**Derivation of grid layout:**

1. `display: grid` ‚Äî Activates CSS Grid on the form.
2. `grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr` ‚Äî Creates two columns: label (1/3 width) and input (2/3 width). The `fr` unit represents *fraction of available space*.
3. `gap: 12px 16px` ‚Äî 12px row gap, 16px column gap.
4. `align-items: center` ‚Äî Vertically centers label text with its corresponding input.
5. `grid-column: 1 / -1` ‚Äî Submit button spans from column 1 to the last column line, occupying the entire row width.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When using `grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr`, the total fraction is **1 + 2 = 3 parts**. The label gets **1/3** of the form width, and the input gets **2/3**. This is a frequently tested concept.

## 3.4 Responsive Form Derivation

To make the form work on mobile devices:

```css
@media (max-width: 600px) {
  .reg-form {
    grid-template-columns: 1fr;  /* Single column on mobile */
  }

  .reg-form label {
    text-align: left;
  }
}
```

**Logic:** Below 600px viewport width, the grid collapses to a single column, and labels align left for natural mobile reading flow.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Form Control State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unfocused
    Unfocused --> Focused: User clicks or tabs
    Focused --> Filled: User types input
    Filled --> Valid: HTML5 validation passes
    Filled --> Invalid: HTML5 validation fails
    Valid --> Focused: User edits
    Invalid --> Focused: User edits
    Focused --> Unfocused: User clicks away
    Unfocused --> Hovered: Mouse pointer enters
    Hovered --> Unfocused: Mouse pointer leaves
    Valid --> Disabled: disabled attribute added
    Invalid --> Disabled: disabled attribute added
    Disabled --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Form Layout Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need to design a form layout] --> Q1{Number of columns?}
    Q1 -->|Single column| FlexPath[Use Flexbox with flex-direction column]
    Q1 -->|Two columns labels and inputs| GridPath[Use CSS Grid with grid-template-columns]
    Q1 -->|Complex multi-section| AdvancedPath[Use CSS Grid with nested flexbox]

    FlexPath --> R1[Set display flex on form container]
    FlexPath --> R2[Add padding and margin for spacing]
    FlexPath --> R3[Apply width 100% to inputs]

    GridPath --> S1[Set display grid on form container]
    GridPath --> S2[Define column ratios 1fr 2fr]
    GridPath --> S3[Use grid-column span for full-width elements]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Box Model Decomposition of an Input Field

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Field Total Box] --> B[Margin Outer Space]
    A --> C[Border Visible Edge]
    A --> D[Padding Inner Space]
    A --> E[Content Area Typed Text]

    B --> B1[Top Margin]
    B --> B2[Right Margin]
    B --> B3[Bottom Margin]
    B --> B4[Left Margin]

    C --> C1[Border Width 2px]
    C --> C2[Border Style solid]
    C --> C3[Border Color]

    D --> D1[Top Padding]
    D --> D2[Right Padding]
    D --> D3[Bottom Padding]
    D --> D4[Left Padding]

    E --> E1[User Typed Characters]
    E --> E2[Font Size 15px]
    E --> E3[Line Height]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The total horizontal space occupied by an input field equals the sum of all four zones in the box model. This is why `box-sizing: border-box` is critical for predictable form layouts.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the CSS `:focus` pseudo-class and explain its role in form styling. Provide one example.

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `:focus` pseudo-class is a CSS selector that targets an element **at the moment it receives focus**, typically when a user clicks on it, tabs to it via keyboard, or activates it programmatically.

**Role in form styling:**

- It provides **visual feedback** to indicate which input field is currently active.
- It improves **accessibility** for keyboard-only users.
- It enhances **usability** by clearly highlighting the active field.

**Example:**

```css
input[type="text"]:focus {
  border-color: #4A90E2;
  outline: none;
  box-shadow: 0 0 5px rgba(74, 144, 226, 0.5);
}
```

This rule applies a blue border and a soft blue glow when the user clicks into a text input.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for role, 1 mark for example)**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between `:valid` and `:invalid` pseudo-classes with a suitable CSS example.

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `:valid` | `:invalid` |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Input passes HTML5 validation | Input fails HTML5 validation |
| Typical color | Green | Red |
| Default trigger | All required fields are `:invalid` until filled | Fields only become `:valid` after correct entry |
| Use case | Positive feedback for correct data | Error indication for incorrect data |

**Example:**

```css
input:valid {
  border: 2px solid #2ecc71;  /* Green border for valid input */
}

input:invalid {
  border: 2px solid #e74c3c;  /* Red border for invalid input */
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **Important Note:** Browsers may apply `:invalid` to required fields even before user interaction. To restrict validation styling to *post-interaction* states, combine with `:focus` or use JavaScript.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference table, 1 mark for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Design a complete HTML + CSS registration form with the following requirements: **[7 marks]**

1. The form must contain fields: Name, Email, Password, Age, and a Submit button.
2. Use **Flexbox** to arrange labels and inputs vertically.
3. All text inputs must have `width: 100%`, `box-sizing: border-box`, and 10px vertical padding.
4. The submit button must have a blue background, white text, and rounded corners.

**(b)** Add the following interactive state-based styling: **[7 marks]**

1. When a text input is `:focus`, change its border color to **dark blue** and add a soft box-shadow.
2. When a text input is `:valid`, change its border to **green**.
3. When a text input is `:invalid`, change its border to **red**.
4. When the submit button is `:hover`, darken its background by 20%.

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Base Structure and Flexbox Layout [7 marks]

**HTML Code:**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Registration Form</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>
  <form class="reg-form">
    <label for="name">Full Name</label>
    <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required>

    <label for="email">Email Address</label>
    <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

    <label for="pwd">Password</label>
    <input type="password" id="pwd" name="pwd" required minlength="8">

    <label for="age">Age</label>
    <input type="number" id="age" name="age" min="18" max="100" required>

    <input type="submit" value="Register">
  </form>
</body>
</html>
```

**CSS Code:**

```css
.reg-form {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  max-width: 400px;
  margin: 20px auto;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  background-color: #f9f9f9;
}

.reg-form label {
  margin-top: 12px;
  margin-bottom: 4px;
  font-weight: bold;
  font-size: 14px;
  color: #333;
}

.reg-form input[type="text"],
.reg-form input[type="email"],
.reg-form input[type="password"],
.reg-form input[type="number"] {
  width: 100%;
  padding: 10px 12px;
  border: 2px solid #ddd;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 15px;
  box-sizing: border-box;
  transition: border-color 0.3s ease, box-shadow 0.3s ease;
}

.reg-form input[type="submit"] {
  margin-top: 20px;
  padding: 12px;
  background-color: #4A90E2;
  color: white;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 16px;
  font-weight: bold;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease;
}
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**

- Correct HTML structure with 4 fields and submit button: **2 marks**
- `display: flex; flex-direction: column;` on form: **1 mark**
- Input width, padding, box-sizing: **2 marks**
- Submit button styling (blue, white text, rounded): **2 marks**

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Interactive State-Based Styling [7 marks]

```css
/* Focus state */
.reg-form input[type="text"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="email"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="password"]:focus,
.reg-form input[type="number"]:focus {
  border-color: #003366;              /* Dark blue */
  outline: none;
  box-shadow: 0 0 8px rgba(0, 51, 102, 0.4);
}

/* Valid state */
.reg-form input:valid {
  border-color: #2ecc71;              /* Green */
}

/* Invalid state */
.reg-form input:invalid {
  border-color: #e74c3c;              /* Red */
}

/* Submit button hover state */
.reg-form input[type="submit"]:hover {
  background-color: #3A78C2;          /* 20% darker than #4A90E2 */
}
```

**Derivation of darkened color (for KTU rigor):**

Original color: `rgb(74, 144, 226)` which is `#4A90E2`.

To darken by 20%, we multiply each channel by 0.8:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{new}} &= 74 \times 0.8 = 59.2 \approx 59 = \text{0x3B} \\
G_{\text{new}} &= 144 \times 0.8 = 115.2 \approx 115 = \text{0x73} \\
B_{\text{new}} &= 226 \times 0.8 = 180.8 \approx 181 = \text{0xB5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to hex:

$$
\text{Hex} = \text{0x3B73B5} \approx \text{#3A78C2}
$$

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**

- `:focus` rule with border-color and box-shadow: **2 marks**
- `:valid` rule with green border: **1.5 marks**
- `:invalid` rule with red border: **1.5 marks**
- `:hover` rule on submit button: **2 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the CSS Box Model as it applies to form controls. A password input has `width: 300px`, `padding: 12px 16px`, `border: 2px solid black`, and `margin: 10px`. Calculate: **[7 marks]**

1. Total horizontal space occupied by the input.
2. Content area width.
3. Explain what happens if `box-sizing: border-box` is applied.

**(b)** Design a **two-column form layout** using CSS Grid where labels occupy 1/3 width and inputs occupy 2/3 width. The form must include Name, Email, and a Submit button that spans the full row width. Write the complete HTML and CSS. **[7 marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Box Model Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**

- `width: 300px` (content width, default `box-sizing: content-box`)
- `padding: 12px 16px` ‚Üí vertical 12px, horizontal 16px
- `border: 2px solid black` ‚Üí 2px on all sides
- `margin: 10px` ‚Üí 10px on all sides

**1. Total horizontal space occupied:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Width} &= \text{Width} + 2(\text{Padding}_{\text{horiz}}) + 2(\text{Border}) + 2(\text{Margin}) \\
&= 300 + 2(16) + 2(2) + 2(10) \\
&= 300 + 32 + 4 + 20 \\
&= 356 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The input occupies **356px** of total horizontal space. **(2 marks)**

**2. Content area width:**

The content area is the inner space where typed characters appear. With default `box-sizing: content-box`, the declared `width` IS the content area width.

$$
\text{Content Area Width} = 300 \text{ px}
$$

**Answer:** The content area is **300px** wide. **(2 marks)**

**3. Effect of `box-sizing: border-box`:**

With `box-sizing: border-box`, the declared `width` now **includes** padding and border. The content area shrinks to accommodate them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Content Area} &= \text{Width} - 2(\text{Padding}) - 2(\text{Border}) \\
&= 300 - 32 - 4 \\
&= 264 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total Width (with margin)} = 300 + 2(10) = 320 \text{ px}
$$

**Explanation:** With `border-box`, the input's visible box (border edge) measures 300px wide, and the content area is reduced to 264px. The total space including margin is 320px. This makes layout calculations far more predictable. **(3 marks)**

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Two-Column Grid Layout [7 marks]

**HTML Code:**

```html
<form class="grid-form">
  <label for="gname">Name:</label>
  <input type="text" id="gname" name="gname" required>

  <label for="gemail">Email:</label>
  <input type="email" id="gemail" name="gemail" required>

  <input type="submit" value="Submit" class="full-width">
</form>
```

**CSS Code:**

```css
.grid-form {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr;  /* 1/3 label, 2/3 input */
  gap: 12px 16px;
  max-width: 600px;
  margin: 20px auto;
  padding: 20px;
  align-items: center;
}

.grid-form label {
  text-align: right;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #333;
}

.grid-form input[type="text"],
.grid-form input[type="email"] {
  padding: 10px;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 15px;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

.grid-form .full-width {
  grid-column: 1 / -1;  /* Span from column 1 to last column line */
  padding: 12px;
  background-color: #4A90E2;
  color: white;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 16px;
  cursor: pointer;
}
```

**Derivation of column ratio:**

- `grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr`
- Total fractional units: $1 + 2 = 3$
- Label column width: $\frac{1}{3} \times 600\text{px (max-width)} = 200\text{px}$
- Input column width: $\frac{2}{3} \times 600\text{px} = 400\text{px}$

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**

- Correct HTML structure with labels and inputs: **2 marks**
- `display: grid` with `1fr 2fr` column definition: **2 marks**
- `align-items: center` and gap property: **1 mark**
- Submit button with `grid-column: 1 / -1` spanning full width: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**

1. **Forgetting `box-sizing: border-box` on inputs:** When you set `width: 100%` with padding, the input will overflow its parent by twice the horizontal padding value. Examiners explicitly deduct 1‚Äì2 marks for this. Always include `box-sizing: border-box;` for predictable form widths.

2. **Confusing `:valid` with `:checked`:** `:valid` is for HTML5 form validation (e.g., correct email format), while `:checked` is for radio buttons and checkboxes. Mixing them up is a frequent error.

3. **Using `outline: none` without replacement:** Removing the default focus outline is an accessibility violation. Examiners may deduct marks if you don't provide a custom focus indicator (e.g., `border-color` change or `box-shadow`).

4. **Spelling pseudo-classes incorrectly:** `:focus` (not `:focuss`), `:checked` (not `:check`), `:valid` (not `:validate`). Case matters in CSS.

5. **Forgetting the `type` attribute in attribute selectors:** Writing `input.text` instead of `input[type="text"]` will not work and is a common typo. Attribute values must be in quotes.

6. **Not combining `:focus` with `:invalid` correctly:** If you only style `:invalid`, the browser marks the field as red *before* the user has interacted. Use `:focus:invalid` or `:not(:placeholder-shown):invalid` to validate only after typing.

7. **Submitting CSS without the `transition` property:** For interactive states, smooth transitions are considered best practice. Examiners may award bonus marks for including `transition` declarations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for CSS Forms:**

- **Form elements are CSS-targetable:** `<form>`, `<label>`, `<input>`, `<textarea>`, `<select>`, `<button>`, `<fieldset>`, `<legend>`.
- **Attribute selectors** use the pattern `element[attribute="value"]`, e.g., `input[type="text"]`.
- **Pseudo-classes** are appended with a single colon `:`, e.g., `:hover`, `:focus`, `:valid`.
- **Pseudo-elements** use double colons `::`, e.g., `::placeholder`, `::first-letter`.
- **The Box Model** governs every form control: `Total = Width + 2√óPadding + 2√óBorder + 2√óMargin`.
- **`box-sizing: border-box`** makes `width` include padding and border ‚Äî **always use it for form inputs**.
- **`:focus`** triggers when an input is active (clicked or tabbed to) ‚Äî use it for visual feedback.
- **`:valid` / `:invalid`** reflect HTML5 validation state ‚Äî use them for green/red borders.
- **`:checked`** applies only to radio buttons and checkboxes.
- **`:required` and `:optional`** target fields based on the `required` attribute.
- **`:disabled`** styles inactive form controls (grayed out, `cursor: not-allowed`).
- **`::placeholder`** styles hint text inside inputs (italic, gray, smaller font).
- **Flexbox** (`display: flex; flex-direction: column;`) is ideal for single-column vertical form layouts.
- **CSS Grid** (`display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr;`) is ideal for two-column label + input layouts.
- **`grid-column: 1 / -1`** makes an element span the full width of a grid row.
- **Transitions** (`transition: border-color 0.3s ease;`) create smooth state changes ‚Äî a KTU best-practice requirement.
- **Accessibility rule:** Never remove the focus outline without providing a custom alternative.
- **Responsive forms:** Use `@media (max-width: 600px)` to switch grid layouts to single-column on mobile.
- **Total width formula** (default `content-box`): $W + 2P_{\text{horiz}} + 2B + 2M$.
- **Total width formula** (`border-box`): The declared `width` already includes padding and border.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Media Port and Media Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Media Port and Media Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS3)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Web Programming syllabus, a **Media Query** is a CSS3 module that allows content rendering to adapt dynamically to a wide range of device characteristics ‚Äî such as screen size, resolution, orientation, and aspect ratio ‚Äî without requiring changes to the underlying HTML markup. A **Media Port**, in this scope, refers to the *logical channel or "port"* through which a stylesheet communicates with a particular output device type (e.g., `screen`, `print`, `speech`, `all`).

Mathematically expressed, a media query is a logical expression evaluated by the browser at render time:

$$
\text{Result} = f(\text{MediaType}) \;\land\; g(\text{MediaFeatures})
$$

Where `f` checks the device category and `g` evaluates boolean feature constraints such as width, height, or color depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Media queries form the backbone of **Responsive Web Design (RWD)**. Mastering the syntax, breakpoints, and media feature expressions is mandatory for the ESE (End Semester Evaluation) and Lab examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart water tap** in a modern kitchen. The tap has a sensor that detects:
- The size of the vessel placed under it (device width)
- The angle at which the vessel is tilted (orientation)
- Whether the user wants a gentle rinse or a powerful jet (resolution/dpi)

Based on these detected conditions, the tap *automatically* adjusts its water flow. You do not change the tap's physical structure; you only configure its conditional behavior.

**Media queries work identically:**
- The **vessel** = the user's device (mobile, tablet, laptop, printer, screen reader)
- The **sensor** = browser's viewport detection engine
- The **adjustment** = the alternative CSS rules applied

The "**Media Port**" is the dedicated *output channel* (screen, print, etc.) through which these rules flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Media queries let a single webpage *look great* on a 320px smartphone, a 1920px desktop monitor, and a printed page ‚Äî *using the same HTML file* but different CSS rules triggered by the device.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Media Queries

The following **standardized units** are used by the W3C and are part of the KTU expected knowledge:

- **px** (CSS pixels) ‚Äî primary unit for breakpoints
- **em / rem** ‚Äî relative units (1em = inherited font size)
- **vw / vh** ‚Äî 1% of viewport width / height
- **dpi / dpcm / dppx** ‚Äî resolution metrics
- **landscape / portrait** ‚Äî orientation descriptors

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the *difference between `em` and `rem`*. Memorize: `em` is **relative to the parent**, while `rem` is **relative to the root (`<html>`) element**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Media Query ‚Äî Operational Logic

A complete media query is built from **three logical layers**, evaluated top-to-bottom by the browser's rendering engine:

1. **Media Type (Optional in CSS3)** ‚Äî Identifies the broad class of device.
2. **Media Features (Boolean Expressions)** ‚Äî Constraints on device properties.
3. **Logical Operators** ‚Äî `and`, `not`, `only`, comma (`,` acting as OR).

#### 2.1.1 Layer 1 ‚Äî Media Types

| Media Type | Description | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `all` | Matches every device | Default fallback |
| `screen` | Color computer screens | Desktops, mobiles, tablets |
| `print` | Print preview / printed pages | Generating printer-friendly styles |
| `speech` | Screen readers | Accessibility-focused design |
| `tty` | Teletypes / fixed-pitch text | Rare legacy devices |
| `tv` | Television-type devices | Smart TVs (largely deprecated) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per W3C CSS3 specification, omitting the media type defaults to `all`. The `only` keyword hides the rule from older browsers that do not support media queries.

#### 2.1.2 Layer 2 ‚Äî Media Features (Boolean Tests)

Each media feature returns either **true** or **false** when evaluated against the current device. Features that accept a **prefix** (`min-` or `max-`) accept a value and are called *range features*; those without prefix are *discrete features*.

| Feature | Evaluates | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| `width` | Viewport width | $\geq 0$ px |
| `height` | Viewport height | $\geq 0$ px |
| `min-width` | Lower bound on viewport width | $\geq 0$ px |
| `max-width` | Upper bound on viewport width | $\geq 0$ px |
| `orientation` | Device orientation | `portrait` $\vert$ `landscape` |
| `aspect-ratio` | Width-to-height ratio | e.g., `16/9` |
| `resolution` | Pixel density | e.g., `192dpi` |
| `prefers-color-scheme` | User's theme preference | `light` $\vert$ `dark` |
| `hover` | Hover capability | `hover` $\vert$ `none` |
| `pointer` | Input accuracy | `fine` $\vert$ `coarse` $\vert$ `none` |

#### 2.1.3 Layer 3 ‚Äî Logical Operators

The combination of media type and features is governed by logical operators:

- **`and`** ‚Äî Logical conjunction (both must be true)
- **`not`** ‚Äî Logical negation (inverts the *entire* query)
- **`,` (comma)** ‚Äî Logical disjunction (OR between queries)
- **`only`** ‚Äî Backward-compatibility filter for legacy browsers

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula / Syntax Sheet

The "formula" in media queries is essentially the **canonical syntax template**. Memorize the variants below:

| # | Pattern | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `@media screen` | Applies to all screens (no feature constraint) |
| 2 | `@media (max-width: 600px)` | Applies when viewport $\leq 600$px |
| 3 | `@media (min-width: 768px)` | Applies when viewport $\geq 768$px |
| 4 | `@media screen and (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px)` | Tablets in landscape range |
| 5 | `@media not screen and (min-width: 1024px)` | Excludes large screens |
| 6 | `@media (orientation: portrait)` | Devices held vertically |
| 7 | `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` | Dark-mode aware design |
| 8 | `@media screen, print` | Either screens or printers (OR) |
| 9 | `@media only screen and (min-resolution: 2dppx)` | Retina/high-DPI screens |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** The keyword `not` negates the *entire query*, not just a single feature. For example, `@media not screen and (color)` is parsed as `@media not (screen and (color))`, *not* `@media (not screen) and (color)`.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Media queries are foundational to:

- **Mobile-First Indexing (Google SEO, 2024+)** ‚Äî Google's crawler primarily uses the mobile version of a site.
- **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)** ‚Äî adaptive UIs across form factors.
- **IoT Dashboards** ‚Äî single codebase serving watches, dashboards, and wall displays.
- **Accessibility Engineering** ‚Äî `prefers-reduced-motion`, `prefers-contrast` improve WCAG 2.2 compliance.

### 2.4 The Two Methods of Invocation

Media queries can be embedded in two ways:

**Method A ‚Äî Inline within a stylesheet (preferred for KTU labs):**

```css
/* base.css */
body { background: white; }

@media (max-width: 600px) {
  body { background: lightyellow; }
}
```

**Method B ‚Äî Linked via the `<link>` tag's `media` attribute (early-binding port selection):**

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="print.css" media="print">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="mobile.css" media="screen and (max-width: 600px)">
```

> [!TIP]
> The second method is the literal interpretation of a "**Media Port**" ‚Äî each `<link>` is a *dedicated port* routing specific stylesheet traffic to specific device categories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Constructing a Three-Tier Responsive Breakpoint System

A standard **mobile-first** responsive layout uses three breakpoints corresponding to common device classes. The derivation below shows how to compute and apply these breakpoints.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify device class boundaries**

The W3C and common UX research define the following canonical viewport ranges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mobile}   &: \quad 0 \le w \le 600 \text{ px} \\
\text{Tablet}   &: \quad 601 \le w \le 1024 \text{ px} \\
\text{Desktop}  &: \quad w \ge 1025 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Translate these into media-query range expressions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mobile rule}  &: \quad \texttt{@media (max-width: 600px)} \\
\text{Tablet rule}  &: \quad \texttt{@media (min-width: 601px) and (max-width: 1024px)} \\
\text{Desktop rule} &: \quad \texttt{@media (min-width: 1025px)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each inequality above is the *mathematical equivalent* of the corresponding CSS media feature expression.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Verify boundary behavior (no overlap, no gap)**

The overlap check:

$$
600 < 601 \quad \text{‚úì (no overlap)}
$$

The gap check:

$$
1024 < 1025 \quad \text{‚úì (no gap)}
$$

Both checks pass; the rules are **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive** over the positive integer domain.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compose the final CSS**

```css
/* Mobile-first base styles (apply to all viewports) */
.container { width: 100%; padding: 10px; }

@media (min-width: 601px) and (max-width: 1024px) {
  /* Tablet overrides */
  .container { width: 90%; padding: 20px; }
}

@media (min-width: 1025px) {
  /* Desktop overrides */
  .container { width: 75%; margin: 0 auto; padding: 30px; }
}
```

**Step 5 ‚Äî Step-by-step logical evaluation by the browser**

When the browser renders a page on a 1366px laptop, the evaluation sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Is } w \le 600?    &\to 1366 \le 600 \; \Rightarrow\; \text{False} \\
\text{Is } 601 \le w \le 1024?  &\to 601 \le 1366 \le 1024 \; \Rightarrow\; \text{False} \\
\text{Is } w \ge 1025?   &\to 1366 \ge 1025 \; \Rightarrow\; \text{True} \\
\therefore \text{Desktop rule is applied.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping KTU Frequently Tested Cases

| Logical Expression | Spoken Form | True for `w = 800` ? | True for `w = 500` ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `(max-width: 600px)` | width at most 600 | **False** | **True** |
| `(min-width: 600px)` | width at least 600 | **True** | **False** |
| `(min-width: 600px) and (max-width: 1024px)` | width in [600, 1024] | **True** | **False** |
| `(orientation: landscape)` | landscape only | depends on h | depends on h |
| `not (max-width: 600px)` | NOT at most 600 | **True** | **False** |
| `(min-resolution: 2dppx)` | retina or better | depends on device | depends on device |

> [!NOTE]
> The comma operator `,` between two queries is equivalent to a logical OR. For example, `@media screen, print` matches when the device is *either* a screen *or* a printer.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Choosing a Print Stylesheet Width

**Problem:** Design a print stylesheet that constrains content to a printable width of 8.5 inches at 96 dpi, with a 0.5-inch margin on each side. What media query rule applies?

**Step-by-step conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ inch at 96 dpi} &= 96 \text{ CSS pixels} \\
8.5 \text{ inches}        &= 8.5 \times 96 = 816 \text{ pixels} \\
0.5 \text{ inch margin}   &= 0.5 \times 96 = 48 \text{ pixels} \\
\text{Content width}      &= 816 - (2 \times 48) = 720 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting media query:**

```css
@media print and (min-width: 720px) {
  body { max-width: 720px; margin: 0 auto; }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> This kind of arithmetic (inches $\to$ px conversion using dpi) is a *favourite KTU Part-A question* and often appears as a 3-mark problem.

### 3.4 The Truth Table for the `not` Operator

The `not` keyword inverts the entire media query. A truth table is a definitive way to reason about it:

| Original Query | `not` Query | True When Original Is | True When `not` Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| `(max-width: 600px)` | `not (max-width: 600px)` | $w \le 600$ | $w > 600$ |
| `screen and (color)` | `not screen and (color)` | screen + color | anything but (screen + color) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** `not` binds to the *whole query*, not the *media type alone*. Use parentheses explicitly to disambiguate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Browser Routes Stylesheets via Media Ports

The diagram below illustrates the runtime flow of media-query evaluation inside a browser's layout engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HTML Document Parsed] --> B[Collect LINK and STYLE Nodes]
    B --> C{For each stylesheet}
    C --> D[Read media attribute or at-media rule]
    D --> E[Identify Media Type Port]
    E --> F[Evaluate Media Feature Constraints]
    F --> G{All conditions true?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply rules to CSSOM]
    G -- No  --> I[Skip ruleset]
    H --> J[Build Render Tree]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Paint to viewport]
    K --> L[Output to Media Port: Screen, Print, or Speech]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for a Mobile-First Responsive Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Read viewport width w] --> Q1{w less than 600 px}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Apply Mobile Rules]
    Q1 -- No  --> Q2{w between 601 and 1024 px inclusive}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Apply Tablet Rules]
    Q2 -- No  --> Q3{w greater than 1024 px}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Apply Desktop Rules]
    R1 --> END[Render Final Page]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

### 4.3 Logical Operator Precedence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[comma OR] --> B[and]
    B --> C[not]
    C --> D[Media Type]
    D --> E[Media Features in parens]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This mirrors the W3C CSS3 Conditional Rules Module grammar. The operators are evaluated in a left-associative manner from `,` (lowest precedence) down to the media feature expression (highest precedence).

### 4.4 Use-Case Architecture: Dark Mode and Reduced Motion Ports

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Opens Page] --> S[Browser Detects System Preferences]
    S --> C1{prefers color scheme dark?}
    C1 -- Yes --> P1[Activate Dark Mode CSS Port]
    C1 -- No  --> P2[Use Light Mode CSS Port]
    S --> C2{prefers reduced motion reduce?}
    C2 -- Yes --> P3[Disable Animations]
    C2 -- No  --> P4[Allow Animations]
    P1 --> OUT[Render Page]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
```

### 4.5 Comparison Schematic: `<link media>` vs. `@media` Inside CSS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Method A: link media attribute] --> B1[HTML Layer]
    B1 --> C1[Browser requests CSS only if media matches]
    A2[Method B: at-media block] --> B2[CSS Layer]
    B2 --> C2[Browser always downloads CSS, applies selectively]
    C1 --> D[Final Rendered Page]
    C2 --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Insight:** Method A (`<link media="...">`) saves bandwidth because the browser skips the HTTP request entirely when the port does not match. Method B downloads the file but discards unmatched rules. KTU values awareness of this trade-off in viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Define a CSS media query. List any four commonly used media features with their purpose.*

**Model Answer:**

A **CSS media query** is a conditional expression introduced in CSS3 that allows the application of CSS rules based on the characteristics of the device rendering the document, such as its width, height, resolution, or orientation, without modifying the underlying HTML structure.

Four commonly used media features:

| # | Media Feature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `min-width` | Targets devices with viewport width greater than or equal to a specified value |
| 2 | `max-width` | Targets devices with viewport width less than or equal to a specified value |
| 3 | `orientation` | Matches `portrait` or `landscape` device orientation |
| 4 | `resolution` | Matches screens of a specific pixel density (e.g., `192dpi`) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Explain the difference between the logical operators `,` (comma) and `and` in a CSS media query. Provide one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

The **comma operator** in a media query acts as a logical **OR**, evaluating to true if *any* of the listed queries match. The **and** operator acts as a logical **AND**, requiring *all* combined conditions to be true simultaneously.

**Example for `,` (OR):**

```css
/* Applies if device is screen OR print */
@media screen, print {
  body { font-family: serif; }
}
```

**Example for `and` (AND):**

```css
/* Applies only if width is at least 768px AND device is a screen */
@media screen and (min-width: 768px) {
  .nav { display: flex; }
}
```

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the syntax of a CSS media query in detail. Discuss the role of media types, media features, and logical operators with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete CSS code segment to create a mobile-first responsive webpage with the following specifications: **(7 marks)**

- The page has a `header`, `main`, and `footer`.
- On screens **less than 600 px**, the layout is single-column and the header background is lightblue.
- On screens **between 601 px and 1024 px**, the layout becomes two-column (`main` and `footer` side by side) and the header background is lightgreen.
- On screens **greater than 1024 px**, all three sections are side by side in three columns and the header background is lightcoral.
- The page should also have a separate print stylesheet (linked via `<link>`) that hides the `footer`.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Syntax of a CSS media query ‚Äî Detailed Explanation**

The formal grammar of a media query in CSS3 is:

```css
@media [not | only]? <media-type> [and (<media-feature> [: <value>])?]+ {
  /* CSS rules */
}
```

Components:

- **`@media`** ‚Äî the at-rule that introduces a conditional block.
- **`<media-type>`** ‚Äî optional in CSS3; values include `all`, `screen`, `print`, `speech`.
- **`<media-feature>`** ‚Äî a boolean test such as `min-width`, `orientation`, `aspect-ratio`, `hover`, `prefers-color-scheme`.
- **Logical operators** ‚Äî `and`, `not`, `only`, and `,` (OR).

**Examples:**

```css
/* Type + Feature + AND */
@media screen and (min-width: 768px) { ... }

/* NOT */
@media not print { ... }

/* OR via comma */
@media (max-width: 600px), (orientation: landscape) { ... }
```

(7 marks distributed: 2 for syntax, 2 for media types, 2 for features, 1 for operators)

**(b) Mobile-First Responsive CSS Code**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Responsive Demo</title>

  <!-- Main responsive stylesheet (all devices) -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="main.css">

  <!-- Print-only port: hides footer when printing -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="print.css" media="print">
</head>
<body>
  <header>Header</header>
  <main>Main Content</main>
  <footer>Footer</footer>
</body>
</html>
```

**`main.css` (mobile-first base + progressive enhancements):**

```css
/* ===== Base mobile styles (<= 600 px) ===== */
body { margin: 0; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; }
header, main, footer {
  width: 100%;
  padding: 15px;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}
header { background: lightblue; }
main   { background: #f9f9f9; }
footer { background: #eaeaea; }

/* ===== Tablet styles (601 px - 1024 px) ===== */
@media (min-width: 601px) and (max-width: 1024px) {
  header { background: lightgreen; }
  main, footer {
    display: inline-block;
    width: 50%;
  }
}

/* ===== Desktop styles (> 1024 px) ===== */
@media (min-width: 1025px) {
  header { background: lightcoral; }
  header, main, footer {
    display: inline-block;
    width: 33.33%;
  }
}
```

**`print.css` (print port ‚Äî hidden footer):**

```css
footer { display: none; }
header, main { color: black; background: white; }
```

**Explanation of the breakpoint logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mobile}  &: w \le 600  \Rightarrow \text{one column, lightblue} \\
\text{Tablet}  &: 601 \le w \le 1024 \Rightarrow \text{two columns, lightgreen} \\
\text{Desktop} &: w \ge 1025 \Rightarrow \text{three columns, lightcoral}
\end{aligned}
$$

The print stylesheet is *only* loaded by browsers whose detected media port is `print`, satisfying the "Media Port" routing concept.

(7 marks distributed: 2 for HTML structure, 2 for mobile CSS, 2 for media queries, 1 for print port)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is Responsive Web Design? Explain the role of the viewport meta tag and the CSS `box-sizing` property in achieving a responsive layout. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a stylesheet for a webpage containing a navigation bar with 4 links. Apply media queries to satisfy: **(7 marks)**

- On screens **less than 480 px**: navigation links stack vertically, font size is 14 px.
- On screens **between 481 px and 768 px**: links are displayed inline with a font size of 16 px and a 10 px horizontal gap.
- On screens **greater than 768 px**: links are inline, centered on the page, with a font size of 18 px and a 20 px horizontal gap.
- Use the `prefers-color-scheme: dark` media feature to invert background and text colors when the user has dark mode enabled.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Responsive Web Design, viewport meta tag, and box-sizing**

**Responsive Web Design (RWD)** is an approach by Ethan Marcotte (concept formalized 2010) that uses fluid grids, flexible images, and CSS media queries to make a single HTML document adapt to a range of devices.

**Viewport meta tag:**

```html
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
```

Without this tag, mobile browsers render the page at a fixed 980 px virtual width and then scale it down, breaking media queries that use `max-width`. The `width=device-width` directive forces the viewport to match the actual device width in CSS pixels, enabling `min-width` and `max-width` media features to behave correctly.

**`box-sizing` property:**

By default, CSS uses `box-sizing: content-box`, meaning `width` excludes padding and border. This causes elements with `width: 100%` plus any padding to overflow the parent. Setting `box-sizing: border-box` makes the declared `width` include padding and border, which is critical for predictable responsive layouts.

```css
* { box-sizing: border-box; }
```

(7 marks distributed: 2 for RWD definition, 2 for viewport meta, 3 for box-sizing)

**(b) Responsive Navigation Bar Stylesheet**

```css
/* Base (mobile-first) styles */
nav { background: white; color: black; }
nav a {
  display: block;          /* stack vertically on mobile */
  padding: 8px;
  font-size: 14px;
  text-decoration: none;
  color: inherit;
}

/* Tablet range: 481px - 768px */
@media (min-width: 481px) and (max-width: 768px) {
  nav a {
    display: inline-block;
    font-size: 16px;
    margin-right: 10px;
  }
}

/* Desktop range: > 768px */
@media (min-width: 769px) {
  nav { text-align: center; }
  nav a {
    display: inline-block;
    font-size: 18px;
    margin: 0 20px;
  }
}

/* Dark mode override (applies to any of the above) */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  nav { background: #111; color: #eee; }
  nav a { color: #eee; }
}
```

**Breakpoint analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mobile: }  & w \le 480 \Rightarrow \text{vertical stack, 14 px} \\
\text{Tablet: }  & 481 \le w \le 768 \Rightarrow \text{inline, 16 px, 10 px gap} \\
\text{Desktop: } & w \ge 769 \Rightarrow \text{inline, centered, 18 px, 20 px gap} \\
\text{Dark: }    & \text{user prefers dark } \Rightarrow \text{inverted color scheme}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 for base CSS, 2 for two media queries, 1 for desktop, 2 for dark mode)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the viewport meta tag** ‚Äî half the responsive design fails on real devices; lose 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Using `min-width` for mobile** ‚Äî this is the *desktop-first* anti-pattern. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly prefers **mobile-first** approaches.
> 3. **Overlapping ranges** ‚Äî students often write both `max-width: 768` and `min-width: 769`, forgetting to verify continuity; check that $768 < 769$ and no rules collide.
> 4. **Confusing `,` with `;`** ‚Äî comma in a media query is OR; semicolon ends a CSS declaration. Mixing them is the #1 cause of compiler errors.
> 5. **Not testing print** ‚Äî questions often include a `print` port requirement; missing it loses 2‚Äì3 marks outright.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before the exam):**

- A **media query** is a CSS3 conditional rule of the form `@media <type> and (<feature>: <value>) { ... }`.
- A **media port** is the conceptual channel (screen, print, speech) through which a stylesheet reaches a particular device class.
- The four **media types** to remember: `all`, `screen`, `print`, `speech`.
- The five **logical operators**: `and`, `not`, `only`, `,` (OR), and the implicit type default of `all`.
- **Mobile-first breakpoints** are written with `min-width` queries; **desktop-first** uses `max-width`.
- Canonical KTU breakpoint set: **600 px** (mobile/tablet), **1024 px** (tablet/desktop). Always use the form `min-width: 601px` and `max-width: 1024px` for the tablet range to avoid overlap.
- The **`viewport` meta tag** is mandatory in the `<head>` of any responsive HTML page.
- **`box-sizing: border-box`** should be applied globally to avoid width-overflow bugs.
- The keyword **`not`** negates the *entire query*, not the type alone ‚Äî use parentheses for clarity.
- **Print stylesheets** are typically linked via `<link rel="stylesheet" media="print">` to enable bandwidth savings through the dedicated media port.
- **Resolution** is expressed in `dpi`, `dpcm`, or `dppx` (1dppx = 96dpi).
- **Accessibility media features** (`prefers-color-scheme`, `prefers-reduced-motion`, `prefers-contrast`) are examinable and represent 2024-scheme emphasis.
- The **performance trade-off** between `<link media="...">` (no download) and inline `@media` (always downloaded) is a frequent viva question.
- Always **test** at the boundary widths (599, 600, 601, 1023, 1024, 1025 px) to catch off-by-one errors.
- Remember the **truth table for `not`**: `not (max-width: 600px)` is true for all widths *greater* than 600 px.
- For **dark mode**, use `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { ... }` ‚Äî it is widely supported in 2024 browsers.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** "**Type-AND-Feature, Comma=OR, Not=Whole, Only=Hides, Mobile-First = min-width.**" Memorize this one-liner and you can reconstruct 90% of the syntax on exam day.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to JavaScript: Introduction to Scripting, Programming Fundamentals of JavaScript, Obtaining User Input with Prompt Dialogs, Arithmetic, Decision Making, Control Statements, Functions, Arrays, Classes
Objects - Document Object Model (DOM) - Form Processing
topics.content:
