This is a **critical KTU exam point**. ### 2.3.1 String Constraints $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Keyword} & \textbf{Meaning} \\ \hline \texttt{minLength} & \text{Minimum number of characters} \\ \texttt{maxLength} & \text{Maximum number of characters} \\ \texttt{pattern} & \text{ECMA-262 regular expression the string must fully match} \\ \texttt{format} & \text{Named format like \texttt{email}, \texttt{date}, \texttt{uri}, \texttt{uuid}} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ### 2.3.2 Numeric Constraints $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Keyword} & \textbf{Meaning} \\ \hline \texttt{minimum} & \text{Value must be } \geq \texttt{minimum} \\ \texttt{exclusiveMinimum} & \text{Value must be } > \texttt{exclusiveMinimum} \\ \texttt{maximum} & \text{Value must be } \leq \texttt{maximum} \\ \texttt{exclusiveMaximum} & \text{Value must be } < \texttt{exclusiveMaximum} \\ \texttt{multipleOf} & \text{Value must be an integer multiple of this number} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ### 2.3.3 Object Constraints $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Keyword} & \textbf{Meaning} \\ \hline \texttt{properties} & \text{Map of property name to its own schema} \\ \texttt{required} & \text{Array of property names that must be present} \\ \texttt{additionalProperties} & \text{If \texttt{false}, forbids any property not listed in \texttt{properties}} \\ \texttt{minProperties} & \text{Minimum number of properties} \\ \texttt{maxProperties} & \text{Maximum number of properties} \\ \texttt{patternProperties} & \text{Properties whose names match a regex} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ### 2.3.4 Array Constraints $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Keyword} & \textbf{Meaning} \\ \hline \texttt{items} & \text{Schema applied to every element (uniform arrays)} \\ \texttt{minItems} & \text{Minimum array length} \\ \texttt{maxItems} & \text{Maximum array length} \\ \texttt{uniqueItems} & \text{If \texttt{true}, all elements must be distinct} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ## 2.4 Composition Keywords (Building Complex Schemas) > [!NOTE] > These keywords are heavily tested in KTU 14-mark questions because they test *logical reasoning*. - **`allOf`** ‚Äî Instance must validate against **every** listed schema (logical AND). - **`anyOf`** ‚Äî Instance must validate against **at least one** schema (logical OR). - **`oneOf`** ‚Äî Instance must validate against **exactly one** schema (XOR). - **`not`** ‚Äî Instance must **fail** the listed schema (logical NOT). ## 2.5 The `$ref` Keyword (Reusability) > [!IMPORTANT] > `$ref` allows a schema to **reference** another schema, enabling DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) design. The reference value is a JSON Pointer or a URI. ```json { "definitions": { "address": { "type": "object", "properties": { "street": { "type": "string" }, "city": { "type": "string" } } } }, "properties": { "billing": { "$ref": "#/definitions/address" }, "shipping": { "$ref": "#/definitions/address" } } } ``` `#/definitions/address` is a **JSON Pointer** ‚Äî the `#` denotes the root, then path segments. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use Case | |---|---| | **REST API Design** | OpenAPI 3.1 uses JSON Schema to define request/response bodies (Swagger UI auto-generates docs). | | **MongoDB** | Collection-level `$jsonSchema` validator enforces document structure. | | **Form Validation** | Libraries like `react-jsonschema-form` auto-generate HTML forms from a schema. | | **Message Queues** | Apache Kafka / RabbitMQ validate JSON payloads against registered schemas. | | **Configuration Files** | Tools like `ajv-cli` validate `package.json`, `tsconfig.json` against community schemas. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix In a *formal* mathematical sense, JSON Schema validation can be modelled as a **constraint satisfaction problem (CSP)**. Let us derive the formal validation logic, then build a complete schema from scratch. ## 3.1 The Formal Validation Function Let $S$ be a schema (a JSON object) and $I$ be an instance (the data to validate). Define the validation function: $$ V(S, I) \rightarrow \{\texttt{valid}, \texttt{invalid}\} $$ **Step 1 ‚Äî Type Check.** The first gate is the type constraint. If the schema declares `type: "integer"`, the instance must satisfy: $$ V_{\text{type}}(S, I) = \begin{cases} \texttt{valid} & \text{if } I \text{ matches } S.\text{type} \\ \texttt{invalid} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Constraint Check.** For each non-type keyword $k$ in $S$ (e.g., `minimum`, `maxLength`, `pattern`), define a predicate $C_k(I)$ that returns `true` if the constraint is satisfied. For example: $$ C_{\texttt{minimum}}(I) = (I \geq S.\texttt{minimum}) $$ $$ C_{\texttt{maxLength}}(I) = (\text{len}(I) \leq S.\texttt{maxLength}) $$ $$ C_{\texttt{pattern}}(I) = (I.\text{match}(S.\texttt{pattern}) \neq \texttt{null}) $$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Aggregation.** The overall validation is the logical AND of all constraint predicates: $$ V(S, I) = V_{\text{type}}(S, I) \;\land\; \bigwedge_{k \in \text{keywords}(S)} C_k(I) $$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Recursive Descent.** For complex types, recursion is used: $$ V_{\texttt{object}}(S, I) = \bigwedge_{p \in S.\texttt{properties}} V(S[p], I[p]) \;\land\; \bigwedge_{r \in S.\texttt{required}} (r \in \text{keys}(I)) $$ $$ V_{\texttt{array}}(S, I) = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{|I|-1} V(S.\texttt{items}, I[i]) $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Worked Example: Building a Student Schema Let us derive a complete JSON Schema for a `Student` object by reasoning through the requirements one by one. ### Step 1: Define the outermost container The student is an object. We start with the **type** keyword, the foundation of all type validation. ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "Student", "type": "object" } ``` ### Step 2: Identify properties Requirements: `id` (integer, required), `name` (string, min 2 chars), `email` (string, email format), `age` (integer, 18‚Äì100), `courses` (array of strings, min 1 item). ```json "properties": { "id": { "type": "integer" }, "name": { "type": "string" }, "email": { "type": "string" }, "age": { "type": "integer" }, "courses": { "type": "array" } } ``` ### Step 3: Add constraints per property Now we expand each property with its sub-keywords. ```json "id": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 }, "name": { "type": "string", "minLength": 2, "maxLength": 50 }, "email": { "type": "string", "format": "email" }, "age": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 18, "maximum": 100 }, "courses": { "type": "array", "minItems": 1, "items": { "type": "string" }, "uniqueItems": true } ``` ### Step 4: Enforce mandatory fields Without `required`, missing fields pass silently. We add it: ```json "required": ["id", "name", "email", "age"] ``` ### Step 5: Forbid unknown fields (security hardening) To prevent accidental data leakage, we close the door on extra properties. ```json "additionalProperties": false ``` ### Step 6: The final assembled schema ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "Student", "description": "Schema for a university student record", "type": "object", "properties": { "id": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 }, "name": { "type": "string", "minLength": 2, "maxLength": 50 }, "email": { "type": "string", "format": "email" }, "age": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 18, "maximum": 100 }, "courses": { "type": "array", "minItems": 1, "items": { "type": "string" }, "uniqueItems": true } }, "required": ["id", "name", "email", "age"], "additionalProperties": false } ``` ### Step 7: Test against an instance **Valid instance:** ```json { "id": 101, "name": "Ananya", "email": "[email protected]", "age": 21, "courses": ["CS301", "CS405"] } ``` Running $V(S, I)$: - $V_{\text{type}}$: object ‚úì - `id`: integer ‚â• 1 ‚Üí 101 ‚â• 1 ‚úì - `name`: string, length 6, between 2 and 50 ‚úì - `email`: matches `email` format ‚úì - `age`: 21, in [18, 100] ‚úì - `courses`: array of length 2 ‚â• 1, items are strings, all unique ‚úì - `required`: all four keys present ‚úì - `additionalProperties: false`: no extra keys ‚úì Result: **VALID**. **Invalid instance (test edge cases):** ```json { "id": -5, "name": "A", "email": "not-an-email", "age": 15, "phone": "9999999999" } ``` Result: **INVALID** ‚Äî four violations (`id < 1`, `name length 1`, `email format`, `age < 18`, and the extra `phone` field). ## 3.3 Composition Example Using `anyOf` Suppose we want the `id` field to accept either an **integer** OR a **UUID string**. We use `anyOf`: ```json "id": { "anyOf": [ { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 }, { "type": "string", "format": "uuid" } ] } $$ Logically: $$ V_{\texttt{anyOf}}(S, I) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} V(S_i, I) $$ The instance passes if **at least one** branch validates ‚Äî the dual of `allOf`. ## 3.4 KTU Analytical Reference Table | Validation Behaviour | Logical Operator | Boolean Equivalent | |---|---|---| | `allOf` | All branches must pass | $\land$ (AND) | | `anyOf` | At least one branch must pass | $\lor$ (OR) | | `oneOf` | Exactly one branch must pass | XOR | | `not` | The branch must fail | $\neg$ (NOT) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of JSON Schema Validation The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end validation pipeline** from a client's JSON payload to the server's response. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Sends JSON Payload] --> B[API Gateway Receives Request] B --> C{Schema Registered?} C -- Yes --> D[Load JSON Schema File] C -- No --> E[Allow Without Validation] D --> F[Validator Engine e.g. Ajv] F --> G{Validate V S I} G -- Valid True --> H[Route to Business Logic] H --> I[Process Request] I --> J[Return 200 OK Response] G -- Invalid False --> K[Collect Violations] K --> L[Format Error Response] L --> M[Return 400 Bad Request] ``` ## 4.2 Keyword Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[JSON Schema Document] --> B[Meta Keywords] A --> C[Type Keyword] A --> D[Constraint Keywords] A --> E[Composition Keywords] A --> F[Reference Keywords] B --> B1[$schema] B --> B2[$id] B --> B3[title] B --> B4[description] C --> C1[string] C --> C2[number] C --> C3[integer] C --> C4[boolean] C --> C5[object] C --> C6[array] C --> C7[null] D --> D1[String minLength maxLength pattern format] D --> D2[Number minimum maximum multipleOf] D --> D3[Object properties required additionalProperties] D --> D4[Array items minItems maxItems uniqueItems] E --> E1[allOf] E --> E2[anyOf] E --> E3[oneOf] E --> E4[not] F --> F1[$ref] ``` ## 4.3 Validation Decision Flow for a Single Field ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Field to Validate] --> TypeCheck{Type matches
schema type?} TypeCheck -- No --> Fail1[FAIL Type Mismatch] TypeCheck -- Yes --> HasConst{Any constraint
keywords?} HasConst -- No --> Pass1[PASS] HasConst -- Yes --> EvalConst[Evaluate each constraint] EvalConst --> AllOK{All constraints
satisfied?} AllOK -- No --> Fail2[FAIL Constraint Violation] AllOK -- Yes --> Pass2[PASS] ``` ## 4.4 How `$ref` Resolution Works ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Validator participant Ref as $ref Pointer participant Target as Referenced Schema Client->>Ref: Encounter $ref key Ref->>Target: Resolve JSON Pointer alt Local reference Target-->>Client: Return embedded definition else External reference Target->>Target: Fetch from URI Target-->>Client: Return remote schema end Client->>Client: Continue validation with referenced schema ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Style]** ### **Q1. Define JSON Schema. List any four keywords used in it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** JSON Schema is a specification for validating the structure and content of JSON documents. It is itself a JSON document that describes what valid data looks like ‚Äî defining data types, required fields, value ranges, and patterns. **Four keywords (1 mark each, any four):** 1. `type` ‚Äî Specifies the data type (string, number, object, etc.). 2. `properties` ‚Äî Defines the fields of an object and their individual schemas. 3. `required` ‚Äî Lists property names that must be present in the object. 4. `additionalProperties` ‚Äî Controls whether undeclared properties are allowed. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between `allOf` and `anyOf` composition keywords in JSON Schema.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `allOf` | `anyOf` | |---|---|---| | Logic | Logical AND | Logical OR | | Validation | Instance must satisfy **every** sub-schema | Instance must satisfy **at least one** sub-schema | | Failure | Fails if even one sub-schema fails | Passes if at least one sub-schema passes | | Use Case | Combining multiple constraints (e.g., must be a string AND match a pattern) | Allowing multiple types (e.g., either integer or string) | **Example:** `allOf` is used when a field must match both a type constraint and a format constraint. `anyOf` is used when a field can be one of several valid types. --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Question Paper Style]** ### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Standard Choice** #### **Q. (a) Explain the architecture of JSON Schema validation with a neat diagram. How does it enforce data integrity in RESTful APIs? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** JSON Schema is a declarative, JSON-based specification used to validate, document, and constrain the structure of JSON data. It acts as a contract between client and server, defining the expected shape of request and response payloads. **Architecture Steps (4 marks):** 1. **Schema Definition Phase:** The API developer writes a `.schema.json` file describing expected types, required fields, value ranges, and formats. 2. **Registration Phase:** The schema is registered with a validator engine (e.g., `Ajv` in Node.js, `jsonschema` in Python). 3. **Validation Phase:** When an HTTP request arrives, the validator parses the JSON body and applies the schema. Each keyword acts as a constraint gate. 4. **Response Phase:** On success, the request is forwarded to business logic. On failure, a `400 Bad Request` is returned with a detailed list of violations. **Data Integrity in REST APIs (1 mark):** JSON Schema ensures type safety, prevents missing required fields, blocks extra/unexpected fields, and enforces business rules at the API boundary ‚Äî catching bad data before it reaches the database or business layer. #### **Q. (b) Design a JSON Schema for a "Book" object with the following requirements. Validate the given instance against it. (7 marks)** **Requirements:** - `isbn` (string, 13 characters, required) - `title` (string, 2‚Äì100 characters, required) - `author` (string, required) - `price` (number, between 100 and 5000, required) - `genre` (string, must be one of: "Fiction", "Non-Fiction", "Science", "History") - `publishedYear` (integer, ‚â• 1900) **Model Schema (5 marks):** ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "Book", "type": "object", "properties": { "isbn": { "type": "string", "minLength": 13, "maxLength": 13 }, "title": { "type": "string", "minLength": 2, "maxLength": 100 }, "author": { "type": "string" }, "price": { "type": "number", "minimum": 100, "maximum": 5000 }, "genre": { "type": "string", "enum": ["Fiction", "Non-Fiction", "Science", "History"] }, "publishedYear": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1900 } }, "required": ["isbn", "title", "author", "price", "genre"], "additionalProperties": false } ``` **Validation of an instance (2 marks):** ```json { "isbn": "9780131103627", "title": "The C Programming Language", "author": "Kernighan & Ritchie", "price": 850, "genre": "Science", "publishedYear": 1978 } ``` - `isbn`: length 13 ‚úì - `title`: length 26, in [2, 100] ‚úì - `author`: string ‚úì - `price`: 850, in [100, 5000] ‚úì - `genre`: "Science" is in enum ‚úì - `publishedYear`: 1978 ‚â• 1900 ‚úì - All required fields present ‚úì - No extra properties ‚úì **Result: VALID** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice** #### **Q. (a) Explain the following JSON Schema keywords with examples: (i) `$ref` (ii) `enum` (iii) `uniqueItems` (iv) `additionalProperties`. (7 marks ‚Äî 1.75 marks each)** **Model Answer:** **(i) `$ref` (1.75 marks):** The `$ref` keyword allows a schema to reference another schema definition, enabling reuse and modularity. ```json { "definitions": { "address": { "type": "object", "properties": { "city": { "type": "string" }, "pincode": { "type": "string", "pattern": "^[0-9]{6}$" } } } }, "properties": { "homeAddress": { "$ref": "#/definitions/address" }, "officeAddress": { "$ref": "#/definitions/address" } } } ``` **(ii) `enum` (1.75 marks):** The `enum` keyword restricts a value to a fixed set of allowed values. It is heavily used for status fields, categories, and roles. ```json { "type": "string", "enum": ["pending", "approved", "rejected"] } ``` **(iii) `uniqueItems` (1.75 marks):** When set to `true`, all elements in an array must be distinct. Useful for ensuring no duplicate entries in lists such as tags, IDs, or email addresses. ```json { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string", "format": "email" }, "uniqueItems": true } ``` **(iv) `additionalProperties` (1.75 marks):** Controls whether properties not declared in `properties` are allowed. Setting it to `false` creates a **closed schema** that strictly forbids unknown fields ‚Äî a security best practice. ```json { "type": "object", "properties": { "username": { "type": "string" } }, "additionalProperties": false } ``` #### **Q. (b) Compare JSON Schema Draft 7 and Draft 2020-12. Mention at least four improvements in the newer draft. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** JSON Schema has evolved through multiple drafts. Draft 7 (2018) was the most widely adopted for years, but Draft 2020-12 (and subsequent 2020-12 revisions) brought significant improvements and was eventually standardized as an IETF draft. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Aspect | Draft 7 | Draft 2020-12 | |---|---|---| | `$id` resolution | URI only, anchored to base | Full URI resolution with new keyword `$anchor` | | Boolean schemas | `true`/`false` valid as schema | Now officially standardized with clear semantics | | `items` as array | Allowed but discouraged | Discouraged in favour of prefixItems | | `if`/`then`/`else` | Basic support | Full conditional schema support without `required` bug | | Vocabulary system | Implicit | Explicit vocabularies declared via `$vocabulary` | | `unevaluatedProperties` | Not present | **NEW** ‚Äî catches properties not validated by any sibling schema | | Format annotations | Inconsistent | Moved to a separate vocabulary (format-annotation vs format-assertion) | **Four Major Improvements (2 marks):** 1. **$vocabulary and $dynamicRef:** Enables true extensibility and custom validation vocabularies. 2. **`unevaluatedProperties` / `unevaluatedItems`:** Allows fine-grained control over which properties get "consumed" by sibling schemas ‚Äî solving a long-standing limitation in `allOf`. 3. **Better boolean schema semantics:** Clarifies the difference between `true` (always validate) and `false` (never validate). 4. **Format as separate vocabulary:** Splits "format as annotation" (informational) from "format as assertion" (validating), preventing false validation errors. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing `number` and `integer`:** A `price: 99.99` is a valid `number` but **NOT** a valid `integer`. Always check whether the requirement says "whole number" or "decimal allowed". > 2. **Forgetting `required` is an array, not a boolean:** Writing `"required": true` is a **syntax error** and will fetch zero marks. It must be `"required": ["field1", "field2"]`. > 3. **Forgetting `enum` uses an array, not a single value:** Correct syntax is `"enum": ["A", "B"]`, not `"enum": "A"`. > 4. **Not setting `additionalProperties: false`:** Many students leave it out, allowing unknown fields to pass ‚Äî examiners will deduct marks for insecure schema design. > 5. **Misusing `allOf` for "either-or":** Use `anyOf` or `oneOf` for OR-logic, NOT `allOf` (which is AND). This is a top-3 error. > 6. **Confusing `exclusiveMinimum` (Draft 7 boolean style vs Draft 2020-12 numeric style):** In Draft 2020-12, `exclusiveMinimum` is a **number**, not a boolean. Misusing this is a common KTU trap. > 7. **Writing `$ref` without `definitions`:** A reference like `"$ref": "#/definitions/address"` only works if `definitions` exists in the same schema. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for JSON Schema** - **Definition:** JSON Schema is a JSON document used to validate other JSON documents. - **Top-level keywords:** `$schema`, `$id`, `title`, `description`, `type`. - **Seven primitive types:** `string`, `number`, `integer`, `boolean`, `object`, `array`, `null`. - **String constraints:** `minLength`, `maxLength`, `pattern` (ECMA-262 regex), `format`. - **Number constraints:** `minimum`, `maximum`, `exclusiveMinimum`, `exclusiveMaximum`, `multipleOf`. - **Object constraints:** `properties`, `required`, `additionalProperties`, `minProperties`, `maxProperties`, `patternProperties`. - **Array constraints:** `items`, `minItems`, `maxItems`, `uniqueItems`. - **Composition keywords:** - `allOf` ‚Üí AND (all must pass) - `anyOf` ‚Üí OR (at least one must pass) - `oneOf` ‚Üí XOR (exactly one must pass) - `not` ‚Üí NOT (must fail) - **Reusability:** `$ref` with JSON Pointers (`#/definitions/...`). - **Boolean schemas:** `true` = always valid; `false` = always invalid. - **Conditional logic:** `if` / `then` / `else`. - **Advanced:** `$defs` (replacement for `definitions` in newer drafts), `unevaluatedProperties` (2020-12). - **Practical use:** OpenAPI/Swagger, MongoDB `$jsonSchema`, React form generation, API gateway validation. - **Common validators:** `Ajv` (JavaScript), `jsonschema` (Python), `everit` (Java). - **Draft versions to remember:** Draft 7 (broadly supported legacy) and Draft 2020-12 (current IETF track). - **Security best practice:** Always set `additionalProperties: false` for inbound API payloads. - **KTU exam mantra:** When in doubt, use `type` first, then `properties`, then `required`, then `additionalProperties: false`.

topics.title: JSON Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JSON Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**JSON Server** is a **Node.js-based, zero-configuration mock REST API tool** that allows developers to rapidly prototype, develop, and test front-end applications by generating a complete set of HTTP endpoints (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) directly from a single plain JSON file (`db.json`). It emulates a real backend server without requiring any server-side scripting, database setup, or routing logic to be written by the developer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** JSON Server is studied under the broader topic of *Client-Side Scripting / RESTful API Consumption* in **Module 4** of PCCNT503 (Web Programming). It is a **Type-4 prototype** API tool, classified under *Rapid Application Development (RAD)* utilities, primarily used as a learning vehicle to demonstrate how RESTful clients (e.g., `fetch()`, `XMLHttpRequest`, Axios) interact with HTTP-based services.

Formally, JSON Server is built on top of:
- **Node.js Runtime** ‚Üí Executes JavaScript outside the browser.
- **`express.js` Underlying Engine** ‚Üí Handles HTTP routing internally.
- **`lowdb` Persistence Layer** ‚Üí In-memory JSON store backed by a file.

The default port is **port 3000**, and the canonical installation command (according to the official `npm` registry) is:
```
npm install -g json-server
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**, but the actual land is still under legal dispute. You cannot wait for the land. So, you build a **wooden replica of the neighborhood on a separate plot of land** ‚Äî complete with same-size roads, same-shape plots, and mock neighbors. The architect (your front-end app) walks around this replica as if it were real, tests door designs, and places furniture. Only later, when the real land is available, the same architect's drawings are "dropped" onto the real construction site.

**JSON Server is exactly that wooden replica** for a back-end API:
- The **JSON file (`db.json`)** is the mock neighborhood map.
- The **REST endpoints** are the roads leading to each plot.
- The **front-end app** (the architect) thinks it is talking to a real server.
- When the real back-end is ready, the front-end simply **points to a new URL** ‚Äî no code changes needed in the client logic.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted in Bold)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Port** | **3000** | Where JSON Server listens by default |
| **Default Data File** | **`db.json`** | Source-of-truth JSON database |
| **CRUD Coverage** | **GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE** | All five HTTP verbs auto-mapped |
| **Default Resource ID** | **`id`** (auto-incremented) | Primary key for each resource |
| **Response Format** | **application/json** | IANA-standard MIME type |
| **Status Code (Success)** | **200 OK / 201 Created** | Standard HTTP success codes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout ‚Äî REST (Representational State Transfer):** REST is an architectural style (defined by Roy Fielding, 2000) where every URL represents a *resource* and HTTP verbs represent the *action* to be performed on that resource. JSON Server strictly implements REST conventions on top of a JSON file.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture: Why and How

JSON Server operates through a **three-layer pipeline**. Each layer is responsible for a specific transformation:

### Layer 1: Persistence Layer (lowdb)
- **Why:** Avoids the overhead of configuring a real database (MySQL, MongoDB) for prototyping.
- **How:** The `db.json` file is loaded into memory once on server start. All read operations happen in-memory (RAM), and any write operation is **synchronously flushed back to the file** to guarantee persistence across restarts.

### Layer 2: Routing Layer (express.js)
- **Why:** Manual route definition is tedious; JSON Server auto-generates them.
- **How:** On server start, the package **parses the keys** of `db.json` and dynamically registers express.js routes for each one. A key like `"posts"` becomes `GET /posts`, `POST /posts`, etc.

### Layer 3: Response Layer (JSON Serialization)
- **Why:** Front-end frameworks expect a serialized JSON response.
- **How:** All route handlers wrap the in-memory data with `res.jsonp()` or `res.json()`, applying transformations (filtering, sorting, pagination) **before** sending the response.

## 2.2 The Six Standard Auto-Generated Endpoints

For **any** resource name (let's call it `<resource>`), JSON Server creates these endpoints:

| HTTP Method | URL Pattern | Action | Equivalent CRUD |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/<resource>` | List all (with filters) | **R**ead (Collection) |
| `GET` | `/<resource>/:id` | Fetch single item | **R**ead (Single) |
| `POST` | `/<resource>` | Create new item | **C**reate |
| `PUT` | `/<resource>/:id` | Replace entire item | **U**pdate (Full) |
| `PATCH` | `/<resource>/:id` | Modify specific fields | **U**pdate (Partial) |
| `DELETE` | `/<resource>/:id` | Remove item | **D**elete |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equality Filter** | `?key=value` | Field equals value | `/posts?author=John` |
| **Range Filter** | `?key_gte=X&key_lte=Y` | Greater/less than (inclusive) | `/products?price_gte=100&price_lte=500` |
| **Strict Range** | `?key_gt=X&key_lt=Y` | Greater/less than (exclusive) | `/products?price_gt=100` |
| **Negation** | `?key_ne=value` | Not equal to | `/users?role_ne=admin` |
| **Pattern Match** | `?key_like=pattern` | SQL-style `LIKE` | `/posts?title_like=API` |
| **Full-Text Search** | `?q=keyword` | Searches all string fields | `/posts?q=hello` |
| **Sort** | `?_sort=field&_order=asc/desc` | Sort by field | `/posts?_sort=date&_order=desc` |
| **Pagination** | `?_page=N&_limit=M` | Page N, M items per page | `/posts?_page=2&_limit=10` |
| **Slice** | `?_start=N&_end=M` | Range-based slicing | `/posts?_start=5&_end=10` |
| **Embed** | `?_embed=child` | Include children | `/posts/1?_embed=comments` |
| **Expand** | `?_expand=parent` | Include parent | `/comments?postId=1&_expand=post` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The `_sort` and `_order` query parameters are **single-field only** in vanilla JSON Server. Multi-field sort requires a custom middleware or sorting client-side. Many students write `?_sort=field1,field2` ‚Äî this **silently fails** and returns the default order.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Front-End Development** | Decouple UI development from back-end timeline. |
| **Education** | Teach REST concepts without a heavy database setup (this is the **primary KTU use-case**). |
| **Testing & QA** | Provide deterministic data fixtures for end-to-end (E2E) tests in Cypress/Playwright. |
| **Hackathons** | Spin up a working API in under 60 seconds. |
| **Mobile Development** | Provide a mock data layer for React Native, Flutter, or Swift apps. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Installation & First-Run (End-to-End Derivation)

The following is the **complete, exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** to instantiate a JSON Server instance. Each step is annotated with the underlying *why* so the logic is preserved for the examination.

### Step 1: Initialize a Node.js Project
```
mkdir json-server-demo
cd json-server-demo
npm init -y
```
**Why:** `npm init -y` creates a `package.json` file with default values. This is required because JSON Server (and any npm package) needs to be installed *within* a Node project context, and a project without `package.json` cannot install dependencies properly.

### Step 2: Install JSON Server
```
npm install json-server
```
**Why:** This downloads the JSON Server package and its transitive dependencies (`express`, `lowdb`, `body-parser`, etc.) into the local `node_modules/` folder. The version installed is recorded in `package.json` under `"dependencies"`.

### Step 3: Create `db.json` (The Source of Truth)
Create a file named `db.json` in the project root:
```json
{
  "posts": [
    { "id": 1, "title": "JSON Server Tutorial", "author": "KTU Student" },
    { "id": 2, "title": "REST API Basics", "author": "Web Dev" }
  ],
  "comments": [
    { "id": 1, "body": "Great post!", "postId": 1 },
    { "id": 2, "body": "Very helpful.", "postId": 2 }
  ],
  "profile": { "name": "Admin", "role": "Developer" }
}
```
**Why:** Each **top-level key** in this JSON object becomes a *resource* (a collection URL). A single object (like `profile`) becomes a *singleton resource* (only `GET/PUT /profile` is created).

### Step 4: Start the Server
```
npx json-server db.json
```
**Why:** `npx` is Node's package executor. It finds the local `json-server` binary in `node_modules/.bin/` and runs it with `db.json` as the argument. The server boots on port **3000** by default and prints the available routes.

### Step 5: Verify Endpoints
Open a browser or `curl`:
```
http://localhost:3000/posts
http://localhost:3000/comments/1
http://localhost:3000/profile
```
**Why:** These URLs confirm the auto-generated routing is working. The browser response will be a JSON array (for collections) or a JSON object (for single items/singletons).

## 3.2 Mathematical / Logical Derivation of the Filter Pipeline

When a request like `GET /posts?author=John&_sort=title&_order=desc` arrives, the internal pipeline processes the query string in **five deterministic stages**:

$$
\text{Raw} \rightarrow \text{Stage 1 (Parse)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2 (Filter)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3 (Sort)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 4 (Slice)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5 (Serialize)}
$$

### Stage 1: Query String Parsing
The query string is split on `&`, then each pair is split on `=`:

$$
Q = \{ \text{author}=\text{"John"},\ \text{\_sort}=\text{"title"},\ \text{\_order}=\text{"desc"} \}
$$

### Stage 2: Filter Application
For every key-value pair **not** starting with `_`, a predicate is generated:

$$
P(\text{record}) = (\text{record.author} == \text{"John"})
$$

The dataset is reduced to the subset where the predicate evaluates to `true`:

$$
D_{\text{filtered}} = \{ r \in D \mid P(r) = \text{true} \}
$$

### Stage 3: Sort Application
The `_sort` and `_order` keys are consumed, and a stable sort is applied. The comparator is:

$$
\text{Compare}(a, b) = 
\begin{cases} 
-1 & \text{if } a.\text{title} < b.\text{title} \\
+1 & \text{if } a.\text{title} > b.\text{title} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

For `desc`, the result is multiplied by `-1`.

### Stage 4: Slice / Paginate
If `_page` and `_limit` are present:

$$
\text{Items Returned} = D_{\text{sorted}} \big[ ( \text{\_page} - 1 ) \cdot \text{\_limit} \ \big| \ \text{\_page} \cdot \text{\_limit} \big)
$$

In set-builder notation, where `offset = (page ‚àí 1) * limit`:

$$
\text{Result} = \{ D_{\text{sorted}}[i] \mid i \in \mathbb{Z},\ \text{offset} \le i < \text{offset} + \text{limit} \}
$$

### Stage 5: Serialization
The remaining array is wrapped with metadata headers (`X-Total-Count`, `Access-Control-Expose-Headers`) and sent as `application/json`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** A `Link` header is auto-attached in pagination responses with the URLs of the `first`, `prev`, `next`, and `last` pages. This conforms to **RFC 5988 (Web Linking)**. The examiner may ask why pagination metadata is in the header and not the body ‚Äî the answer is the **stateless nature of HTTP** and **separation of concerns** between data and metadata.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Difference Between PUT and PATCH

| Aspect | `PUT /posts/1` | `PATCH /posts/1` |
|---|---|---|
| **Body Required** | Entire object | Only changed fields |
| **Replaces Resource** | Yes (overwrites) | No (merges) |
| **Missing Fields** | Set to `null` / deleted | Preserved from existing record |
| **HTTP Standard** | RFC 7231 ¬ß4.3.4 | RFC 5789 |
| **Idempotent** | **Yes** | **No** (depends on merge logic) |
| **JSON Server Behavior** | Replaces whole record | Deep-merges object body |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often answer "PUT is for update, PATCH is for delete" ‚Äî this is **incorrect**. PATCH is also an update method (partial update). DELETE has its own verb and never uses PATCH.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete request-response lifecycle** of a JSON Server instance. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords used as standalone IDs).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Client Browser or Postman] --> A2[HTTP Request with URL and Query String]
    A2 --> A3[Express.js Server on Port 3000]
    A3 --> A4{Request Method and URL Match}
    A4 -->|GET Collection| A5[Apply Filter Operator Pipeline]
    A4 -->|GET Single Item| A6[Lookup by ID in In-Memory Array]
    A4 -->|POST New Item| A7[Auto Increment ID and Append]
    A4 -->|PUT Full Update| A8[Replace Object at ID]
    A4 -->|PATCH Partial| A9[Deep Merge Object at ID]
    A4 -->|DELETE Item| A10[Splice Out and Return 200]
    
    A5 --> A11[Apply Sort Comparator]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Slice or Pagination Logic]
    A12 --> A13[Serialize Result as JSON]
    
    A6 --> A13
    A7 --> A14[Persist Changes to db.json File]
    A8 --> A14
    A9 --> A14
    A10 --> A14
    
    A14 --> A13
    A13 --> A15[Send HTTP Response with JSON Body]
    A15 --> A16[Client Receives Parsed Data]
```

## 4.2 Resource Relationship Diagram (Embed and Expand)

This diagram illustrates how `_embed` and `_expand` handle **parent-child relationships** between `posts` and `comments`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["POST 1 Title JSON Server Tutorial"] 
    P2["POST 2 Title REST API Basics"]
    C1["COMMENT 1 Body Great post"]
    C2["COMMENT 2 Body Very helpful"]
    
    P1 -->|has many| C1
    P2 -->|has many| C2
    C1 -->|belongs to| P1
    C2 -->|belongs to| P2
```

### Explanation
- **`GET /posts/1?_embed=comments`** ‚Üí Returns POST 1 *with* its comments **embedded** inside an array called `comments`.
- **`GET /comments/1?_expand=post`** ‚Üí Returns COMMENT 1 *with* its parent post **expanded** inside an object called `post`.

The relationship is established automatically by JSON Server by detecting the **foreign-key convention**: a field named `<resource>Id` (e.g., `postId`) on the child resource points to the `id` of the parent.

## 4.3 Custom Routes Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[User Defines routes Object in JSON] --> B2[JSON Server Reads routes.json]
    B2 --> B3{Key Format Check}
    B3 -->|Single Colon| B4[Simple Rewrite - /api/posts to /posts]
    B3 -->|Double Colon| B5[Parametric Route - /api/posts/:id]
    B3 -->|Wildcard| B6[Use of Asterisk for Default Params]
    B4 --> B7[Register Custom Route in Express]
    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[New Endpoint Available at Custom URL]
```

### Example `routes.json` Mapped to the Above
```json
{
  "/api/\\:resource/\\:id/show": "/:resource/:id",
  "/api/\\:resource": "/:resource",
  "/blog/posts/show/\\:id": "/posts/:id"
}
```
> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Note:** In `routes.json`, every colon (`:`) and forward slash (`/`) used as a parameter must be **escaped with double backslashes** (`\\:`, `\\/`) in the **JSON string value**, but in Mermaid and other rendering tools the literal `:` is shown. This is a frequent point of confusion in the lab exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, PCCNT503]**

### Q1. What is JSON Server? Mention any two of its features.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
JSON Server is a Node.js-based tool that creates a fully functional fake REST API from a single `db.json` file without requiring any backend code. It is used primarily for front-end prototyping and testing. 
**Features (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Zero configuration** ‚Äî Just a JSON file is enough; no server-side code required.
2. **Auto-generated CRUD endpoints** ‚Äî GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE are auto-mapped for every top-level key in `db.json`.
3. **Built-in filtering, sorting, and pagination** via query parameters (`_sort`, `_order`, `_page`, `_limit`).
4. **Relationship support** through `_embed` and `_expand` query strings.

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, PCCNT503]**

### Q2. Differentiate between PUT and PATCH with respect to JSON Server.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| `PUT` | `PATCH` |
|---|---|
| Updates the **entire** resource; missing fields become `null` or get deleted. | Updates **partial** fields; existing fields not in the body are preserved. |
| Idempotent (same result on repeated calls). | Not necessarily idempotent (depends on body). |
| Defined in RFC 7231. | Defined in RFC 5789. |

Example: `PUT /posts/1` with body `{ "title": "New" }` deletes the `author` field. `PATCH /posts/1` with the same body keeps `author` intact.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, PCCNT503, Module 4]**

### ‚ùì Question A (14 Marks)

**Consider the following `db.json` file and answer the questions that follow:**

```json
{
  "books": [
    { "id": 1, "title": "JavaScript Basics", "price": 299, "author": "John" },
    { "id": 2, "title": "Advanced Python", "price": 499, "author": "Sara" },
    { "id": 3, "title": "Web API Design", "price": 399, "author": "John" }
  ]
}
```

#### (a) Write the complete procedure to set up and run JSON Server for the above `db.json`. Also explain any two filtering query parameters with examples. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Project Initialization (1 mark)**
Open a terminal, navigate to the project folder, and run:
```
npm init -y
```
This creates a `package.json` file.

**Step 2: Install JSON Server (1 mark)**
```
npm install json-server
```

**Step 3: Create `db.json` (1 mark)**
Save the given JSON content into a file named `db.json` in the project root.

**Step 4: Start the Server (1 mark)**
```
npx json-server db.json
```
The server starts on port 3000. The console displays:
```
Resources
http://localhost:3000/books
Home
http://localhost:3000
```

**Step 5 & 6: Two Filtering Query Parameters (3 marks)**

**Filter 1 ‚Äî Range Filter (1.5 marks):**
`GET /books?price_gte=300&price_lte=450`
This returns books with `price` between 300 and 450 inclusive:
```json
[
  { "id": 3, "title": "Web API Design", "price": 399, "author": "John" }
]
```

**Filter 2 ‚Äî Full-Text Search (1.5 marks):**
`GET /books?q=JavaScript`
This searches all string fields and returns records where any field contains "JavaScript":
```json
[
  { "id": 1, "title": "JavaScript Basics", "price": 299, "author": "John" }
]
```

#### (b) Write the HTTP request to perform each of the following operations and explain the response: (7 marks)
1. Add a new book with title "Node.js Guide", price 599, author "Mike".
2. Update only the price of book id=2 to 550.
3. Delete book id=1.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Operation 1: Create New Book (2.5 marks)**
```
POST http://localhost:3000/books
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "title": "Node.js Guide",
  "price": 599,
  "author": "Mike"
}
```
**Response:** HTTP **201 Created** with the newly created object including the auto-generated `id: 4`. The new record is appended to the in-memory array and persisted to `db.json`.

**Operation 2: Partial Price Update (2.5 marks)**
```
PATCH http://localhost:3000/books/2
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "price": 550
}
```
**Response:** HTTP **200 OK** with the updated object `{ "id": 2, "title": "Advanced Python", "price": 550, "author": "Sara" }`. Note that `title` and `author` are preserved (this proves PATCH is partial).

**Operation 3: Delete Book (2 marks)**
```
DELETE http://localhost:3000/books/1
```
**Response:** HTTP **200 OK** with an empty object `{}`. The book with `id: 1` is removed from the in-memory array and `db.json`.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, PCCNT503, Module 4]**

### ‚ùì Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the concept of relationships in JSON Server with a suitable example. Differentiate between `_embed` and `_expand`. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
JSON Server auto-detects **one-to-many** relationships by looking for foreign-key fields of the form `<resource>Id`. For example, in the `db.json`:
```json
{
  "posts": [
    { "id": 1, "title": "REST", "views": 100 }
  ],
  "comments": [
    { "id": 1, "postId": 1, "body": "Nice" },
    { "id": 2, "postId": 1, "body": "Helpful" }
  ]
}
```
JSON Server infers that `comments.postId` is a foreign key linking to `posts.id`.

**`_embed` (2.5 marks):**
`_embed` includes **children** in the parent response.
`GET /posts/1?_embed=comments` returns:
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "title": "REST",
  "views": 100,
  "comments": [
    { "id": 1, "postId": 1, "body": "Nice" },
    { "id": 2, "postId": 1, "body": "Helpful" }
  ]
}
```

**`_expand` (2.5 marks):**
`_expand` includes the **parent** in the child response.
`GET /comments?postId=1&_expand=post` returns:
```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "postId": 1,
    "body": "Nice",
    "post": { "id": 1, "title": "REST", "views": 100 }
  }
]
```

**Key Difference:** `_embed` pulls *child-to-parent* (parent contains children array), whereas `_expand` pulls *parent-to-child* (child contains parent object).

#### (b) Explain the following with examples: (7 marks)
1. Sorting and pagination query parameters.
2. Custom routes using `routes.json`.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Sorting and Pagination (3.5 marks):**

**Sorting (1.75 marks):**
`GET /books?_sort=price&_order=desc`
Sorts books by `price` in descending order. Valid `order` values are `asc` and `desc` (default is `asc`).

**Pagination (1.75 marks):**
`GET /books?_page=1&_limit=2`
Returns the first 2 books. JSON Server also adds the following response headers:
- `X-Total-Count: 3` ‚Üí Total number of records.
- `Link: <...>; rel="next", <...>; rel="last"` ‚Üí Navigation links per **RFC 5988**.

**2. Custom Routes using `routes.json` (3.5 marks):**

Create a file `routes.json`:
```json
{
  "/api/all-books": "/books",
  "/api/books/show/:id": "/books/:id",
  "/api/books/delete/:id": "/books/:id"
}
```

Start the server with the routes file:
```
npx json-server db.json --routes routes.json
```

Now `GET http://localhost:3000/api/all-books` returns the same as `GET /books`. Custom routes allow **API versioning** and **URL structure customization** without modifying the client code that hits real back-ends.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Common Mark-Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the default port (3000):** Students who write `localhost:8080` lose 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `_sort` with `sort`:** The underscore prefix is mandatory; `sort` is ignored.
> 3. **Using `PUT` instead of `PATCH` for partial update:** The entire record gets wiped, marking the answer wrong.
> 4. **Not specifying HTTP status codes:** Examiners expect `200` and `201` to be mentioned in CRUD answers.
> 5. **Writing `delete` instead of `DELETE` in HTTP methods:** HTTP verbs are case-sensitive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **JSON Server** = Node.js tool that turns `db.json` into a full fake REST API.
- [x] **Installation:** `npm install json-server` (locally) or `npm install -g json-server` (globally).
- [x] **Startup Command:** `npx json-server db.json` (or `json-server --watch db.json` for auto-reload).
- [x] **Default Port:** **3000** (configurable via `--port` flag).
- [x] **Six Auto-Endpoints per Resource:** `GET (collection)`, `GET (by id)`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`.
- [x] **Filter Operators:** `_gte`, `_lte`, `_gt`, `_lt`, `_ne`, `_like` ‚Äî all suffix-based.
- [x] **Special Query Keys:** `q` (full-text), `_sort`, `_order`, `_page`, `_limit`, `_start`, `_end`, `_embed`, `_expand`.
- [x] **ID Auto-Increment:** New `POST` records get `id = max(existing IDs) + 1`.
- [x] **Persistence:** Every write is flushed to `db.json` synchronously.
- [x] **PUT vs PATCH:** PUT = full replace; PATCH = deep merge of body into existing object.
- [x] **Singleton Resources:** An object (not array) at the top level of `db.json` becomes a single resource (e.g., `/profile`).
- [x] **Relationships:** Convention is `<parentResource>Id`; auto-detected; no manual FK definition needed.
- [x] **Custom Routes:** Use `routes.json` with `--routes` flag; double-escape colons in JSON.
- [x] **Static File Serving:** `json-server` can also serve static files via `--static` flag, transforming it into a mini full-stack dev environment.
- [x] **CORS Enabled by Default:** Browsers can call it cross-origin without extra configuration.
- [x] **Limitations:** Not for production; no authentication; no authorization; single-process (no clustering).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulating JSON Data with PHP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Manipulating JSON Data with PHP # Module 4: Manipulating JSON Data with PHP ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** is a lightweight, language-independent, text-based data interchange format derived from JavaScript object literal syntax, standardized as **ECMA-404** and **RFC 8259**. In the context of PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor), JSON manipulation refers to the process of **serializing** PHP data structures (arrays, objects, scalars) into JSON strings and **deserializing** JSON strings back into native PHP variables using the built-in JSON extension. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the JSON extension in PHP provides a bi-directional bridge between server-side application logic and client-side / external-system data exchange layers, primarily over HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) using RESTful (Representational State Transfer) APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCNT503 - Module 4):** > Students must be able to (i) encode PHP data into JSON, (ii) decode JSON strings into PHP arrays/objects, (iii) handle file-based JSON storage, and (iv) implement error checking using `json_last_error()`. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Universal Courier Envelope" Imagine you are working in a **post office** in India that needs to send parcels between Kerala, Tokyo, and Berlin. The post office (PHP) only understands local packaging rules (PHP arrays and objects). However, the destination sorting centers (JavaScript, Python, Java, mobile apps) each speak different languages. **JSON acts as a standardized courier envelope:** - You take your local parcel (PHP array) ‚Üí pack it in a universally agreed envelope format (`json_encode`) - The envelope travels anywhere in the world - At the destination, the receiver unpacks it back into their local structure (`json_decode`) Just like a courier envelope has fields for *sender, receiver, weight, contents* written in a standard format, JSON has *keys, values, arrays, and objects* written in a standard text format. PHP's JSON functions are the **packers and unpackers** that make this translation seamless. ## 1.3 Why JSON with PHP? > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Reasons for using JSON in PHP:** > 1. **Native Support**: JSON support is compiled into PHP core since **PHP 5.2.0** (June 2006) ‚Äî no external library installation required. > 2. **Lightweight**: JSON payloads are typically **30%‚Äì40% smaller** than equivalent XML, reducing network bandwidth. > 3. **REST API Standard**: Modern RESTful services (used by KTU lab projects, e-commerce sites, social APIs) overwhelmingly use JSON over XML. > 4. **JavaScript Compatibility**: Browser-native `JSON.parse()` and `JSON.stringify()` can directly consume PHP-generated JSON. > 5. **Database Interchange**: Popular NoSQL stores like **MongoDB** use BSON (Binary JSON), and modern relational stores (PostgreSQL, MySQL 5.7+) offer native JSON column types. --- ## 1.4 The Two Pillars of PHP-JSON Manipulation | Operation | PHP Function | Direction | Common Use Case | |-----------|-------------|-----------|-----------------| | Encoding | `json_encode($value, $options, $depth)` | PHP ‚Üí JSON | API responses, AJAX payloads, file storage | | Decoding | `json_decode($json, $assoc, $depth, $options)` | JSON ‚Üí PHP | Reading configs, consuming third-party APIs | These two functions are the **only mandatory functions** required to handle 95% of KTU examination questions on this topic. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 PHP Data Type ‚Üí JSON Type Mapping (The Core Translation Table) Before writing any code, every KTU student must internalize this mapping. PHP's `json_encode` converts PHP types to JSON types as follows: | PHP Type | JSON Type | Example (PHP) | Result (JSON) | |----------|-----------|---------------|---------------| | `string` | `string` | `"Kerala"` | `"Kerala"` | | `integer` | `number` | `503` | `503` | | `float` | `number` | `3.14` | `3.14` | | `boolean` (true) | `true` | `true` | `true` | | `boolean` (false) | `false` | `false` | `false` | | `NULL` | `null` | `NULL` | `null` | | `array` (indexed) | `array` | `[1,2,3]` | `[1,2,3]` | | `array` (associative) | `object` | `["id"=>1]` | `{"id":1}` | | `object` (stdClass) | `object` | `new stdClass` | `{}` (or populated) | | `resource` | *not supported* | `fopen(...)` | `{}` (empty) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** A **sequential numeric-indexed PHP array** becomes a JSON **array `[ ]**, while a **string-keyed (associative) PHP array** becomes a JSON **object `{ }`. This single distinction is the most-tested concept in board exams.** ## 2.2 Function Signatures ‚Äî The Exact Specifications ### 2.2.1 `json_encode()` ```php string json_encode(mixed $value, int $flags = 0, int $depth = 512) ``` **Parameters:** 1. `$value` (mixed) ‚Äî The PHP value to encode. Can be any type except `resource`. 2. `$flags` (int) ‚Äî Bitmask of options (default: 0). See table below. 3. `$depth` (int) ‚Äî Maximum nesting depth (default: **512**). Set to prevent stack overflow on deeply nested data. **Return Value:** Returns a JSON-encoded `string` on success, or `false` on failure. ### 2.2.2 `json_decode()` ```php mixed json_decode(string $json, bool $assoc = false, int $depth = 512, int $flags = 0) ``` **Parameters:** 1. `$json` (string) ‚Äî The JSON string to decode. Must be UTF-8 encoded. 2. `$assoc` (bool) ‚Äî **Critical KTU parameter.** When `true`, returns PHP **associative arrays**; when `false` (default), returns PHP **`stdClass` objects**. 3. `$depth` (int) ‚Äî Maximum nesting depth. 4. `$flags` (int) ‚Äî Bitmask of decoding options. **Return Value:** Returns the decoded PHP value, or `null` on failure (be careful ‚Äî valid JSON `null` also returns `null`; use `json_last_error()` to disambiguate). ## 2.3 The Complete JSON Flags Cheat Sheet (KTU High-Yield) | Constant | Used In | Purpose | Typical KTU Use | |----------|---------|---------|-----------------| | `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT` | encode | Adds whitespace/indentation | Debugging, human-readable files | | `JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES` | encode | Don't escape `/` as `\/` | URLs in JSON | | `JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE` | encode | Don't `\uXXXX`-encode Unicode | Malayalam/regional text | | `JSON_NUMERIC_CHECK` | encode | Converts numeric strings to numbers | API normalization | | `JSON_FORCE_OBJECT` | encode | Forces object output for empty arrays | Avoids `[]` vs `{}` ambiguity | | `JSON_HEX_TAG` | encode | Converts `<`, `>` to `\u003C` etc. | XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) safety | | `JSON_BIGINT_AS_STRING` | decode | Large integers as strings instead of floats | Preserving precision | | `JSON_OBJECT_AS_ARRAY` | decode | Same as `$assoc = true` (alias) | Reading JSON as arrays | ## 2.4 The Error-Handling Function ‚Äî `json_last_error()` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** Writing `json_encode($x)` or `json_decode($x)` without error handling is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. Always pair decoding with `json_last_error()` (or `json_last_error_msg()`). ```php int json_last_error() // Returns integer error code string json_last_error_msg() // Returns human-readable message (PHP 5.5+) ``` **Critical Error Codes (must memorize):** | Constant | Integer | Meaning | |----------|---------|---------| | `JSON_ERROR_NONE` | 0 | No error | | `JSON_ERROR_DEPTH` | 1 | Maximum stack depth exceeded | | `JSON_ERROR_STATE_MISMATCH` | 2 | Invalid/ malformed JSON (e.g., missing brackets) | | `JSON_ERROR_CTRL_CHAR` | 3 | Control character found | | `JSON_ERROR_SYNTAX` | 4 | Syntax error | | `JSON_ERROR_UTF8` | 5 | Malformed UTF-8 characters | ## 2.5 File-Based JSON Manipulation (Server-Side Persistence) When JSON must be stored on disk (e.g., a configuration file, a small user database in a KTU lab project), the standard KTU pattern uses three native file functions plus JSON encoding: 1. `file_get_contents("data.json")` ‚Äî Reads the entire file into a string. 2. `file_put_contents("data.json", $data)` ‚Äî Writes a string to file (returns bytes written). 3. `is_file()` / `filesize()` ‚Äî Pre-checks before reading. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** KTU lab exams frequently ask students to "create a PHP script that reads student records from a JSON file, adds a new record, and saves the file back." This is the **exact** pattern they expect. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility ‚Äî Where This Is Used in Industry - **REST APIs**: Laravel, Symfony, CodeIgniter all return `json_encode()` responses with the `Content-Type: application/json` header. - **Frontend Frameworks**: React, Vue, Angular consume PHP API responses via `fetch()` and `JSON.parse()`. - **Configuration**: Many PHP frameworks (Laravel's `config/*.php` after caching) use `.json` for cache files. - **Mobile Backends**: Android (Kotlin) and iOS (Swift) apps exchange data with PHP backends via JSON. - **IoT Devices**: Sensors send telemetry as JSON, PHP gateways decode and process. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The following examples progress from a single scalar to a complete CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) system on a JSON file. Each step explicitly maps the *input*, *function call*, and *output*, exactly as expected in KTU model answers. --- ## 3.1 Example 1 ‚Äî Encoding a Simple Associative Array **Problem:** Convert a PHP associative array representing a KTU student to a JSON string. ### Step 1: Define the PHP Data Structure ```php "KTU2024CS001", "name" => "Ananya Pillai", "branch" => "Computer Science", "cgpa" => 8.72, "active" => true, "courses" => ["PCCNT503", "PCCNT504", "HSIRT505"] ]; ``` The variable `$student` is an **associative array** because its keys are strings (`"reg_no"`, `"name"`, etc.). Therefore, by the mapping rule in Section 2.1, this will encode as a **JSON object** `{ }`, not a JSON array `[ ]`. ### Step 2: Apply `json_encode()` ```php $jsonString = json_encode($student); echo $jsonString; ``` ### Step 3: Resulting JSON String ```json {"reg_no":"KTU2024CS001","name":"Ananya Pillai","branch":"Computer Science","cgpa":8.72,"active":true,"courses":["PCCNT503","PCCNT504","HSIRT505"]} ``` ### Step 4: Observe the Type Mappings Notice in the output: - PHP integer `8.72` (a float) ‚Üí JSON `8.72` (number) - PHP boolean `true` ‚Üí JSON `true` - PHP indexed array `["PCCNT503", ...]` ‚Üí JSON array `[ ... ]` - PHP associative array outer ‚Üí JSON object `{ ... }` --- ## 3.2 Example 2 ‚Äî Decoding with `assoc` Flag Comparison **Problem:** Decode the same JSON string and observe the difference between `assoc=false` (default) and `assoc=true`. ### Step 1: Set Up the JSON Input ```php name; // Access via arrow operator (object syntax) echo gettype($obj); // Outputs: "object" ``` **Output:** `Ananya Pillai` and `object`. The decoded result is a `stdClass` (standard class) instance. Members are accessed using the object operator `->`. ### Step 3: Decode as Associative Array ```php $arr = json_decode($jsonInput, true); // $assoc = true echo $arr['name']; // Access via bracket syntax (array syntax) echo gettype($arr); // Outputs: "array" ``` **Output:** `Ananya Pillai` and `array`. The decoded result is a PHP associative array. Members are accessed using bracket `[]` syntax. ### Step 4: Analytical Comparison Table | Aspect | `assoc = false` (default) | `assoc = true` | |--------|---------------------------|----------------| | Returned type | `stdClass` object | Associative array | | Access syntax | `$obj->key` | `$arr['key']` | | Best for | OOP (Object-Oriented Programming) code, Laravel models | Procedural code, simple lookups | | Memory overhead | Slightly higher | Slightly lower | | KTU lab recommendation | Less common | **More common** (used in 90% of KTU exam answers) | --- ## 3.3 Example 3 ‚Äî Using `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT` for Human-Readable Output **Problem:** Generate a formatted JSON suitable for storage in a `.json` configuration file. ### Step 1: Define Data and Encode with Pretty Print ```php [ "host" => "localhost", "port" => 3306, "username" => "ktu_admin", "password" => "secret123" ], "app" => [ "name" => "KTU Portal", "debug" => true ] ]; $pretty = json_encode($config, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT); echo $pretty; ``` ### Step 2: Output ```json { "database": { "host": "localhost", "port": 3306, "username": "ktu_admin", "password": "secret123" }, "app": { "name": "KTU Portal", "debug": true } } ``` ### Step 3: Analysis Without `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT`, all this data would collapse onto a single line. The pretty-print option adds: - **2-space indentation** (default) - **Newline characters** `\n` after each key-value pair and bracket - **Readability** for humans at the cost of slightly larger file size > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "store data in a JSON file in a readable format", always include `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT`. Examiners check for it. --- ## 3.4 Example 4 ‚Äî File-Based JSON CRUD (Complete Lab Solution) This is a **full mini-application** representing a KTU exam-style problem: maintain a JSON file of students, with Create, Read, Update, Delete operations. ### Step 3.4.1 ‚Äî Initialize: Create `students.json` ```php 1, "name" => "Rahul Menon", "marks" => 85 ], [ "id" => 2, "name" => "Sneha Iyer", "marks" => 92 ] ]; $json = json_encode($initialData, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT); file_put_contents("students.json", $json); echo "File created successfully."; ``` **Explanation:** 1. The outer PHP array uses **sequential integer keys (0, 1)** implicitly when initialized with `[ [..], [..] ]`. Therefore, `json_encode` produces a **JSON array `[ ... ]`** at the top level. 2. The inner arrays use **string keys** (`"id"`, `"name"`, `"marks"`), so each inner element becomes a **JSON object `{ ... }`**. ### Step 3.4.2 ‚Äî READ: Load and Display All Students ```php Student List"; echo ""; echo ""; foreach ($students as $s) { echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; } echo "
IDNameMarks
" . $s['id'] . "" . $s['name'] . "" . $s['marks'] . "
"; ``` ### Step 3.4.3 ‚Äî CREATE: Add a New Student ```php count($students) + 1, "name" => "Aravind Krishnan", "marks" => 78 ]; $students[] = $newStudent; // Append to the PHP array $updatedJson = json_encode($students, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT); file_put_contents("students.json", $updatedJson); echo "Student added. Total records: " . count($students); ``` ### Step 3.4.4 ‚Äî UPDATE: Modify an Existing Student ```php $s) { if ($s['id'] == 2) { $students[$key]['marks'] = 95; // Update the marks field break; } } file_put_contents("students.json", json_encode($students, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT)); echo "Record updated."; ``` ### Step 3.4.5 ‚Äî DELETE: Remove a Student ```php $s) { if ($s['id'] == 1) { unset($students[$key]); // Remove the element break; } } $students = array_values($students); // Re-index to make keys sequential (0,1,2...) file_put_contents("students.json", json_encode($students, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT)); echo "Record deleted."; ``` ### Step 3.4.6 ‚Äî The Critical `array_values()` Call > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** After `unset()`, the array's keys become non-sequential (e.g., `0, 2, 5`). When this is encoded to JSON, it becomes a JSON **object** instead of a JSON **array** ‚Äî because PHP treats non-sequential string/numeric-keyed arrays as associative. Calling `array_values()` re-numbers the keys to `0, 1, 2, ...`, restoring the indexed-array behavior and producing a clean JSON array `[ ]`. --- ## 3.5 Example 5 ‚Äî Robust Error Handling **Problem:** Decode user-supplied JSON safely, with full error checking. ```php true, 'data' => $result]; case JSON_ERROR_DEPTH: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Maximum stack depth exceeded']; case JSON_ERROR_STATE_MISMATCH: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Underflow or mode mismatch']; case JSON_ERROR_CTRL_CHAR: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Unexpected control character found']; case JSON_ERROR_SYNTAX: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Syntax error: malformed JSON']; case JSON_ERROR_UTF8: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Malformed UTF-8 characters']; default: return ['success' => false, 'error' => 'Unknown error']; } } // Usage $userInput = '{"name":"Test","age":25}'; // Valid JSON $response = safeJsonDecode($userInput); print_r($response); // Output: // Array ( [success] => 1 [data] => Array ( [name] => Test [age] => 25 ) ) ``` --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî `json_encode` Behavior on Edge Cases | Input | `json_encode` Output | Reason | |-------|----------------------|--------| | `[]` (empty array) | `[]` | Empty indexed array ‚Üí JSON empty array | | `["a" => 1]` (1 element assoc) | `{"a":1}` | String key triggers object | | `new stdClass()` (empty object) | `{}` | Empty stdClass ‚Üí empty object | | `PHP_INT_MAX` (9223372036854775807) | `9223372036854775807` | Native int ‚Üí number | | `NAN` or `INF` | `0` (with warning) | Not valid JSON; PHP coerces | | `fopen("file","r")` (resource) | `{}` (with warning) | Resources not supported | | `"string with \"quotes\""` | `"string with \"quotes\""` | Special chars are escaped | | Multibyte UTF-8 (e.g., "ý¥Æý¥≤ý¥Øý¥æý¥≥ý¥Ç") | `"\u0d2e\u0d32\u0d2f\u0d3e\u0d33\u0d02"` (default) | Unicode escape | | Same input with `JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE` | `"ý¥Æý¥≤ý¥Øý¥æý¥≥ý¥Ç"` | Raw UTF-8 retained | > [!WARNING] > **`NAN` and `INF` Pitfall:** In some PHP versions, encoding `NAN` or `INF` returns `false` (failure). Always sanitize floating-point inputs before encoding. --- ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example ‚Äî JSON Size Comparison **Problem (KTU Lab Analytical Question):** Compare the byte size of equivalent PHP-data representations. Given data: `["KTU", 2024, true, null, ["a","b"]]` | Representation | Byte Count | Notes | |----------------|-----------|-------| | JSON (compact) | 30 bytes | `["KTU",2024,true,null,["a","b"]]` | | JSON (pretty) | 49 bytes | With `\n` and indentation | | PHP `serialize()` | ~70 bytes | Includes type-prefixed metadata | | XML equivalent | ~95 bytes | Verbose tags | **Conclusion:** Compact JSON is **~57% smaller** than the equivalent XML, justifying its dominance in modern APIs. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## üìò PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define JSON. List any four PHP functions used to manipulate JSON data with a one-line description of each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** is a lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format used to transmit data between a server and a client (or between different programming languages). It is based on a subset of JavaScript object literal syntax and is standardized as RFC 8259. **Four PHP functions for JSON manipulation:** 1. **`json_encode($value, $flags, $depth)`** ‚Äî Converts a PHP variable (array, object, scalar) into a JSON-formatted string. Returns the string on success or `false` on failure. 2. **`json_decode($json, $assoc, $depth, $flags)`** ‚Äî Converts a JSON string back into a PHP variable. If `$assoc` is `true`, returns an associative array; otherwise returns a `stdClass` object. 3. **`json_last_error()`** ‚Äî Returns the last JSON encoding/decoding error as an integer constant (e.g., `JSON_ERROR_NONE` for success). 4. **`json_last_error_msg()`** ‚Äî Returns a human-readable string describing the last JSON error. Available since PHP 5.5. *(Optional 5th for extra credit: `JSON_THROW_ON_ERROR` flag introduced in PHP 7.3 that makes `json_encode`/`decode` throw a `JsonException` instead of returning false.)* --- ### **Question 2: Differentiate between `json_encode()` and `json_decode()` with a suitable PHP code example for each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `json_encode()` | `json_decode()` | |--------|----------------|----------------| | **Purpose** | PHP ‚Üí JSON (serialization) | JSON ‚Üí PHP (deserialization) | | **Input** | PHP variable (array, object, scalar) | JSON string | | **Output** | JSON string (or `false` on failure) | PHP variable (array/object, or `null` on failure) | | **Direction** | Server-to-client preparation | Client-to-server parsing | **Example 1 ‚Äî `json_encode()`:** ```php 95, "Physics" => 88, "Chemistry" => 91]; $jsonOutput = json_encode($marks); echo $jsonOutput; // Output: {"Maths":95,"Physics":88,"Chemistry":91} ?> ``` **Example 2 ‚Äî `json_decode()`:** ```php 95 [Physics] => 88 [Chemistry] => 91 ) ?> ``` --- ## üìó PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): JSON File-Based Student Records Management System** **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a complete PHP script that performs the following: - Reads existing student records from a JSON file named `students.json`. - Decodes the JSON data into a PHP associative array. - Displays all records in an HTML table with columns: `ID`, `Name`, `Branch`, `CGPA`. **(b) [7 Marks]** Extend the above script to add a **new student record** to the JSON file. **Part (a) ‚Äî Solution: Encoding with Flags [7 marks: 3 for explanation, 4 for code]**

**Theoretical Explanation of Flags:**

The `json_encode()` function accepts a bitmask of optional flags that modify its default behavior. These flags are predefined PHP constants starting with the prefix `JSON_`. Multiple flags can be combined using the bitwise OR operator `|`.

| Flag | Constant Value | Purpose |
|------|---------------|---------|
| `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT` | 128 | Returns formatted JSON with indentation for human readability |
| `JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE` | 256 | Preserves Unicode characters as-is (e.g., Malayalam) instead of `\uXXXX` |
| `JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES` | 64 | Does not escape forward slashes `/` to `\/` |
| `JSON_NUMERIC_CHECK` | 32 | Converts numeric strings to actual numbers |
| `JSON_FORCE_OBJECT` | 1 | Forces output to be a JSON object even for empty arrays |
| `JSON_HEX_TAG` | 2048 | Converts `<` and `>` to `\u003C` and `\u003E` (XSS prevention) |

> 1. **Forgetting `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT`** when storing data to file ‚Üí Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing `json_last_error()` check** after `json_decode()` ‚Üí Lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Not passing `true` as second argument** to `json_decode()` for procedural code ‚Üí Lose 1 mark (examiner expects array access syntax).
> 4. **Forgetting `htmlspecialchars()`** when echoing JSON inside HTML ‚Üí Lose 1 mark (XSS safety).
> 5. **Confusing `JSON_ERROR_SYNTAX` (4)** with `JSON_ERROR_STATE_MISMATCH (2)` ‚Üí Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not using `array_values()` after `unset()`** in DELETE operation ‚Üí produces a JSON object instead of array ‚Üí Lose 2 marks.
> 7. **Writing `json_decode` on a file resource directly** ‚Äî must read file with `file_get_contents` first ‚Üí Lose 2 marks.
