## üßÝ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

### Core Functions (Must Memorize)
- ‚≠ê `json_encode($value, $flags, $depth)` ‚Äî PHP ‚Üí JSON
- ‚≠ê `json_decode($json, $assoc, $depth, $flags)` ‚Äî JSON ‚Üí PHP
- ‚≠ê `json_last_error()` ‚Äî Returns error code
- ‚≠ê `json_last_error_msg()` ‚Äî Returns error message string

### The Golden Rules
- üìå **Indexed array (numeric keys 0,1,2...)** ‚Üí JSON **array `[ ]`**
- üìå **Associative array (string keys)** ‚Üí JSON **object `{ }`**
- üìå `json_decode($json, true)` returns array; `json_decode($json)` returns object
- üìå Always use `json_last_error()` after decode; never trust user input
- üìå Use `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT` for file storage; omit for API responses
- üìå Use `JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE` for regional language data
- üìå After `unset()`, call `array_values()` to re-index

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- ‚ùå Forgetting `, true` in `json_decode` (returns object, not array)
- ‚ùå Not closing JSON braces/brackets manually
- ‚ùå Using single quotes in JSON (must be double quotes)
- ‚ùå Treating JSON `null` as error ‚Äî it's a valid value
- ‚ùå Storing `NAN`/`INF` in JSON (not supported)

### File I/O Pattern (Recap)
```php
// READ
$data = json_decode(file_get_contents("file.json"), true);

// WRITE
file_put_contents("file.json", json_encode($data, JSON_PRETTY_PRINT));
```

### Exam-Favorite Flag Combinations
- üéØ File storage ‚Üí `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT`
- üéØ Malayalam/Hindi text ‚Üí `JSON_PRETTY_PRINT | JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE`
- üéØ API with numbers in strings ‚Üí `JSON_NUMERIC_CHECK`
- üéØ XSS-safe output ‚Üí `JSON_HEX_TAG | JSON_HEX_AMP | JSON_HEX_APOS | JSON_HEX_QUOT`

### Key Constants to Memorize (Integer Values)
- `JSON_ERROR_NONE` = **0**
- `JSON_ERROR_DEPTH` = **1**
- `JSON_ERROR_STATE_MISMATCH` = **2**
- `JSON_ERROR_CTRL_CHAR` = **3**
- `JSON_ERROR_SYNTAX` = **4** (most common in KTU questions)
- `JSON_ERROR_UTF8` = **5**

### One-Line Summary for Viva
> *"JSON is a text-based data interchange format. PHP's `json_encode()` converts PHP arrays/objects/scalars to JSON strings, and `json_decode()` reverses the process, with the second parameter controlling whether the result is an associative array or a `stdClass` object."*

topics.title: Web Development Frameworks: Laravel Overview - Features of Laravel - Setting up a Laravel Development Environment - Application Structure of Laravel - Routing - Middleware - Controllers - Route Model Binding - Views - Redirections - Request and Responses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNT503 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Web Development Frameworks: Laravel Overview - Features of Laravel - Setting up a Laravel Development Environment - Application Structure of Laravel - Routing - Middleware - Controllers - Route Model Binding - Views - Redirections - Request and Responses # SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Laravel? **Laravel** is a free, open-source **PHP web application framework** created by **Taylor Otwell** (first released June 2011) that follows the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architectural pattern. It provides an elegant syntax and a rich toolset for building modern, maintainable, scalable web applications ‚Äî from simple REST APIs to enterprise-grade SaaS platforms. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Memorize This Verbatim)** > *"Laravel is a PHP-based, open-source web application framework that uses the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern to provide a clean, elegant syntax and expressive tools for rapid web application development."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Skyscraper Construction" View Imagine building a house from scratch vs. using a pre-engineered kit: | Approach | Analogy | Outcome | |---|---|---| | **Raw PHP** | Mixing your own cement, cutting your own wood, wiring your own electricity. Full control, but extremely slow, error-prone. | Custom but chaotic | | **Laravel** | A pre-engineered building kit with pre-fabricated walls, plumbing templates, electrical conduits, and a master architect's blueprint. | Fast, standardized, robust | Laravel is that **master architect's toolkit**. It gives you ready-made blueprints (routing, ORM, authentication, templating) so you can focus on the *unique* business logic of your application instead of reinventing the wheel. ## 1.3 Why Laravel for KTU Students? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this is in your syllabus:** The KTU 2024 PCCNT503 Web Programming module bridges raw PHP (Modules 1‚Äì3) with real-world, industry-standard framework development. After learning PHP, MySQL, sessions, and AJAX, you need to understand how frameworks elevate these primitives into production-ready systems. --- ## 1.4 Core Features of Laravel (Official Syllabus Highlights) Laravel ships with a vast ecosystem. Below are the **must-know** features for KTU exams: 1. **MVC Architecture** ‚Äî Separation of concerns via Models, Views, and Controllers. 2. **Eloquent ORM** ‚Äî Object-Relational Mapper that lets you interact with databases using PHP syntax instead of raw SQL. 3. **Blade Templating Engine** ‚Äî Lightweight templating with `@if`, `@foreach`, layout inheritance. 4. **Artisan CLI** ‚Äî Command-line tool (`php artisan ...`) for scaffolding, migrations, testing. 5. **Routing System** ‚Äî Clean, expressive URL definition via `routes/web.php` and `routes/api.php`. 6. **Middleware** ‚Äî Filter HTTP requests (auth, CSRF, logging) entering the application. 7. **Database Migrations & Seeders** ‚Äî Version-controlled schema changes. 8. **Authentication & Authorization** ‚Äî Built-in gates, policies, and scaffolding via `Breeze` / `Jetstream`. 9. **Queues, Events, Broadcasting** ‚Äî For real-time and asynchronous processing. 10. **Testing Support** ‚Äî PHPUnit and Pest integration out of the box. 11. **Package Ecosystem (Composer)** ‚Äî Composer-based dependency management. > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield:** Memorize *at minimum* the 6 features in **bold** above. They are the most frequently asked Part-A questions. --- ## 1.5 Laravel Version & Runtime Requirements > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Current Stable Major:** Laravel 11.x (and Laravel 10.x LTS ‚Äî supported until **February 2025**). > - **Minimum PHP Version:** PHP **8.1+** (for Laravel 10) and PHP **8.2+** (for Laravel 11). > - **Composer:** Required (PHP dependency manager). > - **Database:** MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, SQL Server supported natively. > - **Web Server:** Apache / Nginx (or PHP's built-in dev server via `php artisan serve`). --- # SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Laravel Architecture ‚Äî The MVC Triad Laravel implements the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern. The request lifecycle is: 1. **Request hits `public/index.php`** (front controller). 2. **`bootstrap/app.php` boots the kernel and service providers.** 3. **HTTP Kernel dispatches request through Middleware stack.** 4. **Router matches URL ‚Üí executes Controller (or closure).** 5. **Controller interacts with Model (Eloquent) ‚Üí queries DB.** 6. **Controller passes data to View (Blade template).** 7. **Response is sent back through middleware ‚Üí browser.** > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic ‚Äî "R-C-M-V-R":** Request ‚Üí Controller ‚Üí Model ‚Üí View ‚Üí Response. --- ## 2.2 Feature Deep-Dive (Exam-Ready Notes) ### 2.2.1 Eloquent ORM Eloquent maps a database table to a PHP class. Each row is an object instance. ```php // app/Models/Student.php class Student extends Model { protected $table = 'students'; protected $fillable = ['name', 'rollno', 'cgpa']; } ``` Common operations: - `Student::all()` ‚Äî `SELECT * FROM students` - `Student::find(5)` ‚Äî `SELECT * WHERE id = 5` - `Student::where('cgpa', '>=', 9)->get()` ‚Äî conditional query. - `$student->save()` ‚Äî INSERT/UPDATE. ### 2.2.2 Blade Templating Engine Blade files end with `.blade.php` and live in `resources/views/`. They support: - `{{ $variable }}` ‚Äî escaped output (XSS safe). - `{!! $variable !!}` ‚Äî unescaped (use cautiously). - `@if`, `@else`, `@foreach`, `@csrf`, `@method('PUT')`. **Layout Inheritance Example:** ```php @yield('title') @yield('content') ``` ```php @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'Students') @section('content')

All Students

@endsection ``` ### 2.2.3 Artisan CLI (Command-Line Interface) Artisan is Laravel's command-line assistant. High-yield commands: | Command | Purpose | |---|---| | `php artisan serve` | Run dev server on `http://127.0.0.1:8000` | | `php artisan make:controller StudentController` | Generate controller | | `php artisan make:model Student -m` | Generate model + migration | | `php artisan migrate` | Run all pending migrations | | `php artisan route:list` | Display all registered routes | | `php artisan tinker` | Interactive PHP REPL with Laravel context | --- ## 2.3 Setting Up a Laravel Development Environment ### 2.3.1 Prerequisites (Must Be Installed First) - **PHP 8.1+** with extensions: `openssl`, `pdo`, `mbstring`, `tokenizer`, `xml`, `ctype`, `json`, `bcmath`. - **Composer** (https://getcomposer.org). - **A database** (MySQL/MariaDB/SQLite). - **Node.js & NPM** (for compiling frontend assets via Vite). ### 2.3.2 Two Installation Methods **Method 1 ‚Äî Via Composer (Official, Recommended)** ```bash composer create-project laravel/laravel myapp cd myapp php artisan serve ``` **Method 2 ‚Äî Via Laravel Installer** ```bash composer global require laravel/installer laravel new myapp cd myapp php artisan serve ``` ### 2.3.3 Environment Configuration ‚Äî The `.env` File The `.env` file holds environment-specific configuration (DB credentials, app key, mail, cache driver). ```env APP_NAME=MyApp APP_ENV=local APP_KEY=base64:GENERATED_KEY_HERE APP_DEBUG=true APP_URL=http://localhost DB_CONNECTION=mysql DB_HOST=127.0.0.1 DB_PORT=3306 DB_DATABASE=myapp_db DB_USERNAME=root DB_PASSWORD=secret ``` > [!WARNING] > Never commit `.env` to public version control. It contains secrets. --- ## 2.4 Application Structure of Laravel (Directory Map) A fresh Laravel project contains the following **KTU-favorite** directory tree: ``` myapp/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app/ # Core application code ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Http/ ‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Controllers/ # Controller classes ‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Middleware/ # Custom middleware ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Models/ # Eloquent models ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ bootstrap/ # Framework bootstrap & cache ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config/ # All configuration files ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ database/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ migrations/ # Schema definitions ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ seeders/ # Test data ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ factories/ # Model factories ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ public/ # Web server entry point (index.php) ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ resources/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ views/ # Blade templates ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ css/ # Stylesheets ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ js/ # JavaScript ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ routes/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ web.php # Web (session) routes ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api.php # Stateless API routes ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ console.php # Closure-based console commands ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ storage/ # Caches, logs, uploaded files ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/ # PHPUnit / Pest tests ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ vendor/ # Composer dependencies ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .env # Environment variables ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ artisan # Artisan CLI executable ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ composer.json # PHP dependencies manifest ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Question Pattern:** *"Explain the directory structure of a Laravel application."* ‚Äî Memorize the 5 most important folders: `app/Http/Controllers`, `routes/`, `resources/views/`, `database/migrations/`, `public/`. --- ## 2.5 Routing in Laravel (High-Yield Topic) Routes are defined in `routes/web.php` (web) or `routes/api.php` (API). ### Route Types ‚Äî The Complete List | HTTP Verb | Method | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | `GET` | `Route::get()` | Display / read data | | `POST` | `Route::post()` | Submit form / create | | `PUT` | `Route::put()` | Full update | | `PATCH` | `Route::patch()` | Partial update | | `DELETE` | `Route::delete()` | Delete | | `ANY` | `Route::any()` | Match any verb | | `MATCH` | `Route::match(['get','post'], ...)` | Match listed verbs | ### Route Examples ```php use App\Http\Controllers\StudentController; // Basic GET route (closure) Route::get('/welcome', function () { return view('welcome'); }); // Route pointing to controller method Route::get('/students', [StudentController::class, 'index']); // Resource route (auto-creates 7 RESTful routes) Route::resource('students', StudentController::class); // Route with URL parameter Route::get('/students/{id}', [StudentController::class, 'show']); // Named route (used for redirection) Route::get('/profile', [UserController::class, 'show'])->name('profile.show'); // Route group with middleware Route::middleware(['auth'])->group(function () { Route::get('/dashboard', [DashboardController::class, 'index']); }); ``` > [!TIP] > **Named routes** are critical because they decouple URLs from logic. Use `route('profile.show')` instead of hardcoding `/profile` in your code. --- ## 2.6 Middleware in Laravel **Middleware** is a series of "filters" that HTTP requests pass through before reaching your controller. They perform tasks like authentication, CSRF validation, logging, and input sanitization. ### 2.6.1 Middleware Types | Type | Description | |---|---| | **Global Middleware** | Runs on every HTTP request. Defined in `app/Http/Kernel.php`. | | **Route Middleware** | Assigned to specific routes via `middleware()` method. | | **Group Middleware** | Applied via `Route::group(['middleware' => 'auth'], ...)`. | ### 2.6.2 Built-in Middleware (KTU-Favorite) - `auth` ‚Äî Verifies user is logged in. - `guest` ‚Äî Ensures user is NOT logged in. - `csrf` ‚Äî Validates CSRF token on POST/PUT/DELETE. - `throttle:60,1` ‚Äî Rate-limits to 60 requests/minute. ### 2.6.3 Creating Custom Middleware ```bash php artisan make:middleware CheckAge ``` ```php // app/Http/Middleware/CheckAge.php public function handle(Request $request, Closure $next) { if ($request->age < 18) { return redirect('home'); } return $next($request); } ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Middleware Flow:** *"Request ‚Üí Middleware Stack ‚Üí Controller ‚Üí Response"* ‚Äî Like airport security checkpoints. If middleware blocks, the request never reaches the controller. --- ## 2.7 Controllers in Laravel **Controllers** group related request-handling logic into a single class. They live in `app/Http/Controllers/`. ```bash php artisan make:controller StudentController --resource ``` The `--resource` flag generates the standard 7 RESTful methods: | Method | HTTP Verb | URL | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `index()` | GET | `/students` | List all | | `create()` | GET | `/students/create` | Show form | | `store()` | POST | `/students` | Save new | | `show($id)` | GET | `/students/{id}` | Display one | | `edit($id)` | GET | `/students/{id}/edit` | Edit form | | `update(Request, $id)` | PUT/PATCH | `/students/{id}` | Update | | `destroy($id)` | DELETE | `/students/{id}` | Delete | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** The 7 resource methods (`index, create, store, show, edit, update, destroy`) ‚Äî memorize this table verbatim. --- ## 2.8 Route Model Binding (KTU High-Yield) **Route Model Binding** is a Laravel feature that automatically injects a model instance into your controller method instead of just the raw ID. It eliminates manual `Model::find($id)` lookups. ### 2.8.1 Implicit Binding (Automatic ‚Äî Most Common) ```php // Route definition Route::get('/students/{student}', [StudentController::class, 'show']); ``` ```php // Controller public function show(Student $student) { // Laravel auto-resolves ID ‚Üí Student object return view('students.show', ['student' => $student]); } ``` Laravel: 1. Sees `{student}` in URL. 2. Resolves the parameter name to the `Student` model (by type-hint). 3. Queries `students WHERE id = $id` automatically. 4. 404s if not found. ### 2.8.2 Explicit Binding (Custom Key) ```php // In RouteServiceProvider::boot() Route::bind('student', function ($value) { return Student::where('rollno', $value)->firstOrFail(); }); ``` --- ## 2.9 Views in Laravel **Views** are the presentation layer ‚Äî typically Blade templates. They are returned from controllers using the `view()` helper. ```php // In controller return view('students.index', ['students' => $students]); // Or with compact() return view('students.show', compact('student')); ``` ### Sharing Data Across All Views ```php // In App\Providers\AppServiceProvider::boot() View::share('siteName', 'KTU B.Tech Portal'); ``` --- ## 2.10 Redirections in Laravel The `redirect()` helper returns a `RedirectResponse`, sending the user's browser to a different URL. ```php // To a URL return redirect('/students'); // To a named route return redirect()->route('students.index'); // To a controller action return redirect()->action([StudentController::class, 'index']); // With flashed data return redirect('/login')->with('status', 'Please log in!'); // Back to previous page return back(); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flashed data** exists only for one subsequent request ‚Äî perfect for success/error messages after form submission. --- ## 2.11 Request and Response Objects (KTU High-Yield) ### 2.11.1 The `Request` Object Injected into controller methods to access all incoming HTTP data. ```php public function store(Request $request) { // Reading data $name = $request->input('name'); $all = $request->all(); $has = $request->has('email'); // Validation $validated = $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required|min:3', 'email' => 'required|email|unique:users', 'cgpa' => 'required|numeric|min:0|max:10', ]); // File uploads $file = $request->file('avatar'); $path = $request->file('avatar')->store('avatars'); } ``` ### 2.11.2 The `Response` Object Returned from controllers. Can be a string, view, JSON, file download, etc. ```php // Plain string return 'Hello World'; // JSON (for APIs) return response()->json(['status' => 'ok', 'data' => $student]); // Custom status code + headers return response('Not Found', 404)->header('Content-Type', 'text/plain'); // File download return response()->download(public_path('files/report.pdf')); // View response return response()->view('hello', ['name' => 'KTU'], 200); ``` --- ## 2.12 KTU High-Yield Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Pattern | KTU Weight | |---|---|---| | **Install Laravel** | `composer create-project laravel/laravel app` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Run server** | `php artisan serve` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Make controller** | `php artisan make:controller XController --resource` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Define GET route** | `Route::get('/url', [Ctrl::class, 'method'])` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Route param** | `Route::get('/user/{id}', ...)` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Named route** | `->name('profile.show')` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Middleware chain** | `Route::middleware('auth')->group(...)` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Resource route** | `Route::resource('photos', PhotoController::class)` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Route Model Binding** | `Route::get('/student/{student}', ...)` + type-hint | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Redirect to route** | `return redirect()->route('home');` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Redirect with data** | `return redirect('/x')->with('key','value');` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Return view** | `return view('folder.file', $data);` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Blade extends** | `@extends('layouts.app')` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Blade yield** | `@yield('section')` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Eloquent fetch all** | `Student::all()` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Eloquent find** | `Student::find($id)` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Eloquent where** | `Student::where('cgpa','>=',9)->get()` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Request input** | `$request->input('name')` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Request validate** | `$request->validate(['email'=>'required'])` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **JSON response** | `response()->json([...])` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | | **Env var** | `env('DB_DATABASE')` from `.env` | ‚òÖ‚òÖ | --- ## 2.13 Real-World Utility & Engineering Context | Use Case | Laravel Component Used | |---|---| | E-commerce site (Amazon-like) | Routing, Controllers, Eloquent, Blade | | REST API for mobile app | `routes/api.php`, JSON responses, Sanctum auth | | Admin dashboard with role-based access | Middleware + Policies | | Form-heavy registration portal | Request validation + flashed redirect data | | SaaS multi-tenant app | Service container, queues, broadcasting | --- # SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Walkthrough ‚Äî Building a "Student Management" Module End-to-End This walkthrough demonstrates the **full Laravel MVC cycle** with code at every stage. KTU exams frequently ask "Explain with an example" ‚Äî this is the canonical structure. ### Step 1: Create the Laravel Project ```bash composer create-project laravel/laravel studentapp cd studentapp ``` **What happens:** - Composer downloads Laravel skeleton + all dependencies into `vendor/`. - Generates `.env` file with a fresh `APP_KEY`. - Creates the default directory structure shown in Section 2.4. ### Step 2: Configure the Database in `.env` ```env DB_CONNECTION=mysql DB_HOST=127.0.0.1 DB_PORT=3306 DB_DATABASE=studentapp_db DB_USERNAME=root DB_PASSWORD= ``` ```bash # Create the database first (in MySQL CLI or phpMyAdmin) mysql -u root -p -e "CREATE DATABASE studentapp_db;" ``` ### Step 3: Create Model + Migration ```bash php artisan make:model Student -m ``` ```php // database/migrations/2025_xx_xx_create_students_table.php public function up(): void { Schema::create('students', function (Blueprint $table) { $table->id(); $table->string('name'); $table->string('rollno')->unique(); $table->float('cgpa'); $table->timestamps(); }); } ``` ```bash php artisan migrate ``` **Explanation of Logic:** - `$table->id()` creates an auto-incrementing primary key. - `$table->string('rollno')->unique()` enforces DB-level uniqueness. - `$table->timestamps()` adds `created_at` + `updated_at` columns. ### Step 4: Define the Model (`app/Models/Student.php`) ```php namespace App\Models; use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Model; class Student extends Model { protected $fillable = ['name', 'rollno', 'cgpa']; } ``` **Why `$fillable`?** Mass-assignment protection. Only listed fields can be filled via `Student::create([...])`. ### Step 5: Create Resource Controller ```bash php artisan make:controller StudentController --resource ``` ```php // app/Http/Controllers/StudentController.php namespace App\Http\Controllers; use App\Models\Student; use Illuminate\Http\Request; class StudentController extends Controller { // GET /students public function index() { $students = Student::all(); return view('students.index', compact('students')); } // GET /students/create public function create() { return view('students.create'); } // POST /students public function store(Request $request) { $validated = $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required|min:3', 'rollno' => 'required|unique:students', 'cgpa' => 'required|numeric|min:0|max:10', ]); Student::create($validated); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student added!'); } // GET /students/{student} (Route Model Binding) public function show(Student $student) { return view('students.show', compact('student')); } // GET /students/{student}/edit public function edit(Student $student) { return view('students.edit', compact('student')); } // PUT /students/{student} public function update(Request $request, Student $student) { $validated = $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required|min:3', 'rollno' => 'required|unique:students,rollno,'.$student->id, 'cgpa' => 'required|numeric|min:0|max:10', ]); $student->update($validated); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student updated!'); } // DELETE /students/{student} public function destroy(Student $student) { $student->delete(); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student deleted!'); } } ``` ### Step 6: Register the Resource Route ```php // routes/web.php use App\Http\Controllers\StudentController; Route::resource('students', StudentController::class); ``` **This single line auto-generates 7 routes:** | Verb | URI | Action | Route Name | |---|---|---|---| | GET | `/students` | `index` | `students.index` | | GET | `/students/create` | `create` | `students.create` | | POST | `/students` | `store` | `students.store` | | GET | `/students/{student}` | `show` | `students.show` | | GET | `/students/{student}/edit` | `edit` | `students.edit` | | PUT/PATCH | `/students/{student}` | `update` | `students.update` | | DELETE | `/students/{student}` | `destroy` | `students.destroy` | > [!IMPORTANT] > Verify all routes via `php artisan route:list` before debugging. ### Step 7: Create Blade Views **resources/views/students/index.blade.php** ```php @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'All Students') @section('content') @if(session('success'))
{{ session('success') }}
@endif + Add Student @foreach($students as $s) @endforeach
NameRoll NoCGPAActions
{{ $s->name }} {{ $s->rollno }} {{ $s->cgpa }} View Edit
@csrf @method('DELETE')
@endsection ``` **resources/views/students/create.blade.php** ```php @extends('layouts.app') @section('content')
@csrf Name:
Rollno:
CGPA:
@endsection ``` ### Step 8: Run and Test ```bash php artisan serve ``` Open `http://127.0.0.1:8000/students` ‚Äî the index page renders. Add, edit, delete ‚Äî every action flows through the request lifecycle described in Section 2.1. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparison Table for Exam Questions > Use this for comparison-type Part-A questions. | Feature | Raw PHP | Laravel | |---|---|---| | Routing | Manual `switch` or `.htaccess` | `Route::get()`, `Route::resource()` | | DB access | `mysqli_query()` / PDO | Eloquent ORM | | Templating | Mixed HTML + PHP | Blade (`@if`, `@foreach`) | | Auth | Build from scratch | `php artisan make:auth` / Breeze | | Migrations | Manual SQL files | `php artisan migrate` | | CSRF | Manual token | `@csrf` directive | | Validation | Manual `if/else` | `$request->validate([...])` | | Middleware | Custom bootstrap code | `Route::middleware('auth')` | --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Request ‚Üí Response Flow (Textual) Starting from the moment a user types a URL into a browser: $$ \text{Browser Request} \;\xrightarrow{\text{HTTP}}\; \text{public/index.php} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Boot}}\; \text{HTTP Kernel} $$ $$ \text{Kernel} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Stack}}\; \text{Middleware}_1 \to \text{Middleware}_2 \to \cdots \to \text{Router} $$ $$ \text{Router} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Match}}\; \text{Controller@method} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Logic}}\; \text{Model (Eloquent) ‚Üî DB} $$ $$ \text{Controller} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Data}}\; \text{Blade View} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Render}}\; \text{HTML} $$ $$ \text{HTML} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Response}}\; \text{Middleware (return path)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Browser}}\; \text{User} $$ **Engineering Insight:** Each arrow represents a discrete, testable boundary ‚Äî making Laravel applications easy to debug, mock, and unit-test. --- # SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Laravel Request Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Browser HTTP Request] --> B[public/index.php] B --> C[Bootstrap app.php] C --> D[HTTP Kernel Boot] D --> E[Middleware Stack] E --> F[Route Registration Match] F --> G[Controller Method] G --> H[Model Eloquent Query] H --> I[Database] I --> H H --> G G --> J[View Blade Render] J --> K[Response Object] K --> L[Middleware Return Path] L --> M[Browser Display] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style M fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style E fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style F fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style G fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8 style H fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d ``` ## 4.2 MVC Architecture in Laravel ```mermaid flowchart LR User([End User]) subgraph LaravelApp[Laravel Application] direction TB Router[Router routes/web.php] Ctrl[Controller] Model[Model Eloquent ORM] View[View Blade Template] DB[(Database MySQL)] end User -- HTTP Request --> Router Router --> Ctrl Ctrl -- fetch/save --> Model Model <--> DB Ctrl -- pass data --> View View -- Rendered HTML --> Ctrl Ctrl -- HTTP Response --> User style User fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style Router fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style Ctrl fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8 style Model fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style View fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style DB fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b ``` ## 4.3 Middleware Pipeline Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR Req([Incoming Request]) --> M1[StartSession] M1 --> M2[ShareErrors] M2 --> M3[VerifyCsrfToken] M3 --> M4[Authenticate] M4 --> M5[ThrottleRequests] M5 --> C[Controller Method] C --> M5b[ThrottleRequests return] M5b --> M4b[Authenticate return] M4b --> M3b[VerifyCsrfToken return] M3b --> M2b[ShareErrors return] M2b --> M1b[StartSession return] M1b --> Res([HTTP Response]) style Req fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style Res fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style C fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8 ``` > [!NOTE] > Middleware executes **on the way in** (req) and **on the way out** (resp), enabling operations like response compression, session writing, and logging. ## 4.4 Route Model Binding Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[URL /students/5] --> B[Router matches /students/{student}] B --> C{Type-hint in controller} C -- Student $student --> D[Eloquent Query SELECT WHERE id=5] D --> E{Record found?} E -- Yes --> F[Inject Student object into method] E -- No --> G[404 ModelNotFoundException] F --> H[Controller runs logic] G --> I[Error 404 page] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style F fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style G fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b ``` ## 4.5 Laravel Application Directory Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[myapp/] --> AppDir[app/] Root --> BootstrapDir[bootstrap/] Root --> ConfigDir[config/] Root --> DatabaseDir[database/] Root --> PublicDir[public/] Root --> ResourcesDir[resources/] Root --> RoutesDir[routes/] Root --> StorageDir[storage/] Root --> TestsDir[tests/] Root --> VendorDir[vendor/] AppDir --> HttpDir[Http/] HttpDir --> CtrlDir[Controllers/] HttpDir --> MiddleDir[Middleware/] AppDir --> ModelsDir[Models/] DatabaseDir --> MigDir[migrations/] DatabaseDir --> SeedDir[seeders/] ResourcesDir --> ViewsDir[views/] RoutesDir --> WebRoutes[web.php] RoutesDir --> ApiRoutes[api.php] style Root fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style AppDir fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style PublicDir fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style ResourcesDir fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style RoutesDir fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8 ``` --- # SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A ‚Äî 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries **3 marks each** and expects 2‚Äì4 sentence precise answers. No sub-parts. --- ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024 Model]` **Define Laravel. List any four features of Laravel.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Laravel is an open-source **PHP web framework** that follows the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architectural pattern, used to develop modern, scalable, and maintainable web applications. Four key features: 1. **Eloquent ORM** ‚Äî Maps DB tables to PHP objects for expressive queries. 2. **Blade Templating Engine** ‚Äî Lightweight templating with inheritance and directives. 3. **Artisan CLI** ‚Äî Command-line tool for scaffolding and migrations. 4. **Built-in Authentication & Middleware** ‚Äî Ready-made security layers. --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model]` **Explain the role of middleware in Laravel with an example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Middleware in Laravel acts as a **filter layer** that processes HTTP requests before they reach the controller and processes the response on the way back. It is used for tasks like **authentication**, **CSRF protection**, and **logging**. **Example:** ```php Route::middleware(['auth'])->group(function () { Route::get('/dashboard', [DashboardController::class, 'index']); }); ``` Here, the `auth` middleware ensures only logged-in users can access `/dashboard`; otherwise, they are redirected to the login page. --- ## PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the given choice. --- ### **Question A ‚Äî 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the features of Laravel. Also describe the steps to set up a Laravel development environment. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Features of Laravel:** 1. **MVC Architecture** ‚Äî Provides a clear separation of concerns between data (Model), UI (View), and logic (Controller). 2. **Eloquent ORM** ‚Äî Enables database interaction using PHP objects without writing raw SQL. 3. **Blade Templating** ‚Äî Lightweight engine with `@if`, `@foreach`, layout inheritance. 4. **Artisan CLI** ‚Äî Automates repetitive tasks like `make:controller`, `migrate`, `route:list`. 5. **Routing System** ‚Äî Clean, expressive URL mapping via `routes/web.php`. 6. **Middleware** ‚Äî Request filters for auth, CSRF, logging. 7. **Migration System** ‚Äî Version control for DB schema. 8. **Composer Integration** ‚Äî Dependency management via Composer. **Setting Up Laravel:** ```bash # Step 1: Install Composer (prerequisite) # Step 2: Install Laravel via Composer composer create-project laravel/laravel myapp # Step 3: Navigate into project cd myapp # Step 4: Configure .env with DB credentials # Edit DB_DATABASE, DB_USERNAME, DB_PASSWORD # Step 5: Generate application key (auto-generated in Laravel 10+) php artisan key:generate # Step 6: Run database migrations php artisan migrate # Step 7: Start the development server php artisan serve ``` The app becomes accessible at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the directory structure of a Laravel application. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** ``` myapp/ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Http/ ‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Controllers/ ‚Üê Request handlers ‚îÇ ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Middleware/ ‚Üê Custom filters ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Models/ ‚Üê Eloquent models ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ bootstrap/ ‚Üê Framework bootstrap ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config/ ‚Üê Configuration files ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ database/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ migrations/ ‚Üê Schema definitions ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ seeders/ ‚Üê Test data generators ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ factories/ ‚Üê Model factories ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ public/ ‚Üê Entry point (index.php) ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ resources/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ views/ ‚Üê Blade templates ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ css/ ‚Üê Stylesheets ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ js/ ‚Üê JavaScript files ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ routes/ ‚îÇ ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ web.php ‚Üê Web routes ‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ api.php ‚Üê API routes ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ storage/ ‚Üê Logs, cache, uploads ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/ ‚Üê Unit/Feature tests ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ vendor/ ‚Üê Composer packages ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .env ‚Üê Environment variables ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ artisan ‚Üê CLI executable ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ composer.json ‚Üê Dependency manifest ``` **Explanation of Key Directories:** - `app/Http/Controllers/` ‚Äî Houses all controller classes handling business logic. - `routes/web.php` ‚Äî Where URL-to-controller mappings are defined. - `resources/views/` ‚Äî Blade template files (`.blade.php`). - `database/migrations/` ‚Äî Schema version-control files. - `public/` ‚Äî The only directory exposed to the web; contains `index.php` (entry point). --- ### **Question B ‚Äî 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023]` **(a) Explain Laravel Routing in detail. Differentiate between GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE routes with examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Routing in Laravel maps **HTTP requests** to **specific controllers or closures**. Routes are defined in `routes/web.php` (stateful) or `routes/api.php` (stateless, prefixed with `/api`). **HTTP Verb Routing ‚Äî Examples:** ```php use App\Http\Controllers\StudentController; // GET ‚Äî Retrieve/display data (e.g., student list page) Route::get('/students', [StudentController::class, 'index']); // POST ‚Äî Submit/create new data (e.g., save new student) Route::post('/students', [StudentController::class, 'store']); // PUT ‚Äî Update entire record Route::put('/students/{id}', [StudentController::class, 'update']); // DELETE ‚Äî Remove record Route::delete('/students/{id}', [StudentController::class, 'destroy']); // Resource route ‚Äî auto-generates all 7 routes Route::resource('students', StudentController::class); ``` **Comparison Table:** | Verb | Purpose | Idempotent? | Example Action | |---|---|---|---| | GET | Read | ‚úÖ Yes | View a page | | POST | Create | ‚ùå No | Submit form | | PUT | Full update | ‚úÖ Yes | Replace record | | PATCH | Partial update | ‚ùå No | Update one field | | DELETE | Remove | ‚úÖ Yes | Delete record | > **Idempotent** means calling the route multiple times produces the same result. **Route Parameters:** ```php // Required parameter Route::get('/user/{id}', function ($id) { return "User $id"; }); // Optional parameter Route::get('/user/{name?}', function ($name = 'Guest') { return "Hello $name"; }); ``` **Named Routes:** ```php Route::get('/profile', [UserController::class, 'show'])->name('profile.show'); // Used in views/controllers: route('profile.show') ``` --- **(b) Explain Controllers and Route Model Binding in Laravel with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Controllers:** Controllers are PHP classes that group related request-handling logic. They prevent route files from becoming cluttered with business logic. ```bash php artisan make:controller StudentController --resource ``` ```php namespace App\Http\Controllers; class StudentController extends Controller { public function index() { /* list */ } public function show($id) { /* display one */ } } ``` Registering in `routes/web.php`: ```php Route::resource('students', StudentController::class); ``` **Route Model Binding:** Route Model Binding **automatically injects a model instance** into controller methods based on the URL parameter and type-hint. **Implicit Binding Example:** ```php // Route Route::get('/students/{student}', [StudentController::class, 'show']); // Controller public function show(Student $student) { // $student is auto-resolved as Student::find($id) // If not found ‚Üí automatic 404 response return view('students.show', compact('student')); } ``` **Steps Laravel Performs Internally:** 1. URL `/students/5` is matched. 2. Parameter `{student}` is extracted ‚Üí `5`. 3. Controller signature `Student $student` triggers Eloquent query. 4. `Student::where('id', 5)->firstOrFail()` is executed. 5. If found ‚Üí `$student` object injected. If not ‚Üí 404 thrown. **Explicit Binding (Custom Key) Example:** ```php // In RouteServiceProvider::boot() Route::bind('student', function ($value) { return Student::where('rollno', $value)->firstOrFail(); }); ``` Now `/students/KTU2024CS001` will fetch the student whose `rollno` is `KTU2024CS001` instead of by primary key. **Benefits:** - Eliminates manual `Model::find($id)` boilerplate. - Automatic 404 handling. - Cleaner, more readable controller code. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (What Examiners Mark Down For):** > > 1. **Confusing `web.php` vs `api.php`** ‚Äî Web routes use sessions and CSRF; API routes are stateless and prefixed with `/api`. Mixing them up loses marks. > 2. **Forgetting `@csrf` in forms** ‚Äî Every POST/PUT/DELETE form in Blade must include `@csrf`. Examiners check this. > 3. **Forgetting `@method('PUT')` / `@method('DELETE')`** ‚Äî HTML forms only support GET/POST. Laravel uses `@method()` spoofing for PUT/PATCH/DELETE. > 4. **Not type-hinting the Model in Route Model Binding** ‚Äî Just writing `public function show($id)` defeats the purpose; you must use `Student $student`. > 5. **Mixing up `redirect()->route()` and `redirect()->action()`** ‚Äî `route()` uses the route **name**, `action()` uses `[Controller::class, 'method']`. > 6. **Forgetting `compact()` vs array syntax in views** ‚Äî `view('x', compact('var'))` is equivalent to `view('x', ['var' => $var])`. > 7. **Not running `php artisan migrate`** ‚Äî A common setup mistake before demonstrating the app. > 8. **Writing `$request->all()` instead of validated data in `store()`** ‚Äî Always pass the **validated array**, not raw input, to `Model::create()`. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Print This Before Exam:** > > - [ ] **Laravel** = PHP framework by **Taylor Otwell**, MVC-based, requires **PHP 8.1+** and **Composer**. > - [ ] **Install:** `composer create-project laravel/laravel appname`. > - [ ] **Run:** `php artisan serve` ‚Üí `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. > - [ ] **MVC** = Model (`app/Models/`) + View (`resources/views/*.blade.php`) + Controller (`app/Http/Controllers/`). > - [ ] **Route file:** `routes/web.php` (stateful) and `routes/api.php` (stateless). > - [ ] **HTTP verbs:** GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove). > - [ ] **Resource route** auto-generates **7 methods**: index, create, store, show, edit, update, destroy. > - [ ] **Middleware** = filter layer ‚Äî global, route, or group. Examples: `auth`, `csrf`, `throttle`. > - [ ] **Route Model Binding** = auto-inject Model via type-hint: `function show(Student $student)`. > - [ ] **View helper:** `view('folder.file', $data)` or `view('x', compact('var'))`. > - [ ] **Redirect:** `redirect('/url')`, `redirect()->route('name')`, `redirect()->back()`, `->with('key','val')`. > - [ ] **Request:** `$request->input('x')`, `$request->validate([...])`, `$request->file('x')`. > - [ ] **Response:** `response()->json([...])`, `response('text', 200)`, `response()->download($path)`. > - [ ] **Blade directives:** `@if`, `@foreach`, `@csrf`, `@method('PUT')`, `@extends`, `@yield`, `@section`. > - [ ] **Artisan essentials:** `make:controller -r`, `make:model -m`, `migrate`, `route:list`, `tinker`. > - [ ] **.env file** holds DB credentials ‚Äî never commit it. > - [ ] **Eloquent basics:** `Model::all()`, `Model::find($id)`, `Model::where(...)->get()`, `$model->save()`. ---
